The serine/arginine-rich splicing factor 2 ( SRSF2) is a key regulator of alternative splicing, and its mutation has been associated with myelodysplastic syndromes and acute myeloid leukemia.
Mutations of splicing factor SRSF2 have been identified as a common feature of many cancers, including myelodysplasia and leukemia.
Here we generated an isogenic cell line with a single nucleotide change in SRSF2 and found that this mutation causes aberrant splicing of many genes.
Our results provide insights into how mutations in splicing factors affect alternative splicing, which may be useful for understanding disease mechanisms and identifying new drug targets.
The parasite Schistosoma mansoni is responsible for causing schistosomiasis, a major global health problem.
We show here that mitochondria are essential for egg production in female schistosomes.
Here we demonstrate that the mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation pathway is required for egg production in Drosophila melanogaster.
We show that the decline in egg production is associated with a decrease in the number of lipid droplets in the reproductive organ ( vitellarium) of the worm.
The egg, or oocyte, is the female gamete in animals and plants.
The molecular mechanisms of egg activation are well understood in vertebrates and echinoderms but not in molluscs.
The oocyte is surrounded by a thin membrane, and it has been suggested that pressure changes in this membrane could be sensed by the oocyte to trigger Ca2+ release.
We show that, as the egg is released from the follicle, it undergoes a series of rapid changes in its internal calcium levels, which are visualized by fluorescent dyes.
The hepatitis C virus ( HCV) is an important cause of chronic liver disease worldwide.
We found that during acute HIV-1 infection, there are two major bottlenecks in virus evolution, and these occur at different stages of the infection.
We found that the first event occurred early during infection, when the virus had not yet reached its peak levels of replication, and was associated with an increase in viral genetic diversity.
We found that the diversity of HIV-1 is greater in regions of the genome that are targeted by the human immune system than in other parts of the virus.

Our study provides new insights into the dynamics of virus evolution during the acute phase of HCV infection and may help to explain why some individuals are able to clear their infections while others progress to chronicity.

This review found that using computerised systems to guide anaesthetic dosing is better than using clinical signs alone, but there was no clear evidence that it reduces the number of deaths or serious complications.
We found no evidence to suggest that any particular type of anaesthetic was better than another in terms of preventing postoperative cognitive dysfunction (POCD) or postoperative delirium (POD).

The review found that there was no clear evidence to show whether or not one type of surgery is better than another in terms of reducing the risk of dying, having complications after surgery, or staying in hospital longer.
The review authors found no studies that compared the effects of these two treatments on pain, function or quality of life in people with shoulder pain and frozen shoulder.
Key results The review found that using anaesthesia guided by bispectral index reduced the risk of postoperative delirium compared to standard care.

Key results The review found that using processed EEG during anaesthesia may reduce the risk of postoperative cognitive dysfunction at 12 weeks after surgery, but there was uncertainty about this effect at one week and 52 weeks after surgery.
The question of how DNA sequence encodes for gene expression has been studied extensively, but it remains unclear how the sequence information is translated into expression levels.
The most common method is to group genes with similar expression patterns together, and then search for enriched motifs within each group.
However, when there is high correlation between genes, such as during development, it becomes increasingly difficult to identify meaningful clusters.
In this work we develop a computational model that predicts the expression levels of genes based on their DNA sequences and the temporal pattern of gene expression during development.
Here we present a method for learning such patterns from time-course data using a novel approach called Graph-regularized Partial Least Squares Regression ( G-PLSR).
The persistence of these cells in the body is a major obstacle for the eradication of HIV-1 infection.
We have identified a novel function of RUNX1, a protein involved in gene expression regulation, as an important factor in maintaining HIV-1 latency.
Here we demonstrate that RUNX1, a protein involved in normal hematopoiesis, binds to the HIV-1 long terminal repeat ( LTR) and inhibits viral gene expression.
We also show that the combination of a small molecule inhibitor of RUNX1 and a histone deacetylase inhibitor synergistically activates latent HIV-1 infection in vitro and in vivo.
Executive functions are thought to be mediated by large-scale brain networks that change their configuration as they process information.
The human brain is an intricate network of billions of neurons and trillions of synapses, which are constantly changing their activity patterns to support cognition.
We used advanced methods from dynamic network neuroscience to study how brain activity patterns change as people perform a simple cognitive task.
We found that functional connectivity between these regions was associated with cognitive performance in tasks requiring working memory and executive control.
However, it is not clear how genetic variation affects gene expression through changes in chromatin.

The folding of many large proteins is slow and inefficient, which has been attributed to the presence of multiple intermediates on the folding pathway.
This suggests that cotranslational folding may be an important mechanism for avoiding kinetic traps in protein folding.
We find that this slowdown is beneficial for cotranslational folding of some proteins and detrimental for others.
Latency is maintained by latency promoting factors ( LPFs), which have been shown to be important for HIV-1 persistence but whose mechanism of action has not been fully elucidated.

We found that the most potent compounds were structurally and functionally related to known inhibitors of thioredoxin reductase, an enzyme required for parasite survival.
We also identified two additional lipopeptides ( PX12 and tiopronin) as LPA inhibitors, both of which have been previously reported to be active against other pathogens.
This study provides a proof of concept that screening for gene expression noise may be used to identify new targets for disease treatment.
The ribosome is a key player in protein synthesis and has been shown to have additional functions in regulating gene expression.
Our results reveal new insights into how muscle cells regulate protein synthesis, which may be important to improve muscle function in disease states such as muscular dystrophy or aging.
The inflammatory response to Ad is triggered by the interaction between viral capsid proteins and host cell surface receptors, which activates the IL-1 receptor I ( IL-1RI) signaling pathway.
The mechanisms by which IFN-I signaling is activated during viral infection are not fully understood.

Our findings reveal an important role for the spleen as a site of immune control of systemic infection by influenza viruses and provide new insights into how the spleen may be targeted to improve vaccine efficacy.
We have used mice infected with T. brucei or T. cruzi to study the role of antibodies in anemia.
These findings provide new insights into the pathogenesis of anemia during trypanosomiasis, which may be relevant for the development of novel therapeutic strategies to treat this disease.
We present a method for identifying the most effective interventions and locations for intervention based on the number of infections prevented.
We find that in many cases, physical distancing is more effective than masks and barriers, but that there are scenarios where masks and barriers may be better.
The current state of the art in association rule mining is to use deterministic algorithms, which enumerate all possible rules and then filter out those with low support or confidence.
However, they are often computationally expensive and may not scale well with large datasets.
We propose a novel algorithm for mining high-confidence association rules in large-scale datasets.
The algorithm is designed to uncover the most important rules by using a probabilistic approach and a general rule importance measure.
We demonstrate the performance gain in both simulated and real data examples.
The ciliates are an important model system for studying this process because their genomes undergo extensive rearrangement during sexual reproduction.
In this study, we show that the genome of Tetrahymena thermophila is composed of thousands of tiny macronuclear chromosomes, each encoding a single gene or a few genes.
The genome is composed of ~16, 000 small chromosomes ranging in length from 469 bp to 66 kb and contains no large-scale repetitive elements.
The genome of T. thermophila has been sequenced, and we have used this information to identify genetic variation within the species.
We have sequenced and assembled the genome of Oxytricha trifallax, which has one of the largest genomes among eukaryotes.
Telomere length is regulated by telomerase, which adds TTAGGG repeats to chromosome ends in most organisms.
The availability of this genome sequence provides a valuable resource to study the mechanisms of programmed genome rearrangement in ciliates.
The results of this review are inconclusive regarding whether there is an association between living in a rural or urban environment and risk of developing schizophrenia.
This study demonstrates how social network structure is related to urbanization, which may have implications for understanding the relationship between urbanization and mental health.

We find that the relationship between urbanization and depression is more complex than previously thought, with higher levels of social interaction associated with lower depression rates in larger cities.
We show that this model is consistent with data from four different sources, and we use it to predict the number of COVID-19 cases in US cities.
We find that people in larger cities have lower rates of depression, suggesting that urbanization may be beneficial for mental health.
Polycystic ovary syndrome ( PCOS) is a common endocrine disorder in women, characterized by irregular menstrual cycles, hyperandrogenism, and anovulation.
Clomiphene citrate is one of the most commonly prescribed drugs to treat anovulatory infertility in women with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS).
Ultrasound-guided transvaginal ovarian needle drilling ( UTND) is a new technique in which a thin needle is inserted through the vagina and guided by ultrasound to puncture the ovaries.
Study characteristics The evidence is current up to November 2018.

Study characteristics We searched the literature up to March 2015 and found two studies, involving 150 women, which compared ultrasound-guided transvaginal needle destruction of the ovaries to laparoscopic surgery to destroy the ovaries.

The review found that it was unclear if IVF using ICSI or IVF using IUI had better outcomes for live birth, clinical pregnancy, ongoing pregnancy, multiple pregnancies, miscarriage, and surgical complications than IVF using IUI in women with unexplained infertility.
The review found no evidence to support the use of clomiphene citrate for women with unexplained subfertility.

The review found no evidence to support the use of ultrasound tracking in addition to gonadotrophin treatment, as there were no differences between groups in terms of live births or surgical complications.

Quality of the evidence The quality of the evidence is very low due to small numbers of women included in each study, poor reporting of methods used to assess outcomes, and differences between studies in how they were conducted.
The replacement of Neanderthals by anatomically modern humans in Western Eurasia has been linked to climate change.
The Chatelperronian is an enigmatic archaeological technocomplex from southern France that has been interpreted as evidence for Neanderthals' ability to use symbolic thinking.
We used ZooMS to identify 28 previously unidentified hominin fossils in the Chatelperronian layer of the Grotte du Renne, France.
The analysis of ancient proteins from fossilized bones has provided important insights into human evolution and the evolutionary history of our species.
The emergence of new diseases is often associated with changes in the hosts or pathogens involved, and these events are known as host shifts.
The impact of climate change on the spread and prevalence of infectious diseases has been well documented, but little is known about how it affects the severity of infection.
We show that susceptibility to infection varies among species, but also changes with temperature.

We found that the relative susceptibility of different species to infection by a new pathogen is not necessarily constant across temperatures, but rather varies in a way that depends on the specific interaction between host and pathogen.
This review aimed to find out whether or not there are effective ways to reduce avoidable hospitalisations in older people.
This study aimed to find out more about how these interventions work and whether or not they could be used in other settings.
This study aimed to identify what is known about how well different strategies work to reduce unplanned hospital admissions, and to find out which strategies are most likely to be effective.
We found 569 studies, but only 12 of these reported on the cost-effectiveness of interventions to reduce alcohol consumption.
The review found that there is some evidence to suggest that these types of interventions may be effective in reducing alcohol consumption and improving health outcomes.

We report here that the high-pressure amorphous solid transforms to low-density amorphous solid through two intermediate states, namely, high-density amorphous solid and liquid-like state, before crystallizing into ice I.
The results are supported by large-scale molecular dynamics simulations, which reveal that the transition between the two phases is continuous and occurs through a series of intermediate states.
This work provides an insight into the complex interplay between polymorphic transitions, which is important for understanding the kinetics of phase transformations.
In this study we show that acentric chromosomes are able to segregate successfully when they contain a single copy of the centromere.
We found that acentric fragments of chromosomes are aligned in both directions relative to the spindle axis, whereas intact chromosomes are aligned only along the spindle axis.
We also show that in some cases, sister chromatids separate from each other at an abnormal time and place.
We show that in the absence of a kinetochore, microtubules are able to generate forces sufficient to separate chromosomes.
We have used RNA interference to show that several host factors involved in vesicle trafficking are required for MHV entry.
We have used a combination of biochemical and cell biological approaches to demonstrate that MHV-68 uses clathrin-mediated endocytosis to enter cells and that this process is essential for infection.

The mechanism of entry is not fully understood, but it has been suggested that the virus enters cells via endocytosis followed by acidification in lysosomes where it is processed by lysosomal enzymes.
We found that the presence of a furin cleavage site in the S protein is critical for efficient infection and that this cleavage event is required to activate the virus for membrane fusion.
The prostaglandin E2 ( PGE2) -mediated signaling pathway is known to play an important role in tumor progression, but its regulation by other lipid mediators has not been fully elucidated.
We found that combined inhibition of cyclooxygenase-2 ( COX-2) and soluble epoxide hydrolase ( sEH) significantly inhibited tumor growth and metastasis in mouse models of breast cancer.
The hypothalamus is thought to be the primary site of long-term control, but it has been suggested that the brainstem also plays an important role in this process.

The inheritance of DNA methylation patterns during cell division is mediated by the protein UHRF1, which recruits the enzyme responsible for maintaining DNA methylation ( DNMT1) to newly replicated DNA.
We have developed a system to study UHRF1 using purified components in vitro and found that UHRF1 binds to nucleosomes containing methylated H3K9 and unmethylated H3K20.
We show that UHRF1 binds to both hemimethylated and unmethylated DNA, but only binds to hemimethylated DNA in the context of replication forks.
The canonical NLRP3 inflammasome is activated by various stimuli such as ATP, reactive oxygen species, or bacterial toxins.
The galectin family of carbohydrate-binding proteins is known to interact with Gram-negative bacteria and contribute to host defense against infection.
The role of galectin-1 in the immune response to pathogens is well established; however, its interaction with bacterial glycans has not been studied.
Our findings reveal a novel mechanism by which galectins regulate caspase-4/11 activation and pyroptosis, providing insights into how cells respond to bacterial infections.
The use of fragment-based screening ( FBS) to identify small molecules for drug development is an important approach in drug discovery.
We have developed a computational method called SILCS ( Site Identification by Ligand Competitive Saturation) that maps the binding sites of proteins using small organic molecules as probes.
The method is based on the principle that fragments with favorable interactions will have lower free energies when bound to the target protein than unbound.
The resulting maps are useful for drug discovery, providing a means to identify potential binding sites on proteins and to predict how water molecules may interact with them.
The review found no evidence from randomised controlled trials to support routine vaccination of pregnant women with tetanus toxoid for preventing neonatal tetanus.
We found no evidence that giving vitamin C supplements to women before conception or in early pregnancy reduced the risk of preterm birth, low birthweight babies or small for gestational age babies.
The review found that giving tetanus toxoid to newborns may reduce the risk of death from tetanus, but there is not enough evidence to be sure about this.
The review found no evidence that vaccination reduced deaths from any cause other than tetanus.
The review found that giving tetanus toxoid to pregnant women before delivery may reduce the risk of tetanus in their babies by about half.

The review found that giving a single dose of diphtheria toxoid to newborns reduced infant mortality by about 10% compared with no vaccination and by about 5% compared with giving a placebo (a dummy vaccine).
Pain at the injection site was reported by more women in the group receiving Tdap compared with those receiving placebo.

These findings provide new insights into how plants integrate multiple environmental signals to control flowering time.

The process of clot formation has been studied extensively, but the mechanism by which it is resolved remains unclear.

We show that this is due to the presence of a specific sequence motif in the coiled-coil domain of fibrinogen, which is exposed by the proteolytic action of plasmin and recognized by the integrins.
The model provides an explanation for the conformational changes of fibrinogen upon adsorption onto solid surfaces, which are important for cell adhesion and blood clotting.

The results also provide insight into the mechanism by which the conformational change in the D domain is coupled to the binding site P1, providing an explanation for the allosteric control of the binding affinity of this site.
It has been known for decades that stereoacuity varies greatly between individuals and that this variation is greater than in any other sensory modality.
We found that microstructure of white matter tracts in the human brain is associated with stereovision ability.
We found that the brain's response to binocular disparity is not limited to the primary visual cortex, but also extends to higher-order areas in the ventral pathway.

We have investigated the genetic diversity of two fungal pathogens, Microbotryum violaceum and M. silenes-latifolii, which cause anther smut disease on Silene latifolia and S. dioica respectively.

We found that the genetic diversity and population structure of the fungal pathogen were similar to those of its host plant, indicating that the migration pattern of the pathogen is dependent on its host.
We found that the fungus had been present in many small refuges throughout the landscape, but was less likely to be dispersed long distances than its host.

We recorded from monkeys with Parkinson's disease ( PD) before and after they received bilateral lesions of the substantia nigra pars compacta, which is the brain region responsible for producing dopamine.
We found that patients with lesions to this network showed impaired task switching but preserved cognitive control performance and beta oscillations during task switching.
We show that different aspects of reward learning are affected differently by dopamine loss, suggesting that they may be mediated by distinct mechanisms.
This study demonstrates that the brain uses multiple mechanisms to compensate for the loss of dopamine, and that these mechanisms are not necessarily linearly related to each other.
This suggests that the relationship between dopamine and cognition is more complex than previously thought, and may be mediated by other factors such as attention.
We found that, in contrast to previous studies, there is no correlation between dopamine levels and cognitive performance.
The use of rapid diagnostic tests ( RDTs) to diagnose malaria has been shown to be effective in reducing the number of patients who are treated with antimalarial drugs unnecessarily.
Study characteristics We found seven studies (involving 17,505 participants) that compared chloroquine to placebo or no treatment for treating malaria.
The quality of evidence was low for all outcomes, mainly due to poor reporting of study methods and results.
The proportion of people receiving treatment based on their test result varied greatly between health workers, ranging from 0% to 81%.
The results suggest that the use of RDTs could reduce unnecessary treatment for malaria by up to 50% if health workers adhere to the test result.

The review found that introducing RDTs did not lead to better health outcomes for people with malaria, but it is possible that using RDTs may be more cost-effective than treating all people who have symptoms of malaria.

Here we show that such delays can actually be beneficial for information processing, as they allow neurons to process more complex patterns of input.

Chimeric antigen receptors ( CARs) are engineered proteins that allow T cells to recognize and kill tumor cells expressing specific antigens.
However, the use of CAR-T cells has been hampered by severe side effects and low persistence in patients.
We have developed a method for generating switch molecules by chemically modifying antibodies with fluorescent dyes at specific sites within their structure.
This work demonstrates an approach for controlling CAR-T cell activity in vivo by modulating their interaction with target cells.

We found that vesicle-mediated transport, primarily endocytosis, is affected in all metal-exposed groups.

We show that FtsZ is initially localized to the growth pole but then moves to the middle of the cell where it interacts with FtsA.
We show that PodJAt, a protein involved in polar auxin transport, is localized at the old pole and later at the growing tip during the cell cycle.
We show that the protein PopZ is localized to the new cell poles and PodJ is localized at the old cell poles in Arabidopsis thaliana.
ChR2 is an ion channel protein that responds to blue light by opening its pore and allowing ions to flow across the cell membrane.
We have developed optogenetic tools based on the algal channelrhodopsin-2 ( ChR2) protein to control the activity of plasmodesmata in plant cells using blue light.

We show that the stress response pathway, which is activated by the unfolded protein response ( UPR) and endoplasmic reticulum stress ( ER-stress), is required for this effect.
We show that this is due to an increase in the number of apoptotic germ cells in the gonads of animals with increased IRE-1 activity.
We show here that binocular rivalry can occur when visual stimuli are not consciously perceived, and that this is due to competition between the two eyes for access to the same cortical neurons.
We found that when one of two competing gratings was invisible and the other was visible, the invisible grating could still influence the visibility of the visible grating.

We show that rivalry occurs in the absence of conscious awareness of conflicting stimuli, suggesting that rivalry is an automatic process that operates independently of higher-level cognitive processes.

We have developed a new docking method, called"RosettaDock", which is based on a coarse-grained representation of proteins and uses an efficient search strategy to find good binding poses.
We also show that our model is able to predict the binding affinity of proteins in a test set of protein-protein interactions.

The results show that the Next-Generation Radar system is capable of resolving the fine-scale details of atmospheric phenomena such as gravity waves and their influence on the chemical composition of the upper atmosphere.

This study provides evidence for a previously unidentified mechanism by which chlorine is converted from its stable reservoirs to reactive forms in the lower atmosphere and subsequently transported to the upper atmosphere where it can contribute to ozone depletion.
We show that the summertime high-pressure system over the Great Plains is an important factor in determining the duration and magnitude of ozone loss during the summer monsoon season.
The ORF45 protein of Kaposi's sarcoma-associated herpesvirus ( KSHV), an oncogenic human g-herpesvirus, has been implicated in viral DNA replication and immune evasion.
We found that the protein is required for efficient virus release, and we propose a model of how it may function during the process.
We have identified a novel interaction between ORF45 and KIF3A, a kinesin-2 family member involved in transport of vesicles and organelles along microtubules.
We show here that ORF45 is required for efficient transport of viral particles to the nucleus, and that it acts by binding to the kinesin-2 motor protein KIF3A.
We found that microtubules are required for efficient transport of these complexes to the cell surface and that they act as highways for this process.
We show here that KSHV is transported along microtubules by the kinesin-2 family member KIF3A.
In most mammals, including humans and mice, the testes descend from their original location near the kidneys to the scrotum during embryogenesis.

We have studied the evolution of two genes involved in testis descent, relaxin/insulin-like family peptide receptor 2 ( RXFP2) and insulin-like 3 ( INSL3), which are expressed by Sertoli cells and Leydig cells, respectively.

We show that the presence of these vestigial structures in females provides strong evidence for the evolutionary history of testicular descent and suggests that this process was present in the last common ancestor of all vertebrates.
We show that the genetic basis for this evolutionary change is a mutation in the gene encoding the protein INSL3, which is known to be essential for male reproductive development in mammals.
In this study, we investigated how the immune system controls MCMV infection in the salivary glands and found that CD8 T cells are essential for controlling virus replication in these tissues.

We found that CD8 T cells are not required for control of MCMV infection in the salivary glands, but instead CD4 T cells play an essential role by secreting IFNg and activating non-hematopoietic cells to produce type I interferons.
We found that cytotoxic T cell responses against MCMV are restricted to CD8+ T cells and that CD4+ T cells do not contribute to control of infection.

We have previously shown that Wolbachia infection in mosquitoes is able to block dengue virus replication, and we are now investigating whether it may be possible to use this property of Wolbachia to control dengue transmission by mosquitoes.
In this study, we report the successful transformation of a local mosquito species with a novel Wolbachia strain and its impact on dengue virus replication in mosquitoes.

We found that the Wolbachia strain wAlbB-Tw is highly effective against Aedes albopictus, and we propose it as a promising candidate for use in mosquito control programs to combat dengue and other diseases transmitted by this species.
We show that the brain's ability to learn about the causes of sensory input is regulated by the emotional value of these inputs.
We show that this interaction is critical for efficient learning, as it allows organisms to adapt quickly to new situations while avoiding overgeneralization of learned information.
We show here that, in contrast with previous studies, Synechococcus populations are not randomly distributed across the world's oceans but instead occupy specific ecological niches.
We found that type IV chromatic acclimaters ( CA4-A/B) were the most abundant phototrophs in the deep ocean, where they were responsible for up to 50% of total photosynthesis.
We found that some phytoplankton species are unable to perform photosynthesis at night and therefore rely on alternative carbon sources for energy production.
The DNA methylation pattern of an organism's genome plays a critical role in regulating gene expression, which is important for development and disease.

We find that many genomic regions remain inaccessible to transcription factors even after loss of DNA methylation in specific sequence contexts, but become accessible upon global loss of DNA methylation.
We also show that DNA methylation can affect chromatin structure independently of gene expression.

In this study we show that the transcription factor Bruchpilot ( BRP) is required for the development of the adult-specific secretory cells of the Drosophila melanogaster brain.
The STAT6 protein is an important mediator of the host response to viral infection, but its role during SARS-CoV-2 infection has not been studied.
STAT6 is an important transcription factor involved in the regulation of gene expression during allergic responses, including those associated with asthma.
This study provides evidence that STAT6 may be a potential therapeutic target for treating inflammatory diseases such as asthma and rheumatoid arthritis.
However, it is unclear how STAT6 recognizes target genes to regulate their expression.
Our results provide insight into how STAT proteins recognize their target sites, which is important to understanding the function of STATs in gene regulation.
The majority of people with an artificial pacemaker have a single-chamber device which only paces the lower chambers (ventricles) of the heart.
This review found that there is not enough evidence from randomised controlled trials to show whether dual-chamber pacing is better than single-chamber pacing for people with heart rhythm problems.

Quality of evidence The overall quality of evidence for all outcomes was low or very low due to small numbers of participants, lack of information about study methods and results, and imprecision in estimates.
The results showed that patients with a dual-chamber pacemaker had better exercise capacity than those with single-chamber devices.
Plants have evolved an array of immune receptors to recognize pathogen effectors, which are secreted proteins injected into plant cells during infection.

In this study, we found that the expression of NLR genes was positively correlated with the accumulation of their corresponding sRNAs in Arabidopsis thaliana seedlings but negatively correlated in adult leaves.
In this study, we found that miRNAs play an important role in regulating the expression of plant immune receptors.
We found that miR6019/6020 is involved in regulating N uptake and assimilation, and its expression is controlled by both transcriptional and post-transcriptional mechanisms.
We found that miR6019 and its target mRNA are required for TMV resistance in Arabidopsis, suggesting that miR6019 is involved in plant defense against virus infection.
The plague bacterium, Yersinia pestis, is transmitted by fleas and causes severe disease in humans and animals.

In this study we show that, within a decade of the introduction of plague to India, a genetic variant of the rat flea species Xenopsylla cheopis was able to resist infection by Yersinia pestis and thus protect its host from disease.
The model reproduces observed changes in the timing of epidemics, and captures the association between climate and flea abundance.

We show that the emergence of resistant hosts in response to infection pressure is an important driver of disease dynamics, which may have contributed to the decline of plague during the late 19th century.
The ability to survive desiccation is a key trait for the survival and propagation of seeds, but it has been lost by many species during evolution.
We have identified several genes involved in seed dormancy using a combination of genetic, genomic, and biochemical approaches.
We also show that PLATZ1 is required for normal development of vegetative tissues and that its overexpression causes developmental defects similar to those seen in dlt mutants.
Individuals with autism spectrum disorder ( ASD) have an abnormal pattern of brain development characterized by rapid brain growth during childhood followed by reduced growth in adolescence.
We found that the copy number of this gene was highly variable among individuals with autism spectrum disorder ( ASD).

Our study provides new insights on how the immune response to dengue virus infection is regulated, which may help us to develop better treatments for severe dengue cases.
We found that the immune response to dengue virus infection is highly variable between individuals, with some people mounting strong responses while others do not respond at all.
These findings provide new insights on the pathogenesis of dengue and may be useful to develop novel diagnostic tools for early detection of severe disease.
We have used comparative genomics to identify regions of the genome that are under strong selection in humans and other primates.
We find that the patterns of variation in these regions are consistent with background selection, which is the process by which natural selection acts on neutral sites near beneficial mutations.
We show that selection has played a major role in shaping human genome variation and identify several genes under strong positive selection during recent human evolution.

The method is applied to predict the effects of genetic mutations, environmental changes, and disease on metabolism.
We have developed a yeast-based platform for high-throughput screening of GPCR ligands using CRISPR-Cas9-mediated genome editing.
This method allows for the simultaneous screening of multiple GPCRs with different ligands in a single reaction tube, which greatly increases throughput and reduces cost.
We found that several of these receptors are involved in pain modulation, including GPR4, GPR65, GPR68, and HCAR3.
Neurotrophins and their receptors play important roles in neuronal development, maintenance, and death.
Our results shed light on the molecular mechanisms underlying the biological functions of the p75^NTR monomer and its role in neurodegenerative diseases.
Giving babies with low birth weight or who are small for their age more nutrients in their milk may help them grow faster and develop better.
The review found no evidence to support the use of nutrient-enriched formula over standard formula for growth or developmental outcomes in preterm infants.
The review found that giving nutrient-enriched formula to preterm babies at home may improve their growth, but there is not enough evidence to be sure about this.
The trials found no clear differences in weight gain or length and head circumference at 6, 9, 12, 15, and 18 months after birth between babies fed high-energy formula and those fed standard formula.

Trimethoprim is an antibiotic used to treat infections caused by bacteria, including methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus ( MRSA), but its effectiveness has been compromised by the emergence of resistant strains.
The stereochemistry of the phospholipid substrate influences the conformational state of the NADPH cofactor and thus affects the reaction mechanism.
We use a combination of experimental and computational methods to identify the molecular mechanism by which this mutation confers resistance to inhibitors.
We show here that the mutant enzyme uses an alternative conformation of its cofactor to avoid inhibition by one class of inhibitors but not another.
Odorants in nature are often complex mixtures of many chemicals.
The ability to distinguish between different odors is important for survival and has been studied extensively in animals.
In this study, we show that the response of olfactory receptors to mixtures is more robust to concentration changes than their responses to single odorants.
We show that mixtures are processed faster than single odors, and that they provide more information about odor identity.
The authors show that bees are not only important for seed set but also for fruit growth, as they contribute to the production of larger fruits.
However, it is not known how many bees need to be present in a field to achieve optimal pollination services and which bees contribute most to this process.
However, we do not yet know how bees recognize their host plants and whether they use visual cues such as color or shape.
We used the unique isotopic signature of iron to track its cycling through the ocean, revealing that phytoplankton take up iron from both dissolved and particulate sources.
We show that the isotopic composition of dissolved iron in the deep ocean is significantly lighter than expected, indicating that it has been reduced by photoreduction or biological activity.
The ability to predict how genetic variation will be maintained within species has been a major challenge in evolutionary biology.
The evolution of adaptation is driven by natural selection, which favors individuals with traits that are better suited to their environment.
We show that positive frequency-dependent selection imposed by predators on mimicry patterns leads to the loss of variation in wing pattern among individuals within species.

We show that this pattern of selection is consistent with the presence of a genetic conflict between two genes, one of which is responsible for the production of dark spots and the other for light stripes.
We show that the combination of positive and negative feedbacks between form and survival maintains a diversity of phenotypes in this system, each providing different levels of camouflage.
The cochlea is a remarkable sensory organ in which mechanical vibrations of the basilar membrane are converted into electrical signals by specialized sensory cells called hair cells.
We have developed a mathematical model of the outer hair cell ( OHC) to study its mechanical properties, which is essential for understanding how it converts electrical signals into sound waves.
We present a computational model for the dynamics of the actomyosin ring, which is based on a mixture-composite theory of the active lateral wall.
We show that the maximum power efficiency of the outer hair cells is determined by their length, which may explain why they are longer at high frequencies where more power is needed.
The mechanism by which apoL1 kills trypanosomes has been studied extensively, but how it is activated to form pores remains unknown.
We have identified several residues in apoL1 that are important for its ability to bind SRA, and we show that these same residues are also important for the ability of apoL1 to kill bacteria.
The conventional wisdom in treating infections is to use high doses of antibiotics for as long as possible.

One way of increasing the effectiveness of antibiotic treatment is to use combinations of drugs, where each drug targets different aspects of bacterial physiology and together they have a greater effect than when used separately.
We show that when we remove the operon from one of these species, it no longer evolves to become an antagonist.

This work highlights the need for careful consideration of drug interactions in combination therapy regimens to avoid unintended consequences such as increased bacterial resistance or exacerbation of disease symptoms.
In this study, we generated mice lacking all three receptors on macrophages and found that they were unable to control fungal infections.
We found that CLR-deficient mice are unable to mount an effective immune response against C. neoformans, and that this is due to a defect in the recruitment of macrophages to the site of infection.
The CLR-deficient mice developed severe inflammation and died early in infection, suggesting that CLRs are important for controlling the inflammatory response during infection.
In recent years there has been a growing interest in using genetic sequence data to trace the history of the spread of infectious diseases.
However, current methods for reconstructing the evolutionary history of pathogens from genetic data do not account for this spatial structure and therefore may be biased by it.
We present a novel approach to reconstructing both the evolutionary history of a pathogen population ( i. e., its phylogeny) and the network of transmissions between hosts in an outbreak.
In this study, we have identified a novel mechanism by which the mosquito's immune system recognizes and responds to malaria parasites.
We have identified two variants of this gene ( TEP1*S and TEP1*R) which differ in their ability to protect against malaria parasites.
The structure of the full-length protein reveals an unexpected flexibility in the N-terminal region of TEP1, which is not observed for other members of this family.
We have identified amino acids in the TED-MG8 interface of TEP1 that are important for maintaining the stability of the thioester bond, which is essential for catalysis.

This study provides evidence for natural selection acting on the TEP1 gene, and suggests that this gene may play an important role in determining the ability of mosquitoes to transmit malaria parasites.
The hepatitis B virus ( HBV) is an important human pathogen that causes chronic liver disease and hepatocellular carcinoma.
We found that phosphorylation is required for efficient reverse transcription and virion maturation but not for other steps of the virus life cycle.
We also show that the cyclin-dependent kinase 2 ( CDK2) is required for this process and that its inhibition blocks CCC formation.
We show here that phosphorylation of the capsid protein by the viral kinase is required for efficient uncoating and genome release.

This review found that there was no evidence from trials to show whether or not urine tests are useful in identifying children who have UTIs, or whether or not they help doctors decide when to give antibiotics.
We found that 1 in 10 children had a high level of anaemia ( haemoglobin < 8 g/dl) at recruitment, which was more common among girls than boys.
We found that 5% of children had a positive test result for schistosomiasis, but only 0.

The study found that four symptoms ( pain on urination, frequency of urination, urgency to urinate, and suprapubic tenderness) and two urine tests ( nitrite test and leukocyte esterase test) could be used together to identify women who have a urinary tract infection.
In this study we have integrated data from multiple studies to identify genes with altered expression levels in trisomy 21 cells compared to euploid cells.
We show that the deregulation of gene expression due to trisomy 21 is not random, but rather follows a pattern of co-expression between genes on chromosome 21 and their interacting partners.
We show that these changes happen in clusters of genes and that they are associated with specific biological processes.
We find that co-expression changes are more likely to occur between genes in the same functional categories or disease classes than expected by chance.
The results from our study provide insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying ALS pathogenesis and identify potential therapeutic targets.
The mechanism of this allosteric coupling between gates is unknown, but it has been proposed to be based on transmembrane allostery.
The results suggest that the low-pH state is an alternative model for the inactivated state of the channel.

In this study we used mathematical models to investigate how different strategies for controlling tsetse flies in the Lake Kariba area of Zambia could be effective at reducing the risk of sleeping sickness.
We developed a mathematical model of malaria transmission in the Lake Kariba region of Zimbabwe, which we used to predict the impact of different interventions on malaria incidence.
We found that sustained high coverage of insecticide-treated bednets is required to eliminate malaria transmission, and that this will require long-term commitment from governments and donors.
The results suggest that elimination of trachoma in the Lake Kariba region will be difficult to achieve if not all areas are targeted for treatment.

In this study we show that two proteins involved in DNA repair, MutSb and MutSa, which are part of the mismatch repair system, are required to prevent trinucleotide repeat expansion in a mouse model of SCA7.
We have found that the mismatch repair proteins MLH1 and MLH3 are required for both types of expansion.
The results show that the mutations have significant effects on the protein-protein interface, which may be important for understanding how the mutations affect the function of the complex.
In this work, we have used molecular dynamics simulations and free energy calculations to investigate how specific mutations in the ribosome affect the binding affinity of aminoglycoside antibiotics.
The type III secretion system is an essential virulence determinant for many Gram-negative pathogens, but its activity must be tightly regulated to avoid host detection.
Here we demonstrate that the [2Fe-2S] cluster-containing protein IscR is an important regulator of virulence in Yersinia pestis, the causative agent of plague.
This study provides new insights into how pathogens have evolved to sense their environment and adapt to survive in different host niches.
We have identified a novel regulatory mechanism for controlling expression of the LcrF virulence factor, which is essential for plague pathogenesis.
Host defense peptides are important components of innate immune systems in animals and plants, which play critical roles in protecting hosts from infection by various pathogens.
In this study, we have used computational approaches to identify common features of all known a-helical domains in proteins.
We also identified novel peptides with potent antimicrobial activities, including one peptide that was more active than vancomycin against methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus ( MRSA).
This work provides a foundation for understanding the molecular basis of aHDPs and their potential as targets for drug development.
This study provides insight into the mechanisms of muscle and bone loss associated with microgravity and may help guide countermeasures to prevent these changes in astronauts.
In this study we show that the histone methyltransferase SETDB1 and its cofactor KAP1 are required for the transcriptional repression of transposable elements during embryogenesis and gametogenesis.
We have found that the protein hnRNP K, which is known to be involved in RNA processing, binds to the SETDB1/KAP1 complex and is essential for its function in mESCs.

We show that hnRNP K is required for SETDB1 recruitment to ERV loci and that this interaction is mediated by the N-terminal domain of hnRNP K. We also find that hnRNP K is required for efficient SUMO conjugation on ERV chromatin.
These results provide new insights into how chromatin is regulated during ESC differentiation.
The authors of this study aimed to determine whether or not sennetsu is present in Laos by testing blood samples from febrile patients for the presence of Bartonella DNA using PCR.
We found that while many people have antibodies against N. sennetsu, few people develop symptoms severe enough to require hospitalization.
However, the epidemiology of this disease is not well understood in humans or animals.
This work describes a method for controlling gene expression in mammalian cells by using small molecules that bind to specific DNA sequences.
Here we present NetSurgeon, a computational method for identifying such interventions by using network models of gene regulation.

We have developed an algorithm called NetSurgeon that uses network models of metabolism and gene expression data to identify genes whose deletion will improve the performance of yeast strains engineered for biofuel production.

This work provides a framework for understanding how yeast cells respond to changes in their environment, which is important for engineering strains with improved performance in industrial bioprocesses.
The frequent co-occurrence of TERT promoter mutations and BRAF alterations in melanoma suggests a potential link between these two oncogenic pathways.
We show here that ERK1/2 activation by oncogenic BRAF mutations is required to maintain high levels of TERT expression in melanoma cells.
We show that the mutant TERT promoter is a target for ERK1/2, and that this interaction is required to maintain elevated TERT expression in cancer cells.
We have used genetic analysis to identify two RNA splicing factors, UAF-1 and SFA-1, as being required for proper muscle function in C. elegans.
We found that the UAF-1 protein is required for proper splicing of the unc-93 pre-mRNA, which contains an intron that is normally removed during splicing.
We found that mutations in this protein affect the selection of the correct 3' splice site, but not the 5' splice site.
We have developed an assay for studying alternative splicing in vivo, which we use to show that UAF-1 is required for proper splicing of several genes.
The outbreak of Marburg virus disease ( MVD) among miners in Kitaka cave, Uganda, was investigated to determine the source of infection.
We found evidence of Marburg virus infection in Egyptian fruit bats, which are known to be natural hosts for this virus.
We found that the colony size is likely in excess of 100, 000 individuals, with potentially over 5, 000 infected bats.
This study provides evidence that Egyptian fruit bats are a natural reservoir of Marburg virus in Africa, which may be responsible for human outbreaks.
We found that mice lacking B4galnt2 have an abnormal gut microbiome, which is similar to that seen in humans with inflammatory bowel disease.
Here, we show that mice lacking B4GalNT2 are more susceptible to infection with Salmonella Typhimurium and Citrobacter rodentium, two enteric pathogens that cause diarrhea and colitis.
We have used genetic engineering techniques to alter the expression of B4GalNT2 in specific tissues and tested how these changes affect susceptibility to S. Typhimurium infection.
We show that mice lacking B4GalNT2 have an altered gut microbiota, which is associated with increased inflammation in response to infection.

We found that mice lacking B4galnt2 were protected from colitis and had reduced bacterial translocation, whereas mice with increased B4galnt2 expression were more susceptible to colitis and had higher levels of bacteria in their blood stream.
However, it remains unclear whether Zika virus ( ZIKV) causes these abnormalities or if they are due to other factors such as maternal health status during pregnancy.
MicroRNAs ( miRNAs) are small non-coding RNAs that control gene expression at the post-translational level.
In this study, we used computational methods to predict which miRNAs are likely to be produced from the viral genome and which host miRNAs may interact with the virus.
These findings provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms of ZIKV infection and may help to develop novel therapeutic strategies against this disease.

We show that the model plant species Arabidopsis thaliana, which is native to Europe and Asia but now widely distributed in North America, is able to rapidly adapt to new environments by altering its gene expression patterns.
We used genome-wide data from 76 wild Arabidopsis thaliana accessions to reconstruct their evolutionary history in Europe.
We show that the genetic diversity of this species is highest in eastern Europe and decreases as one moves westwards across the continent.
We show that this pattern of genetic diversity is consistent with a recent, rapid expansion of a single lineage from the east.
The results indicate that the species was introduced to Europe with the expansion of agriculture in the Neolithic period.
The emergence of antibiotic resistant pathogens is driven by the acquisition of antibiotic resistance genes ( ARG) from environmental bacteria.
The mechanisms by which these mobile genetic elements spread within bacterial populations and between species remain poorly understood.
This work provides insight into the mechanism by which type II topoisomerases act and identifies a novel class of enzymes with potential applications in biotechnology.
We show here that dsRNA is present in all tissues and cell types examined, including those from healthy individuals.
Alu elements are abundant in human genomes and are known to be transcribed as part of pre-mRNAs.

We find that in humans, there are more Alu elements flanking alternatively spliced exons than constitutively spliced ones and that these Alu elements are enriched near recently evolved alternative exons.
These findings highlight the important role of Alu elements in regulating gene expression and suggest that they may be involved in disease susceptibility.
We show that in addition to being localized to the division site, the septum is also enriched for proteins involved in cell wall synthesis and remodeling.

We have found that the two types of actin filaments are differentially regulated by the actin depolymerizing factor ( ADF) family proteins, which are known to regulate actin filament dynamics in other systems.

The authors have used electron microscopy to study how Mycobacterium tuberculosis grows and has found that it does so by adding new cell wall material at its rear end, rather than at its front end as previously thought.
Stem cells are maintained in an undifferentiated state by a balance between intrinsic factors, such as transcriptional regulators, and extrinsic signals from their environment.
We have found that the absence of the PSC does not lead to increased blood cell production and does not affect the number of blood stem cells.
Our study provides new insights into the mechanisms controlling the balance between self-renewal and differentiation in hematopoietic stem cells.
Herbivores are thought to regulate plants by consuming them, thereby reducing their abundance, biomass, and survival.
Here, we use a large dataset of experimental exclosures to show that herbivores have a strong effect on plant productivity and diversity.
The bacterium Pseudomonas aeruginosa is an opportunistic pathogen that causes severe infections in immunocompromised patients, such as those with cystic fibrosis or cancer.
We found that the identity of these residues determines the specificity of the enzyme for different substrates, as well as its ability to cleave certain proteins.
The results show how the enzyme evolved to become more efficient in its function by changing the protein structure outside the active site.
The daily rhythm of the human body is controlled by an internal biological clock, which responds to light and dark signals from the environment.
In contrast to mammals, where only the retina and pineal gland are known to be directly exposed to light, in fish all body parts are exposed to light.
We have identified a novel cis-element, called the Light Response Module ( LRM), which is required for light-induced gene expression in Arabidopsis thaliana.
We show here that this region is also present in all vertebrates and is required for circadian clock function in mammals.
Our study provides new insights into how light regulates gene expression in plants.
We use an experimental system in which fibroblasts are grown on a surface and their calcium dynamics is measured using fluorescence microscopy.
We show that the oscillations are driven by the interplay between the dynamics of the intracellular calcium concentration and the extracellular calcium concentration.
MRI is widely used for diagnosing many diseases, including cancer, stroke, and multiple sclerosis.
However, there is a small chance that people who receive these dyes may develop an untreatable condition called nephrogenic systemic fibrosis ( NSF).
However, there are concerns about potential adverse effects of gadolinium on the brain.
Here, we report on the design and development of an ultra-small superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticle ( ZES-SPION) as a potential alternative to GBCAs.
The ZES-SPIONs have been shown to be safe in animal models, with no signs of toxicity or inflammation.
The SslE protein of E. coli is an important virulence factor involved in bacterial adhesion and biofilm formation.
We have previously shown that SslE is a promising vaccine candidate against this disease, but its efficacy has not been tested in vivo.
We show here that SslE is an enzyme that degrades mucins, which are proteins found on the surface of cells lining the gut and urinary tract.
We found that the mucinase activity is essential for the virulence of E. coli in mice and that it is required to overcome the mucosal barrier.
We have shown that immunization of mice with recombinant SslEIHE3034 protects against infection with E. histolytica strains expressing variant II SslE from different pathotypes.
These results suggest that SslE is an important virulence factor for E. coli and may be used to develop a broadly protective vaccine against E. coli infections.
The enterohemorrhagic Escherichia coli ( EHEC) family of pathogens includes several strains that cause severe disease in humans, including diarrhea and kidney failure.
The bacteria attach to the intestinal wall using a protein called intimin, which is injected into the host cell through a needle-like structure.
We have isolated a nanobody that binds to an EPEC virulence factor called intimin, which is essential for bacterial attachment to host cells.
Here we demonstrate that TD4 is an important virulence factor for EPEC/EHEC pathogenesis in vitro and in vivo.
These findings demonstrate the potential of nanobodies as novel therapeutic agents to treat or prevent EHEC infections.
Chagas' disease is caused by the protozoan parasite Trypanosoma cruzi, which is transmitted by triatomine bugs.
In this study we have used a novel approach combining phage display with immortalized endothelial cells to identify the specific host receptor for gp85/trans-sialidase.
We have found that phages bind to human endothelial cells in vitro, but not to mouse cells.
We found that FLY binds to the vascular endothelium, and that this interaction is dependent on the type of tissue being targeted.
We have shown that the FLY protein interacts with several intermediate filaments, including vimentin and lamin B1, in addition to cytokeratins.
In this study we show that the intermediate filament protein vimentin plays an important role during Trypanosoma cruzi infection.
Salmonella Typhi causes typhoid fever, which is endemic in many developing countries.

Salmonella enterica serovar Paratyphi A ( S. Paratyphi A) has been considered as a less severe disease than S. Typhi but recent studies have shown that it causes similar symptoms and complications in patients.
We found that the majority of patients had severe disease with high fever, low blood pressure and poor kidney function.
We have used whole genome sequencing to identify genetic variants in a subset of these strains and found that they are genetically diverse, with some strains being more closely related than others.
Salmonella Typhi has been replaced by S. Paratyphi A as the predominant cause of typhoid fever in Phnom Penh, Cambodia.
We found that Salmonella Typhi is more likely than Salmonella Paratyphi A to be resistant to multiple antibiotics and to cause severe disease.
The disease is caused by Coxiella burnetii, which is found in many animal species worldwide.
This is the first report on the use of loop-mediated isothermal amplification ( LAMP) for the detection of M. ulcerans, which has been shown to be highly sensitive and specific.
This study demonstrated that the LAMP assay could be used as an alternative diagnostic method for the detection of Coxiella burnetii infection in humans and animals.
In this work, we have used the budding yeast Candida albicans as a model system to investigate how centromeres are replicated during S phase.
We have determined the genome-wide replication origins and their temporal order of firing in the human pathogen Candida albicans.
We found that the newly formed neocentromere is replicated early during S phase and becomes associated with ORC proteins, which are required for initiation of DNA replication at origins.
We show that the location of the centromere is associated with an early-firing origin of replication and that this association is conserved in yeast species.
We have discovered that the superconducting state is suppressed by a magnetic field and reappears as a vortex lattice when the field is reduced to zero.
We show that the transition to the nonthermal state occurs at a temperature below the critical point, which is a characteristic of a first-order phase transition.
The climate system has been shown to be characterized by tipping points in the past, but these are not well represented in current Earth System Models ( ESMs).
The model results are compared with paleoclimate records from the Sahara Desert, where many models fail to capture the magnitude and timing of the Holocene greening and drying of the region.

We find that the greening of the Sahara Desert during the Holocene was caused by changes in vegetation cover due to climate change, but also that the magnitude of these changes is highly sensitive to the representation of vegetation dynamics within Earth system models.
We show that this model is able to reproduce the major features of the climate record and has an abrupt transition from glacial to interglacial conditions at the right time.
The most common treatment for PAD is to use a vein from another part of the body, called an autologous graft, or a synthetic material such as polyethylene terephthalate (PTE).
The risk of blockage of the graft varies from 10% to 50% at one year.
Antiplatelets and anticoagulants are used to prevent blood clots from forming in the stent.
More research is needed to determine whether anticoagulants reduce the risk of death and improve quality of life for people with cancer.
Hair cells are mechanosensors found in the inner ear, responsible for detecting sound and head movement.
In this study we used a forward genetic approach to identify genes required for protection against ototoxicity caused by the aminoglycoside antibiotic neomycin.
We found that persephone is required for the proper function of mechanosensory hair cells and that it acts downstream of the ion channel TRPML1.
The mutant protein is unable to reach the cell surface, where it would normally function as an ion transporter.
We show here that the protective effect of bicarbonate is mediated by its ability to prevent acidification of the endolysosome compartment in which the toxin accumulates.
We found that both mutants and treated worms have reduced uptake of aminoglycoside antibiotics, which is consistent with the proposed role for Persephone in endocytosis.
Our results suggest that the ion transporters in the lateral membrane of cochlear outer hair cells are important for the sensitivity of these cells to ototoxic drugs.
Acute kidney injury is a common complication in critically ill patients and is associated with increased morbidity and mortality.
This review found no evidence to support the use of one solution over another for people with acute pancreatitis.
The only study we found was small and had some methodological limitations, so we are uncertain about the results.
The review found that there was not enough information to be sure whether or not using lactate-based solutions instead of bicarbonate-based solutions in haemodialysis improves outcomes.
The siderophore staphylopine ( StP) is produced by the major human pathogen Staphylococcus aureus to scavenge iron from host tissues.
The structure of the complex reveals how the protein recognizes metals, which is distinct from other known metallochaperones.
We also show that the metal-binding site is important for StP function in vivo by using CRISPR/Cas9 to introduce point mutations at this site.
An individual's behavior is determined by its internal state, sensory input, and social context.
We have developed a method for quantifying the variability in the behavior of individual animals and how it is affected by their social interaction with other animals.
Here, we show that male and female fruit flies interact differently with each other when they are together compared to when they are alone.
We show that the spatial layout of objects in the environment influences the behavior of individual animals, and that they coordinate their actions with each other to maximize reward.
Yaws is a chronic skin infection of humans caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue.

We have developed a new molecular typing system for Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue based on three genes, which we used to type isolates of T. p. pertenue from humans and non-human primates in Africa and Asia, as well as samples collected from yaws lesions on Lihir Island, Papua New Guinea.
The results show that this method is suitable for epidemiological studies, as it provides a high resolution classification of isolates from different geographical locations.
However, there are many different types of mouse models and it has proven difficult to determine which model best represents human disease.
These results provide a framework for selecting appropriate animal models for studying human HCC subtypes.
However, the factors that determine the rate of disease progression are not well understood.
Inhibitory receptors on CD8 T cells have been implicated as a mechanism of immune exhaustion during chronic viral infections, including HIV-1.
We found that expression of Eomesodermin ( Eomes) and T-bet were associated with high levels of CD39 on the surface of activated T cells.
We found that PD-1 expression was associated with viral load and CD4 counts in HIV-infected individuals, and that it increased as disease progressed.

The expression of these molecules has been linked to disease progression, but it is unclear whether they are causal factors for disease progression or merely consequences of chronic immune activation.
Here we show that mitochondria and lysosomes form close appositions in which they exchange metabolites and ions directly with each other.
We show here that mitochondria-lysosome contact sites are important regulators of mitochondrial Ca^2+ homeostasis.
We show that mitochondrial calcium uptake is regulated by two mitochondrial ion channels, which are activated in response to lysosomal acidification.
Our results highlight the importance of mitochondrial-lysosomal contact sites for interorganelle Ca2+ transfer and suggest that defects in these contacts may contribute to disease pathology.
In this study, we have used the fruit fly neuromuscular junction as a model system to investigate how receptors are targeted to synapses.
We found that the absence of Neto-b results in a reduction of the number of functional synapses and a change in the type of glutamate receptors present at these synapses.
We show that neto-b is required for the proper localization and function of the postsynaptic protein PAK at the neuromuscular junction.
We found that Neto proteins are required for proper localization and function of AMPARs at synapses.
We show here that Neto is an important regulator of NMDA receptors ( NMDARs) by controlling their surface expression levels and localization.
The relative contributions of long-lived greenhouse gases ( GHGs) and short-lived climate forcers ( SLCFs) to the total radiative forcing ( RF) are not well known, especially on a regional scale.
We present a method for estimating radiative forcing ( RF) from changes in land cover using a climate model.

The results show that in developed countries, greenhouse gases ( GHGs) have a larger impact on climate than short-lived climate forcers ( SLCFs), while in developing countries, SLCFs play a much larger role.
The net radiative forcing ( RF) of aerosol particles is negative and large in magnitude, which has masked the warming effect of greenhouse gases.
The results of this study provide an important insight for understanding the role of aerosol-radiation interactions on climate change and its regional characteristics.
Vaccines are designed to elicit protective immunity by inducing antibodies that recognize and bind to specific regions of pathogens.
Here we describe a method for generating immunogens that are recognized by a given mAb.
This approach may be useful for studying the immune response to pathogens, as well as for vaccine development.
In this study, we show that nonlinear microscopy ( NLM), which is based on multiphoton excitation of endogenous fluorophores, can be used to distinguish between benign and malignant breast tissues.

Caffeine for prevention of bronchopulmonary dysplasia in very low birth weight infants Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether caffeine reduces the risk of developing bronchopulmonary dysplasia (BPD) in very low birth weight babies.
Pentoxifylline is a drug that reduces inflammation in the body.
This review found no evidence that pentoxifylline reduced the risk of BPD or death before 36 weeks' gestational age in preterm infants.
One study was included in this review, which compared two different types of surgery (open and laparoscopic) to treat inguinal hernia repair.
This review found no evidence to support the use of these drugs in the treatment of schizophrenia.
The review found no evidence from randomised controlled trials to support or refute the use of pentoxifylline for preventing BPD in preterm infants.
The process of stem cell differentiation is often described in terms of transitions between different states, or"attractors," within a high-dimensional space of gene expression levels.
In this paper, we present a method to construct such landscapes for large networks and show how they can be used to predict the dynamics of gene expression in response to environmental changes.
We show how this framework can explain several experimental findings about the dynamics of gene expression during embryonic stem cell differentiation.
In this paper, we show that the switching rate between these states can be controlled by the level of gene expression noise in the system.
In this study, we have identified a novel class of paramutation in Arabidopsis thaliana, which is associated with changes in DNA methylation and histone H3K9 methylation at specific genomic loci.
The molecular mechanisms underlying paramutation are not well understood, but it has been suggested that small RNAs may play an important role.
We have identified a new gene, RMR1, which is required for the maintenance of DNA methylation in this region.
We show that the DNA methylation patterns of the affected loci are similar to those in wild-type plants and thus cannot explain the observed phenotypes.

This study provides evidence for a new mechanism by which genetic variation in gene expression is generated during gametogenesis, and may have implications for our understanding of how genetic variation contributes to disease susceptibility.
Chagas' disease is caused by Trypanosoma cruzi, a protozoan parasite that infects many organs including the heart.

We found that T. cruzi infection activates the calcineurin/nuclear factor of activated T cells ( NFAT) signaling pathway in cardiac tissue, which is responsible for the induction of cyclooxygenase-2 ( COX-2), an enzyme involved in prostaglandin synthesis.
These results provide new insights on the mechanisms by which Chagas disease causes heart failure, and suggest potential therapeutic targets to prevent or treat this devastating disease.
Heme is an essential cofactor for many proteins involved in oxygen transport, electron transfer, and catalysis.
We have developed genetically encoded fluorescent probes for heme in each of the major organelles of the cell.
We show that the two types of heme have different intracellular trafficking patterns, which may explain why they have different functions in cells.

We show that in the presence of oxygen, cells with high oxidative metabolism have a strong preference for glucose as carbon source, whereas those with low oxidative capacity preferentially consume lactate.
We show that the mitochondrial network is highly dynamic and undergoes rapid changes in its structure and function in response to environmental stimuli.
This work provides new insights on how cells adapt their metabolism to meet changing energy requirements, which is important for understanding cellular behavior in health and disease.
We also find that genes with multiple copies are less likely to have loss-of-function mutations, suggesting that they may be more important for fitness.
We have taken a genome-wide approach to identify genes whose functions are masked by their paralogs, using the nematode C. elegans as our model organism.

We show that essential genes are less likely to be duplicated than non-essential genes, but when they do get duplicated, their duplicates tend to persist for longer.

The four genes in this family were derived from three distinct ancestral sources: one gene is an autosomal copy of a gene found on the X chromosome; the other three genes are copies of genes on the X chromosome.

These results suggest that the Y chromosome is not just an evolutionary dead-end but rather contains novel genes that are unique to mammals and possibly play important roles in male reproductive biology.

Mass drug administration ( MDA) for malaria treatment in areas where there are no cases of malaria Mass drug administration ( MDA) involves giving a full course of anti-malarial drugs to all people living in a specific area at the same time.

This systematic review aimed to identify evidence on the effectiveness of mass drug administration ( MDA) interventions for reducing malaria prevalence and incidence in low- and middle-income countries.
We found that mass drug administration reduced malaria parasite levels in the blood (prevalence) and prevented new infections from occurring (incidence).
The evidence is up to date as of July 2015.

The results suggest that MDA may be an effective strategy for reducing the prevalence of malaria in areas where it is endemic, but more research is needed to determine whether or not this approach will have long-term benefits.

The results suggest that mass drug administration may be effective for reducing malaria prevalence in areas where malaria is endemic but that it should not be used as a substitute for other control measures such as bednets or insecticide treated curtains.

In this study, we show that the protein junctophilin-2 ( JP2) is required for maintaining normal structure and function of the junctional membrane complex in cardiomyocytes.
These findings provide new insights into the mechanisms of cardiac hypertrophy and may lead to novel therapeutic approaches for treating heart failure.

However, the mechanisms underlying the coexistence of susceptible and resistant bacteria in the presence of antibiotics remain poorly understood.
We find that longer duration of carriage increases the benefit of resistance to antibiotics, and that this effect is stronger for more severe infections.
We show that this prediction holds for pneumococcus, which is one of the most important causes of bacterial infections worldwide.

Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for treating chronic musculoskeletal pain in adults Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are commonly used to treat pain and inflammation caused by injury or disease affecting muscles, bones, joints, tendons, ligaments, and other tissues.
The review of trials found that topical NSAIDs are effective at reducing pain and improving function in people with OA of the knee.
The studies included in this review were generally of good quality but were limited by their small size and short duration.
Topical NSAIDs (diclofenac and ketoprofen) were better than placebo in reducing pain at 2 to 8 weeks after treatment started.
Topical diclofenac may cause more side effects than placebo or other topical treatments.
Diclofenac and ketoprofen applied to the skin may be effective for some people with osteoarthritis, but we do not know how well they work for other types of pain.
The review found that there was no clear evidence that any particular type of NSAID worked better than another for pain relief or improvement in function.
The loss of biodiversity is an important consequence of human activities, but it is difficult to quantify and predict.

The introduction of non-native species has been identified as one of the major causes of biodiversity loss, but the role of human-mediated disease transmission in driving these losses remains poorly understood.
We have found that some strains of Bd are more virulent than others, but we do not know how this variation is distributed across the globe or whether it has been influenced by human activity.
We have developed a new genotyping tool to identify genetic variation in a large collection of isolates from around the world and used it to study the population structure of this pathogen.
We also found that some of these lineages were not previously known in North America, suggesting that there is still much to be learned about the diversity of Bd in this region.
