The RuvAB complex is thought to act as a molecular motor, pulling DNA strands together to form an HJ.
We show here that the ruvA mutants are defective for recombination, but not for replication or repair, indicating that RuvA is required to stimulate the action of RuvB on Holliday junctions.
The Antarctic Ice Sheet is currently losing mass, but its contribution to global sea level rise remains uncertain due to large uncertainties in ice sheet model parameters.
We show that the collapse of the West Antarctic ice sheet is likely to be irreversible and will lead to an additional contribution of 3 m to global sea level over the next few centuries to millennia.
Vaults are large, barrel-shaped structures composed of multiple copies of a single protein subunit, which enclose a central cavity containing additional proteins and an RNA molecule.
We have determined the structure of a single vault, which is composed of 13 copies of each of three proteins ( Vp1, Vp2, and Vp3) arranged in a cylindrical shell with an internal cavity.
We have used cryo-EM to determine the structure of the complex, and we show that it is composed of two copies of the major vault protein ( MVP) arranged in a head-to-tail configuration.
We have used microarray analysis of gene expression during the transition from planktonic to sessile life forms to identify genes involved in this process.
We have identified 9 transcription factors that are important for the development of the larval stages and adult life cycle of T. brucei.
We found that one of these genes, rlmA, is essential for the fungus to grow and survive within the host.
Odorant receptors ( ORs) are a large family of G-protein coupled receptors expressed on the surface of sensory neurons in the nose, where they detect volatile chemicals in the air.
However, most of these receptors are retained within the endoplasmic reticulum and have not been studied biochemically.
We have used a combination of experimental and computational approaches to identify the molecular features of ORs that are important for their proper localization at the cell surface.
We have used this approach to identify key amino acid positions in ORs that are important for their function and stability, which may be useful for future engineering efforts.
Human herpesviruses have been implicated in triggering or exacerbating MS relapses, but their role in disease progression is unclear.
The long latency period between infection and disease onset makes it difficult to determine whether there is an association between these two events.
We show here that infection of rhesus macaques with human herpesvirus 6 ( HHV-6) accelerates the development of EAE.
Inoculation of virus in the nasal passages of marmosets did not cause any symptoms but resulted in an earlier onset and increased severity of EAE.
We found that HHV-6 is present in the brains of EAE marmosets, particularly in areas where demyelination occurs.
The aim of this study was to assess the cost-effectiveness of different combinations of interventions for controlling dengue fever, including vaccination and vector control.
The results suggest that Wolbachia is likely to be effective at reducing dengue transmission, but the effectiveness will depend on the specific strain used and the level of infection achieved.
We show that while individual interventions may have some impact on reducing disease burden, they are most effective when used in combination with other interventions.
We found that SPDEF is required for maintaining low levels of Foxm1, which is known to be an oncogene.
We also show that SPDEF is down-regulated in human prostate cancer cells and that this correlates with increased expression of Foxm1.
The neural circuits controlling food intake in Drosophila melanogaster have been extensively studied, yet the mechanisms by which they interact with each other remain poorly understood.
We show here that activation of these neurons increases food intake, whereas inhibition of these neurons decreases it.
These results provide new insights into how the brain balances the opposing actions of two neuropeptides to control feeding behavior.

The aim of this review was to find out whether there were any other treatments available for people with chronic pain in the mouth and face who could not have surgery.
The review found that lumbar sympathectomy and prostanoid therapy are effective treatments for reducing pain in people with severe leg pain due to poor blood flow.
The review found one small trial (40 participants) which compared two treatments, sympathectomy and prostanoid therapy, to see if they could improve blood flow to the legs.

Key results The review found low-quality evidence that people who received prostaglandin treatment were more likely to have their ulcers completely healed and had less need for surgery than those who did not receive prostaglandin treatment.

Quality of evidence The quality of evidence was low or very low due to small numbers of participants in each study, poor reporting of some outcomes, and differences between studies in terms of the severity of symptoms, duration of symptoms, and the presence of diabetes mellitus.

We show that the absence of Vav proteins causes an increase in the number of hair follicles, but this effect is restricted to the skin and does not affect other tissues.

We show that the UNC-40/DCC receptor is required for dorsal migration of these cells, while the UNC-5 receptor is required for ventral migration.
Infants as young as six months old are able to distinguish between similar-sounding words in their native language but not in other languages.
We show that this model predicts phoneme learning from naturalistic speech input, which is consistent with infant data.
Our results provide insight into how infants learn speech sounds and lay the groundwork for future studies on the role of statistical learning in early language acquisition.
Autophagy is a conserved cellular process for degradation of proteins and organelles by lysosomes.
However, it remains unclear how plants recognize and target specific cargoes to autophagosomes.
We show that NBR1 is required for autophagy during light stress but not during other stresses such as darkness or pathogen infection.
Inflammatory bowel disease ( IBD), which includes Crohn's disease and ulcerative colitis, is a chronic debilitating disorder characterized by inflammation of the gastrointestinal tract.
The results show that it is possible to identify proteins in the blood of IBD patients and controls by using microarrays.

In this study we have used mass spectrometry-based proteomics to identify proteins in the blood of individuals with inflammatory bowel disease ( IBD), which is comprised of two main subtypes: Crohn's Disease ( CD) and Ulcerative Colitis ( UC).
We have used this approach to identify genetic variants associated with protein levels in Crohn's disease patients.

We found that genetic variants in the gene encoding Macrophage Stimulating 1 ( MST1), which is involved in inflammation and immune response, are associated with both disease severity and survival time.
We have used these data to identify genes and pathways involved in IBD pathogenesis and to predict potential therapeutic targets for treatment of IBD.

Alvimopan for reducing postoperative ileus after abdominal surgery Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether alvimopan reduces the time it takes for people's bowels to return to normal after abdominal surgery.
Study characteristics and key results We found one study comparing alvimopan with placebo (a pill that looks like alvimopan but does not have any active ingredients).
The review of trials found no evidence to support the use of this treatment for people with schizophrenia.
Key results Alvimopan was more effective than placebo in reducing the time it took for participants to tolerate solid foods and have regular bowel movements after surgery.
The number of people who died in hospital was similar between groups, but there were fewer deaths at home with early discharge.
Key results Alvimopan was associated with a lower rate of major complications and improved quality of life compared to placebo.
Key results Alvimopan did not seem to reduce the number of people who were readmitted to hospital after surgery and did not affect the rate of heart problems or the amount of pain relief used.

Alpha 1 antitrypsin (a1PI) is an enzyme that protects the lungs from damage caused by inflammation and infection.
Study characteristics We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) comparing a1-antitrypsin with placebo or no treatment in preterm infants.
The review found that treatment with a1-antitrypsin reduced the number of infants who died, but this was not statistically significant.

We found that, in addition to the expected effects on gene expression, we could identify specific phenotypes associated with each individual copy number change.
We found that for most of the genes involved in these CNVs, their expression levels were significantly correlated with the copy number variation ( CNV) status.

Cancer cells are known to consume glutamine and excrete glutamate, but it is unclear how this process occurs.
We show that glutamine metabolism is highly compartmentalized and that the majority of glutamine is used by mitochondria for energy production.
We show that the amino acid glutamate is secreted by the bacteria, and we propose that this secretion is used to fuel the biosynthesis of nucleotides required for DNA replication.
This work provides new insights into how glutamine metabolism is linked to glutamate secretion, which may be exploited for therapeutic purposes.
We show that the expression of genes in the developing embryo is highly coordinated and that some genes are more tightly regulated than others.
We used a recently available dataset of gene expression in individual cells from human embryos to identify genes whose expression is constant throughout development.
We have also identified genes whose expression is highly variable between embryos at each stage, which may be useful as markers for these stages.
We show that by analyzing the variation in gene expression across cells within each sample, we are able to identify genes and pathways that play important roles during early human development.
In this study, we have used single molecule imaging techniques to visualize the organization of the Kaposi's sarcoma-associated herpesvirus ( KSHV) genome in living cells.
The formation of these structures is dependent on the presence of specific proteins, and they are thought to be involved in chromosome segregation.
The results presented here provide new insights into how KSHV exploits host cell factors for its replication cycle, which may be useful in developing novel therapeutic strategies against this virus.
We show that LANA oligomers are required for efficient recruitment of host transcription factors, which is essential for the maintenance of KSHV latency.
We have used high-resolution in situ TEM to study the dynamic behavior of gold nanoparticles supported on ceria during CO oxidation reaction.
This work provides an important insight into the size-dependent catalysis by metal nanoparticles and may be useful for designing nanocatalysts with high activity.

This review found no evidence to suggest that one method is better than another for providing nutrients to newborn babies who are unable to feed by mouth.

Key results One study found that using a tube inserted through the skin into a vein in the chest reduced the difference between what patients were supposed to get and what they actually got, by about 7%.
The babies who had a percutaneous line needed fewer lines than those who had an umbilical line.

Key results The review found that, in children with cancer who are receiving chemotherapy and have low blood counts, there is some evidence that using a tube inserted through the skin into a vein (percutaneous central venous catheter) to give intravenous nutrition may increase the amount of nutrients given.
The review found that using a single long-term central line may reduce the need for multiple lines, but there is not enough evidence to be sure about this.

These findings highlight the importance of SOD activity in S. aureus virulence and suggest that targeting this enzyme may be a viable strategy for treating infections caused by this organism.
In this study, we found that S. mutans harbors a unique KAT, SmKAT1, which is responsible for acetylating GtfB and GtfC.
In this work, we have used molecular dynamics simulations to investigate the catalytic mechanism of the KAT enzyme and found that it is able to acetylate Gtfs.

However, the clinical characteristics and outcomes of COVID-19 in ESRD patients are not well understood.
We found 34 studies consisting of 1, 944 COVID-19 cases among 21, 873 ESRD patients on RRT.

The results of this study suggest that COVID-19 is more severe in dialysis patients than in non-dialysis patients, and that mortality rates are higher for those on hemodialysis compared to peritoneal dialysis.

This work provides a general framework to study the dynamics of cascading failures in complex networks.
Leishmania donovani is one of the most important species causing human disease in South America, Africa and Asia.
We have identified a novel target of this compound, which is a protein called tryparedoxin peroxidase.
The compound is active against both drug-sensitive and -resistant parasites, and it does not affect host cells.
The deletion of this gene in L. mexicana results in reduced susceptibility to the drug, suggesting that it is an important target for the action of the compound.

This study provides new insights into the mechanism of action of chalcone compounds against Leishmania and highlights the potential of these compounds to be used in combination with other drugs to treat leishmaniasis.

In this study we have used genetic and biochemical approaches to investigate the function of the mitochondrial inner membrane protein PARL ( Presenilin-associated Rhomboid-like) in Drosophila melanogaster.
We have generated mice lacking PARL and found that they develop a severe neurological disorder with symptoms similar to those of human patients suffering from Leigh syndrome.
We show that PARL is required for maintaining the integrity of the respiratory chain in neurons, and that its absence leads to progressive mitochondrial damage and cell death.
However, in some situations, such as when we are in pain, we tend to seek relief from it.
We show that people's willingness to pay to avoid future pain is not consistent with any of these theories and instead follows an exponential decay function.
We find that the value of future pain is not constant over time and instead decreases as it recedes into the future.
We show that this is because people's dread of pain is proportional to their expectation of pain and decreases as they approach the painful event.
We show that framing an outcome as relief rather than gain can reduce the tendency to seek immediate reward and increase the willingness to wait for it.

We show that this is not true for punishment by pain, and we provide evidence that the effect of pain on choice is instead determined by its immediate hedonic impact rather than any enduring aversive value.

In this study, we have used biochemical and genetic approaches to investigate the role of P-TEFb in transcription elongation.
We show here that Spt4 is directly regulated by environmental stressors and that this regulation is important for maintaining efficient transcription elongation in response to stress.
The light-harvesting complex II ( LH2) is an important component of photosynthetic organisms and has been studied extensively due to its unique photophysical properties.

The results have important implications for the design of artificial light-harvesting systems, as well as for the understanding of natural photosynthesis.

Here, we report a simple method to generate an array of nanodroplets in a microfluidic device with controlled size and volume.
The microfluidic device is designed to confine single cells within nanowells, which are in direct contact with a biocompatible surface.
This system is simple to use, does not require any fine pressure control, and has been shown to be effective in reducing the number of animals required for testing.

We found that TRP is an integral membrane protein with a single predicted transmembrane domain, and it has no homologs in other species.

In this study, we have used a combination of genetic and molecular approaches to identify a set of genes that are expressed in specific cell types during early development of the nematode Caenorhabditis elegans.
We show that these genes are required to maintain the expression of the Hox gene mls-2, which is essential for proper development of the head and pharynx.
We have identified two genes, otx-1 and pitx-1, which are members of the bicoid family of transcription factors.
Glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase ( G6PD) deficiency is an inherited disorder of red blood cells that affects millions of people worldwide.

The role of G6PD deficiency in protection against malaria has been controversial, partly because of the complex nature of G6PD itself and the fact that it is difficult to distinguish between protective and non-protective variants.
We found that some variants were associated with increased risk of severe malaria, while others were protective against it.

In this study we show that in addition to being more susceptible to malaria infection than males, females with two copies of the variant allele at one particular site ( G6PD376) have an increased risk of severe malaria compared to those with one copy of the variant allele.

We show that the genetic diversity in this region is maintained by balancing selection, which may explain why some individuals with G6PD deficiency are protected from severe malaria but have an increased risk of other serious health problems.
In contrast to animals, plants have an additional generation called the gametophyte, which is formed by multiple rounds of cell division after meiosis.
We have identified a mutation in the MOS7 gene that is responsible for this defect.
We find that MOS7 is required for proper localization of the nuclear pore complex and for efficient nuclear import of proteins.

Antibiotics for treating gastroenteritis in children Gastroenteritis is an inflammation of the stomach and intestines (gut) which causes symptoms such as vomiting, abdominal pain, fever, and diarrhoea.
The multiplex gastrointestinal pathogen panel ( GPP) is a new test that detects multiple pathogens in stool samples.
We found that there is not enough evidence to show whether or not giving antibiotics to people with acute gastroenteritis reduces the number of deaths, complications, or length of stay in hospital.

The results suggest that, although there is uncertainty around the value for money of these tests, they may be considered as part of a wider strategy to improve the quality of care provided by the NHS.
The GPP test is not perfect and may give false-positive results in some cases.
This would help us find out if people who have positive tests really do need treatment and if so, how much benefit they get from it.
Cells respond to DNA damage by inhibiting transcription of all genes in order to prevent collision between RNA polymerase II ( RNAP) and DNA repair proteins.
We show here that p300 is required for efficient transcription of genes involved in DNA repair and cell cycle arrest after UV irradiation.
We studied the relationship between female sociality and infant mortality in a group of white-faced capuchins in Costa Rica.
We found that females form close relationships with each other based on their genetic relatedness, social status, and whether they have offspring together.
We found that offspring of more social mothers had better survival rates than those of less social mothers.
Our results show that females may be harmed by their social behavior, and that this harm is caused by males.
The axon initial segment ( AIS) is the site of spike initiation and propagation in cortical pyramidal cells.
Here, I show that this is not necessarily true and that the location of the initial spike generation site has a significant effect on the timing of spikes in response to synaptic input.
Hydrogen is an attractive alternative to fossil fuels for transportation and power generation because it has zero greenhouse gas emissions when used in fuel cells or internal combustion engines.
We have developed a synthetic metabolic pathway for the production of hydrogen gas ( H2) and carbon dioxide ( CO2) from glucose and xylose, two major components of lignocellulosic biomass.
Hydrogen is an attractive fuel for transportation and power generation, but it has been difficult to produce in large quantities using renewable resources.

The vitamin A derivative, all-trans-retinoic acid ( RA), is essential for normal embryogenesis and adult tissue homeostasis.

Lytic polysaccharide monooxygenases ( LPMOs) are a newly discovered family of copper-dependent enzymes that have been shown to be involved in the degradation of crystalline polysaccharides, such as chitin and cellulose.
Lignin peroxidases ( LPMOs) are enzymes that have been shown to use hydrogen peroxide as an oxidant, but the mechanism by which they do so is unknown.
We have used a combination of experiments and computer simulations to study the reaction mechanism of this enzyme.
This work provides insight into the mechanism of action of LPMOs and their potential applications as biocatalysts for lignin valorization.
The enzyme MTHFD1 is responsible for converting formate, which is produced by glycolysis, into tetrahydrofolate ( THF), which is required for purine synthesis.
We found that cells lacking MTHFD1 have reduced levels of purines, thymidine monophosphate ( dTMP), and methionine.

We show that the shape of glioma cells plays an important role in determining the pattern of collective movement, with elongated cells forming more organized structures than round cells.

The amino acid methionine is essential for protein synthesis, but it also plays an important role in tumor growth and progression.
The amino acid methionine ( Met) is essential for tumor growth and metastasis.

The use of bacterial enzymes to degrade L-methionine in the gut is an attractive approach to treat methionine-related diseases but has been limited by the potential for immune response against these proteins.
We have developed an enzyme to degrade L-methionine in humans and show that it is safe and effective for use in mice.
Intracellular protein quality control systems recognize and degrade misfolded proteins, including those resulting from mutations associated with disease.

Intermediate filaments ( IFs) are an important component of the cytoskeleton and play a key role in maintaining tissue integrity.
Intriguingly, intermediate filaments are assembled in a unique manner, with an initial lateral association of monomers followed by longitudinal extension.
We have developed a novel method to study this process in real-time using dynamic light scattering.
The results are consistent with a quantitative model for the mechanism of assembly, which is based on the idea that the two proteins interact through their C-terminal domains.
Neglected tropical diseases ( NTDs) are a group of infectious diseases that affect millions of people worldwide.

We found that the number of publications on these diseases has increased over time, but there is still a lack of research in some areas.
The number of NTD researchers in Germany is small, and their work is spread across many different research institutions.

However, nothing is known about whether or not such an immune evasion mechanism exists in humans infected with Trypanosoma brucei gambiense, the parasite responsible for most cases of sleeping sickness.
We found that Gambiense HAT is associated with low levels of circulating CD8+ T cells, which are important for controlling parasitemia in experimental models of infection.
We found that the levels of antibodies against red blood cells were significantly reduced in HAT patients compared to healthy individuals, and that this reduction is associated with disease severity.
The results indicate that B-cells are functional in HAT patients and that their function is not severely impaired by the disease.

The ability of the host to mount an effective immune response against pathogens is dependent on the presence of functional lymphocytes.

We found that individuals who had schistosome infections when they received their first dose of vaccine did not have lower antibody responses than those who were uninfected, but that those infected at the time of vaccination had lower antibody levels after three doses of vaccine compared to those who were uninfected.
We found that schistosome infection does not interfere with the development of immunity to other infections, such as hepatitis B and tetanus toxoid.

We found that schistosomiasis was associated with reduced immune responses to the vaccine at all time points tested, including before vaccination, after the first dose of vaccine, after the second dose of vaccine, and after the third dose of vaccine.

We found that people who had been treated for schistosomiasis were less likely to have antibodies against the vaccine antigens and that their antibody levels declined faster than those who had not received treatment.

In this study we show that the minus-end-directed motor dynein is essential for the formation of microtubules in the absence of centrioles and microtubule nucleation from the chromatin.
In this study, we present a computational model of spindle formation based on experimental data from live-cell imaging studies.
Mosquitoes are important vectors for several human diseases, including malaria and dengue fever.
Our study provides evidence for an additional RNA silencing mechanism in mosquito cells that targets viral genomes.
The most common type of brain tumor is glioma, which occurs in both humans and dogs.
Some breeds, like the Pug and Pekingese, do not appear to be at increased risk for brain tumors.
We found that some of these genes are associated with glioma risk in humans and may be important for understanding the biology of this disease.
The most common approach to identify genetic variants associated with disease is to test each variant individually against the phenotype of interest.
The method is computationally efficient and does not require any additional computational resources for multi-locus analyses.
The results show that the logistic model is more accurate than the linear model, especially for predicting low risk of disease.
The model is based on the assumption that the probability of a neuron firing depends on the sum of inputs it receives from other neurons.
The human auditory system is capable of extracting information from natural sounds at multiple time scales.
Here we show that the human brain is able to track sound patterns on two different timescales, one in the range of 4-7 Hz and another in the range of 31-45 Hz.
The gut microbiome is known to play an important role in host defense against pathogens, but its influence on the severity of disease caused by enteropathogens has not been fully explored.
The role of specific gut microbiota in the pathogenesis of IBD has been increasingly recognized; however, the mechanisms by which these microbes contribute to disease are not well understood.
We found that mice with different gut microbiota compositions had very different responses to C. rodentium infection.
We found that mice treated with antibiotics had an increased susceptibility to infection, and that this correlated with changes in their gut microbiota composition.
In this work, we show that the presence of superionic water and ammonia in the atmosphere is not sufficient to explain the observed temperature profiles.
Theoretical studies have predicted the existence of several superionic phases in Li10O2, but experimental evidence is still lacking.
We report here the first experimental evidence of a two-stage softening of ammonia at high pressure using Brillouin spectroscopy.
The cell type-specific expression of genes is controlled by the coordinated action of multiple transcription factors.
We have developed a computational method to identify pairs of transcription factors that are likely to interact with each other and act together in a cell type-specific manner.
We show that this approach is able to predict the binding sites of transcription factors with high accuracy, even in cell types where they are not bound.
We also show that our method is able to identify known protein-protein interactions and transcription factor binding sites.
We show that coTRaCTE is able to identify novel and biologically relevant co-regulated genes in different cell types.
In this study we show that the circadian clock protein PERIOD ( PER) is degraded by the proteasome in the nucleus of Drosophila melanogaster.
The mechanism of this repression is not well understood but it has been suggested that the PER protein itself may be responsible for the repression.
In this study we show that PER proteins form dimers with each other and with TIM protein, but also that they can form homodimers of themselves.
We found that PER2 is required for normal PER1 localization to the nucleus, where it regulates its own expression.

We show that the protein kinase casein kinase II ( CK2) is required for the formation and stability of the PER dimer, which is essential for its nuclear translocation and function in circadian rhythm regulation.
We show that PER proteins form dimers in solution, which is consistent with our previous structural studies of PER protein crystals.
The molecular mechanisms of how plants sense and respond to abiotic stresses such as drought remain largely unknown.
We have identified several genes in yeast that are induced within minutes after exposure to stress and have begun to characterize their functions.
We show that these genes are also involved in responses to other types of environmental stresses, including drought, heat, cold, oxidative stress, and pathogen infection.
We find that these genes are regulated by the circadian clock in an oscillating manner, with some being induced and others repressed at different times of day.
We have discovered a new DNA sequence element, called the Rapid Stress Response Element ( RSRE), which is found in the promoter regions of many stress-responsive genes.
We propose that the RSRE is an important regulatory element for coordinating gene expression during abiotic stresses, similar to the role of the SREBP-1c/SCAP complex in yeast.
The germline genome is replicated and then segregated to the daughter cells, but it does not divide itself; instead, it is converted into a new somatic nucleus in each daughter cell.
Here we report on the identification of thousands of IESs in the genome of the ciliate Paramecium tetraurelia using high-throughput sequencing technologies.
We also show that some of the IESs are derived from mobile genetic elements ( MGE) similar to those found in other organisms.

The analysis of cancer genomes has been revolutionized by the advent of high-throughput DNA sequencing technologies, but the extent to which these methods are affected by the limited size and heterogeneity of samples obtained from human cancers remains unclear.
We have developed a computational model of tumour evolution that simulates the process of cancer development in space and time.
This work provides a new way to study the evolution of cancer, which is important for understanding how cancers grow and spread.

Rice seed setting is an important trait for increasing crop yields, but it has been difficult to improve using traditional breeding approaches due to its complex genetic control and environmental sensitivity.
Pollen tubes grow rapidly through the style to reach the ovules, where fertilization occurs.
We found that the mutant style has defects in pollen tube guidance and growth.
We found that the rice mutant sss1-d is defective in the cyclic nucleotide gated ion channel gene CNGC13.
In this study we have identified a novel function of the C. elegans DAF-16/FOXO transcription factor in regulating the expression of genes involved in lipid metabolism.
We found that disruption of insulin-like growth factor ( IGF) -1 receptor signaling in C. elegans activates the transcription factor DAF-16/FOXO, which is known to regulate stress resistance.
We show here that DAF-16 activation is not only dependent on insulin/IGF1 signaling but also on other pathways such as the p38 MAPK pathway.
The fungal infection of plants triggers an increase in cytosolic calcium ( Ca2+) levels, which is essential for plant defense responses.
We found that DAF-16, a transcription factor known to be important for longevity, was also essential for resistance to fungal infections and wound healing.
The pluripotency of EPI cells is maintained by a network of transcription factors including Oct4, Sox2, Nanog, and others.
The mechanisms by which these factors control cell fate are not well understood, but it has been proposed that they act through the regulation of gene expression.
In this study, we show that the transcription factor Sox2 is required for the development of the trophectoderm in mouse embryos.
The coordinated motion of cells is a fundamental feature of living systems, from bacteria to animals and plants.
However, it is challenging to establish a direct link between molecular-scale events and macroscopic behavior because of the large difference in time and length scales involved.
We use an experimental platform that allows us to image large-scale bacterial colonies at high resolution and track individual cells within them.
Peroxisomes are organelles found in eukaryotes and play an important role in lipid metabolism.
We have shown that peroxisomes exist in E. histolytica, but they lack several of the typical peroxisomal matrix enzymes.
We used proteomics to identify candidate peroxisomal proteins from Arabidopsis thaliana and found that only about one-third of these candidates were confirmed as peroxisomal.
We found that the presence of this protein is associated with parasitism in Entamoebae, as it was absent from non-parasitic species such as E. dispar, E. nutalli and E. moshkovskii.
The emergence of a new NA gene segment has been reported in the 2014-15 influenza season, which is associated with increased resistance to oseltamivir and zanamivir.
The emergence and rapid spread of this new virus was likely driven by its ability to evade pre-existing immunity in humans.
We found that the presence of the glycan in the hemagglutinin protein is important for efficient infection of cells by influenza A viruses.

We found that viruses with the 245 NA Gly+ genotype were attenuated in their ability to grow in primary human nasal epithelial cells and mice, while viruses with the 245 NA Gly- genotype had no such attenuation.
We found that the 245 N-glycan blocks access of antibodies targeting the active site, but not those targeting other sites on the enzyme.
We found that the presence of this glycosylation site in the NA protein is associated with increased resistance to human antibody neutralization and reduced sensitivity to oseltamivir treatment.
DNA replication is an essential process in all living organisms.
We show here that the MCM2-7 complex, which is required to initiate DNA replication at origins, is downregulated in response to radiation stress.
We show here that the abundance of MCM proteins is regulated by cell cycle progression and that this regulation is important for origin activation during replication stress.
Here we show that in response to DNA damage, cells gradually lose MCM protein levels, which is accompanied by reduced replication fork speed and eventual cell cycle arrest.
We show here that p53 induces a set of microRNAs ( miRs) that target MCMs for degradation, thereby reducing their levels in stressed cells.
Our results suggest that the MCM2-7 complex plays a critical role in maintaining genome stability during replication stress by regulating DNA replication fork speed and promoting fork restart.
The nuclear receptor liver receptor homolog-1 ( LRH-1) is an important regulator of cholesterol and bile acid homeostasis, but its role in cancer has not been well studied.
Our study provides evidence for an important role of LRH-1 in colorectal cancer and suggests that targeting this nuclear receptor may be beneficial to treat this disease.

We have used RNA interference to silence the expression of liver receptor homolog-1 ( LRH-1) in human colon cancer cells and found that loss of LRH-1 results in reduced proliferation and increased apoptosis.

We show that loss of LRH-1 results in reduced expression of genes involved in lipid metabolism and proliferation, and causes changes in gene expression patterns similar to those seen in human liver disease states.
This phenomenon, called hybrid vigor or heterosis, has been known for over a century but its molecular basis remains unknown.
We found that the F1 hybrids had reduced expression of many genes involved in plant defense responses, which may explain why they are more susceptible to disease than their parents.
We show that the hybrid vigor of F6 hybrids is due to the retention of heterozygosity at many loci, which is not observed in backcrosses between parental strains.
These results suggest that the hybrid vigor effect is not due to an increase in gene expression, but rather to changes in the regulation of gene expression.
The mechanism by which these expansions cause disease is not well understood, but it is believed to be due to the altered structure of the expanded proteins.
However, it has been difficult to determine if protein misfolding and aggregation are relevant to disease pathogenesis in vivo.
We have generated mice carrying an expanded alanine repeat in Sox3 and found that they exhibit hypopituitarism similar to that seen in human patients.
We find that the polyalanine expansion causes misfolding of SOX3 protein, which results in its degradation by the proteasome.
These findings demonstrate that the PA mutation is not only a disease-causing mutation but also a gain-of-function mutation in the context of the full-length protein.
We find that both personality and intelligence are strong predictors of academic performance, but they have different effects.
In this paper we show that it is possible to use microRNA-mediated regulation to control the expression level of any gene in a cell.
In this study, we show that the E2F/RB ratio is tightly controlled by feedback loops ( FFL) involving p107 and p130.
Coxsackie viruses ( CVBs) are important pathogens causing severe diseases in humans, including myocarditis and meningoencephalitis.

We found that coxsackievirus B ( CVB) infection of human brain microvascular endothelial cells is triggered by binding to the cell surface protein decay-accelerating factor, which is located on the apical side of these cells.
We found that CVB infection induced an increase in intracellular Ca^2+ levels and activated calpain-2, a calcium-dependent protease, to control the endocytic trafficking of virus particles.
This study provides new insights into how coxsackieviruses use calcium signaling to enter cells, which may be relevant to other viral pathogens.
The use of autologous EBV-specific T cells for treatment of PTLD has shown promising results, but the role of CD4+ T cells is not well understood.
We found that CD8+ T-cells from mice vaccinated with a vaccine containing a viral protein ( gp33) could recognize infected cells, kill them, and prevent death when transferred to naive mice.
We found that CD8+ T cells are not required to control tumor growth and that CD4+ T cells specific for unknown antigens play an important role in controlling tumor growth.
This study provides new insights into the pathogenesis of PTLD, which will be useful for developing more effective treatments.
We find that the frequency of this mutation is higher than expected by chance in Europeans.
The authors have previously shown that UV light induces mutations at specific sites of the genome and that these are similar to those found in human skin cancer cells.

We have used this approach to compare HIV-1 subtypes A and C in two epidemiologically linked pairs of infected individuals, one pair with subtype A and another pair with subtype C. We found that the genetic diversity of each pair was similar at all time points sampled during the course of the infection.
We found that in most cases, only one virus was transmitted to the recipient and this virus was genetically very similar to viruses present in the donor's blood.

We found that the viral populations infecting individuals who acquired HIV from a non-spouse partner had similar levels of diversity as those who acquired HIV from their spouse, suggesting that sexual transmission does not result in a significant reduction in viral diversity.
We found that in nearly half of cases where an individual was infected with someone other than their spouse, they were infected with more than one variant from that person.
We found that individuals with more than one variant were at higher risk of developing an inflammatory genital infection, but not of acquiring HIV-1.

The social cost of carbon ( SCC) is an important metric for evaluating the economic impacts of greenhouse gas emissions, but it has been criticized as being too low to justify stringent climate policies.
We find that climate change will have large effects on human health, but these effects vary greatly across countries and populations.
We show that the value of climate change damages from reduced mortality is substantial, comparable in magnitude to the value of avoided emissions abatement costs.
We found that the surface morphology of dengue virus ( DENV) particles changes from smooth to bumpy when the temperature is raised from 29degC to 37degC, which is consistent with previous reports.
We have used cryoelectron microscopy to study how mutations in the surface protein of dengue virus affect its structure and stability.

We have used molecular dynamics to show that mutations in the E protein dimer interface are responsible for the loss of function, and we have identified specific amino acid residues that are important for this interaction.
We found that the surface morphology of dengue virus particles is highly dependent on temperature.

We show here that Notch1/Jagged1 signaling increases ETBR expression in endothelial cells through activation of STAT3, which is known to be involved in many biological processes including cell proliferation, differentiation, survival, and migration.
We have developed a method for inducing long-lasting immune tolerance using nanoparticles that carry both an antigen and a small molecule immunosuppressant called rapamycin.

We show that treatment with these nanoparticles induces long-lasting immune tolerance to self-antigens, including those associated with autoimmunity and allergy, and prevents the development of disease models of multiple sclerosis and hemophilia A.
Nanoparticles have been shown to be effective in treating various disease models by modulating immune responses.

In this study, we have investigated the role of two mosquito species, Aedes albopictus and Culex pipiens, in the transmission of two emerging mosquito-borne viruses, Zika virus ( ZIKV) and Usutu virus ( USUV).
USUV is known to cause severe disease in horses and other equines but also causes neurological diseases in humans.
We found that USUV is more efficient in infecting cells than ZIKV, leading to increased virus production and stronger host immune responses.
This study provides new insights on the pathogenesis of USUV and highlights the need for further studies to better understand this emerging disease.
The human body is adapted to live in a wide variety of environments, from tropical rainforests to arctic tundras.
We have scanned the entire human genome for regions where alleles are associated with climate using a large dataset of 61 worldwide populations.
We show that adaptation to climate has played an important role in shaping human genetic diversity, and that this process is likely to have occurred on a large scale.
The immune response to DENV is complex and involves both cellular and humoral immunity.
However, antibodies generated in response to one DENV serotype may not provide protection against other serotypes and can even exacerbate disease upon secondary infection.
The dengue virus ( DENV) is an important mosquito-borne pathogen that causes severe disease in humans.
We found that the presence of defective particles in the blood of infected mice is associated with increased viral load and severity of disease.
EBV-infected B cells are driven to proliferate by two viral proteins, LMP1 and LMP2A, which mimic the effects of normal B-cell activation signals.
We have developed a mouse model to study the role of these two proteins in EBV-induced B cell transformation.
We found that EBV infection of B cells results in changes to gene expression consistent with increased activation and differentiation towards plasma cells.
Our study provides new insights into how EBV infection may contribute to the development of EBV-associated cancers by promoting immune evasion.
Atmospheric aerosols, such as dust, sea salt, organic compounds, and pollutants, play an important role in determining the amount of solar radiation absorbed by the Earth.

We found that films from anthropogenic sources had different chemical compositions than those from natural sources, and that these differences could be used to identify the source of organic matter in environmental samples.
We show that water vapor is rapidly released from these particles at high relative humidities, which may explain why they are often found in dry environments.

This work provides new insights into the chemical composition and sources of organic aerosol particles, which is important to improve our understanding of their impacts on climate change and air quality.
The interaction of small molecules with nanoscale materials has been studied extensively, but the molecular-level details are still not well understood.
We have used molecular dynamics simulations to study the interaction between ethanol and calcite nanoparticles ( NPs) under different conditions.

This study demonstrates how the adsorption of small organic molecules onto mineral surfaces can lead to the formation of highly ordered structures that are similar in nature to those formed during nanoparticle synthesis but with weaker bonding.
The LKB1 tumor suppressor protein is mutated in human cancers and in the genetic disease Peutz-Jeghers syndrome ( PJS).
We found that the uterine stroma is hyperproliferative, with increased expression of cell cycle markers and activation of the mTORC1 signaling pathway.
We found that loss of both PTEN and LKB1 is sufficient to induce cervical and vaginal tumors in mice.
Graphene-based nanomaterials ( GBNs) have been widely used in biomedical, electronic, energy storage, and catalysis fields due to their unique properties.
We have used a combination of experimental and theoretical approaches to study the interaction between graphene oxide ( GO) nanosheets and bacteria.
The vertically aligned graphene oxide nanosheets show superior antibacterial performance against E. coli bacteria.
We show that graphene oxide ( GO) nanosheets disrupt phospholipid membranes and alter their permeability.
Mosquitoes are responsible for spreading many diseases such as malaria, yellow fever, and dengue fever.

Genetic modification of mosquitoes has been proposed as an alternative approach to control dengue transmission by reducing mosquito susceptibility to infection or blocking pathogen replication within the mosquito.

We show that transgenic mosquitoes expressing a single-chain variable fragment of a broadly neutralizing dengue virus ( DENV) antibody are highly resistant to infection by all four major DENV serotypes.
This study provides an important step toward the development of a broadly protective vaccine against dengue virus infection.
This study provides a new approach to develop an effective vaccine against dengue and potentially other mosquito-borne viruses.
Here we present the first high-resolution structure of an Hfq protein, which is involved in posttranscriptional regulation of gene expression in bacteria and archaea.
The structure of the full-length protein is presented here at near-atomic resolution.
We show that the C-terminal domain of SsrB is important for its function, and we present a model for how it may act to regulate RNA binding by Hfq.
We also show that the C-terminal domain of Hfq is important for RNA binding and that it may be involved in regulating the function of Hfq in different species.
The SARS-CoV-2 virus uses its surface protein, called the spike protein, to bind to host cells and enter them by fusing the viral membrane with the cell membrane.
The ability to control the shape and structure of self-assembled materials is important for understanding how cells interact with their environment and each other.

We show here that by using a combination of microfluidic devices and external fields, it is possible to shape liquid crystals in a controlled manner into various shapes, including fibers, helices, and vesicles.
We have developed a simple, robust, and versatile method for creating microdroplets of any size and shape by using two surfactants with different solubility properties.
We show that by tuning the temperature-dependent properties of liquid crystals, we can transform an emulsion of spherical droplets into branched fibers and vice versa.
We show that these liquid crystal droplets are capable of self-shaping and self-healing, which is similar to biological membranes.
We show that the shape change is driven by an interfacial energy term that favors an increased surface area and is balanced by the elastic energy cost of bending.
We present a mathematical model of the COVID-19 pandemic that incorporates real-time mobile phone data to account for human mobility and its impact on disease transmission.
We show that our method is able to provide robust results for different scenarios, including those with realistic mobility restrictions, and that they are consistent with epidemiological data.
ToxIN is a type of toxin-antitoxin system that has been shown to mediate abortive infection in Escherichia coli.
We show here that this phage encodes an antisense RNA that suppresses the activity of a bacterial toxin-antidote system, thereby allowing the phage to replicate in the presence of the toxin.
We also found that some of these new viruses were able to spread their own genes for toxin production to other bacteria by transduction, thus spreading the gene for altruistic behavior.
The cell wall is an essential structure for all living organisms, providing mechanical support to cells and maintaining their shape.

The mitotic exit network ( MEN) is an essential pathway for regulating cell cycle progression in eukaryotes, while the cell wall integrity ( CWI) pathway is important for maintaining cell shape and cell wall integrity.
In this study, we found that three proteins ( MoSep1, MoDbf2, and MoMob1) are required for the formation of the MPM in the filamentous fungal pathogen Magnaporthe oryzea.
We also found that MoSep1 is required for activation of the cell wall integrity ( CWI) pathway by phosphorylating its target MoMkk1.
We found that MoSep1-mediated activation of MoMkk1 is critical to maintain the balance between cell wall integrity and cell division in M. oryzae, which is important for fungal pathogenicity.
Water is an essential component of Earth's interior, but its transport and storage are not well understood due to the complex phase behavior of water under high pressure and temperature conditions.
The transition zone is thought to be a major water reservoir in the Earth's interior.
Our results suggest that stishovite is capable of incorporating significant amounts of water in its crystal structure under conditions relevant to Earth's lower mantle.
The electrocatalytic conversion of CO2 to value-added chemicals and fuels is an attractive approach to utilize CO2 as a feedstock.

This work demonstrates how the synergy between the molecular catalyst and the nanoparticle surface enables efficient CO2-to-CO conversion with high selectivity, which is not observed in homogeneous systems.
SATB2 is an important regulator of neuronal function and has been implicated in learning and memory.
We found that common genetic variants at the SATB2 locus are associated with schizophrenia, but not with intellectual disability.
We have used mass spectrometry to identify proteins that bind to SATB2 during development of the cerebral cortex and in adulthood.
We find that SATB2 is required to maintain long-range chromatin interactions, and that it does so by binding to specific DNA sequences in the genome.

We find that common genetic variants associated with intelligence in humans are enriched for interaction partners of SATB2, which is expressed in the developing brain and has been implicated in neurodevelopmental disorders.

These findings suggest that SATB2 may play an important role in regulating gene expression during development, but also has additional functions in adult brain that are not related to its developmental roles.
The ability to mask the cell wall polysaccharide ( 1, 3) -b-D-glucan from immune recognition is a key virulence determinant for C. albicans.
We show here that the cell wall of Candida albicans is covered by an outer layer of b-glucans that masks the presence of other cell wall components from the host's immune system.
In this study, we show that the Cek1 MAPK pathway is required for unmasking in response to stress and that it acts by regulating the phosphorylation state of the protein Nrg1.
We found that the clearance of the STE11DN mutant is dependent on the host immune response and that it is not cleared by the innate immune system but rather by T cells.
We found that Ste11DN467 induces a novel signaling cascade involving Dfi1 and Ssn6 to activate transcription of genes required for filamentation.
The studies were all of poor quality and had small numbers of participants, which made it difficult to be certain about the results.
The review found that there is some evidence to suggest that cord clamping at 1 minute may reduce the risk of anaemia for the baby, but more research is needed to confirm this.
Quality of evidence The quality of evidence was low for all outcomes due to small numbers of participants in each study and some concerns about how well the studies were conducted.
NK cells are known to be involved in the pathogenesis of BA, but their role is not fully understood.
We have previously shown that high-mobility group box-1 ( HMGB1), a proinflammatory mediator, is involved in the pathogenesis of rotavirus-induced BA.
We found that immature NK cells have low cytolytic activity against infected cholangiocytes and are unable to control rotavirus infection in the liver.
We found that NK cells were activated in response to rotavirus infection and that this activation was associated with increased cytotoxicity against infected biliary epithelial cells.

We found that natural killer ( NK) cells play an important role in protection against rotavirus-induced BA and that adoptive transfer of mature NK cells before rotavirus infection prevented BA in neonatal mice.
Toxoplasma gondii is an apicomplexan parasite that infects a wide variety of hosts, including humans and mice.

The mechanism of this process involves the formation of a tight junction between the parasite and the host cell membrane, which is mediated by proteins injected from the parasite's apical secretory organelle called the rhoptry.
The mechanisms by which these proteins are targeted to the rhoptries and how they are released from the parasite during invasion remain poorly understood.
We found that TgDHHC7 is required for the formation of the rhoptry organelles, which are essential for invasion of host cells by the parasite.
We have found that the apical end of the rhoptry is essential for attachment to the host cell surface during invasion.
We show that the rhoptry neck is essential for invasion but not for attachment or egress.
This study provides novel insights into the biogenesis and function of apicomplexan rhoptries, which are essential for host cell invasion by these pathogens.
We show that the ocean's interior is not as homogeneous as previously thought, but instead exhibits distinct layers of varying temperature and salinity.
The results indicate that the deep ocean is not as isolated from the surface waters as previously thought, with implications for global biogeochemical cycling.
The results are important for understanding the dynamics of the present-day ice shelf and its response to climate change.
The measurement of symphysis fundus height ( SFH) is used as an indicator of fetal size.

This review found that there was no evidence from randomised controlled trials that routine use of SFH measurements improves outcomes for babies, but it may be useful in identifying those at risk of IUGR.

The review found that measuring the distance between the top of the pubic bone and the bottom of the uterus (symphysis-fundus height) by tape measure is not as accurate as ultrasound for estimating the size of the baby's head.
This review found no evidence to support the use of antibiotics for preventing preterm birth.
The review of trials found that there was not enough information to be sure if measuring the size of the fundus of the uterus (SFH) could detect growth restriction.
Transcription factors are proteins that bind to DNA and regulate gene expression by recruiting other proteins to specific sites on the genome.
This process involves the binding of proteins to specific DNA sequences and/or post-translational modification of histones.
The location of genes in the nucleus is important for their expression, and this positioning is regulated by transcription factors.
In this study, we show that the yeast protein Ssn6 is required for the proper localization of many genes to the nuclear periphery.
Here we show that the absence of HNF1A leads to the repositioning of genes from their normal location at the nuclear periphery towards the nuclear interior.
Our results provide new insights into how gene expression is regulated at the level of nuclear architecture.
The ability to control the levels of specific proteins within living cells is an important tool for understanding how proteins function, as well as for treating disease.

Thalidomide, an anti-cancer drug used in multiple myeloma treatment, is known to target the E3 ubiquitin ligase Cereblon ( CRBN), which selectively degrades the Ikaros family of transcription factors.

We find that both salinization and alkalinity have increased substantially across much of the country, with 37% of the land surface experiencing increases in salinity and 90% experiencing increases in alkalinity since 1900.
We show that high levels of sodium in soils can lead to increased alkalinity through displacement of calcium and magnesium ions from soil exchange sites.
The effects of this phenomenon are widespread, including increased risk of infrastructure damage, mobilization of contaminants from sediments, and changes to coastal water chemistry.
We have used genetically engineered mice to study how FUS mutations affect normal function.
We found that activation of mGluR1/5 increased FUS protein levels in both wild-type and mutant neurons, suggesting that this receptor may be involved in the pathogenesis of ALS.

Sclerotherapy for leg telangiectasia Background Telangiectasias are small dilated blood vessels (capillaries) on the surface of the skin, which may be visible as red spots or patches.
The review authors found no evidence to support the use of any particular type of exercise for people with schizophrenia.

Study characteristics The review authors found six studies (involving 246 participants) that compared sclerotherapy to either a placebo (a solution of normal saline), no treatment or another type of treatment.
The review found no evidence from randomised controlled trials to support the use of any specific treatment for this condition.
The review found that sclerotherapy is better than placebo (a fake treatment) in reducing the number of varicose veins and improving quality of life.
There was no clear difference between the different treatments in terms of how well they worked and how satisfied patients were with them.
The ability of cells to respond to environmental stimuli is dependent on their capacity to rapidly alter gene expression in response to changes in the environment.
The cap-binding complex ( CBC) is responsible for binding to the mRNA cap and escorting it from the nucleus to the cytoplasm.

In this study, we show that NCBP3 plays an important role in the innate immune response against viral infection by regulating the activation of IRF-3, which is a key transcription factor for IFN production.
We found that NCBP3 is required for efficient viral replication in vitro, but not in vivo.
We found that NCBP3 is required for efficient translation of many mRNAs and appears to act as an adaptor between mRNA and ribosomes.

The DNA methylation of cytosines is one such epigenetic modification, which has been shown to play a key role in many biological processes including gene expression regulation, genomic imprinting, X-chromosome inactivation, and cancer development.
We have used this approach to study the DNA methylation patterns in human peripheral blood mononuclear cells, which are the primary cell type for most genetic studies.
We show that this phenomenon occurs frequently in human cells, and we identify several factors that are associated with it.
This work demonstrates the power of next-generation sequencing technologies to provide high-resolution maps of genome-wide chromatin modifications in mammalian cells.

We found that EchidnaCSI was an effective way to engage people in research and conservation, and that it provided opportunities for people to learn about echidnas and their habitats.

The EchidnaCSI project has demonstrated the power of citizen science in providing valuable genetic information on a poorly known species, and we believe it will be an important resource for future studies of echidnas.
Dengue is an infectious disease caused by four different viruses ( DENV-1 to -4) transmitted by mosquitoes.
We show here that activation of endogenous sialidase activity on platelets and endothelial cells is responsible for this phenomenon, which may contribute to the pathogenesis of thrombosis in sepsis.
In this study, we investigated whether or not the antiviral drug oseltamivir could prevent these complications by blocking the virus from infecting cells.

Evolutionary biologists have long debated whether evolution is an unpredictable process or follows predictable patterns.
We show here that, despite their different evolutionary histories, all animals have evolved similar mechanisms to regulate gene expression in response to environmental stimuli.

This study demonstrates how a common problem has been solved by different species using the same molecular mechanism, illustrating how natural selection can act as an architect of convergent evolution.

We show that the kinks are stabilized by differences in the lengths of the filament segments between adjacent links or by the presence of dislocations ( defects) at the ends of some filaments.
We also show how the kinked state is unstable with respect to small perturbations and how it relaxes back to the straight state.
Dengue is the most prevalent mosquito-borne viral disease in the world, with no specific treatment or vaccine currently available.

The review found that there is some evidence that insecticide-treated bednets reduce malaria, but more research is needed to determine how effective they are and whether or not they should be used as part of a strategy to control malaria.
The studies included in this review were of poor quality, and only nine of them were randomized controlled trials.

We found that using water containers with lids reduced mosquito breeding in water containers by 80% when used alone, and by 99% when combined with community-based environmental management of mosquito breeding sites.

We found that people who had been bitten by mosquitoes in the past year, and those who reported having symptoms of dengue fever or chikungunya fever in the past year, were more likely to have antibodies against ZIKV than those who did not report these symptoms.
The authors found no evidence to support the use of any specific insecticide or application method, but noted that some methods may be more effective than others in certain circumstances.
More research is needed to identify the most effective ways to prevent dengue, especially in areas where it has not been previously reported.

The environmental impacts of shale gas development are an important public concern, but there is limited information on the magnitude of greenhouse gas ( GHG) emissions associated with shale gas production and use.
However, recent studies have reached conflicting conclusions about the magnitude of these impacts.
We find that, on a per unit of electricity generated, methane emissions from shale gas are comparable to those from conventional natural gas and significantly lower than those from coal.

The identification of genetic variants associated with complex traits has been greatly facilitated by genome-wide association studies ( GWAS).
The number of experiments needed for such an approach is very large, which makes it difficult to perform with limited resources.
However, increasing the number of experiments is not always feasible due to cost and time constraints.
However, it is not clear whether integrating multiple types of data will improve the performance of these methods.
In this paper we show that by integrating genetic variation with gene expression data from a segregating population, we can improve our ability to predict how genes interact with each other.
Wolbachia are naturally occurring bacteria that live inside insects and have been shown to block dengue virus replication in mosquitoes.
Mosquitoes are attracted to CO2, and this is used as a lure in traps to capture them.
The introduction of Wolbachia into mosquitoes has shown great promise as a means of controlling mosquito-borne diseases such as dengue, malaria, filariasis, and Zika virus.
We show that this approach is effective in blocking both dengue and Zika virus infection in mosquitoes.

The Sonic Hedgehog ( Shh) signaling pathway plays an important role in embryogenesis and tissue homeostasis, but its dysregulation has been implicated in cancer progression.
The Hedgehog ( Hh) signaling pathway plays an important role in embryonic development and tissue homeostasis.

We show here that, in contrast to previous studies, Shh is not required for the expression of Gli1 and Gli2 in the developing limb bud.
This study provides evidence that the Shh signaling pathway is involved in the regulation of electrical activity in developing neurons, which may be important for their maturation.
Buruli ulcer ( BU) is a neglected tropical disease caused by Mycobacterium ulcerans, which produces a toxin called mycolactone.

We have previously shown that mycolactones are present in skin lesions of patients with Buruli ulcer, but it was not known whether they could diffuse out of the lesion or if their presence was affected by treatment.

In this study, we have developed and validated an analytical method for the detection of these compounds in human plasma using high-performance liquid chromatography coupled to mass spectrometry ( HPLC-MS).
We found that mycolactone is present in the skin lesions of Buruli ulcer patients and in their blood.
We found that mycolactones are present in the blood of patients with BU lesions and that their levels decrease after treatment.
The protein TANK-binding kinase-1 ( TBK1) plays a central role in the activation of innate immune responses to viral infections, including influenza virus.

We found that TBK1 is essential for restricting bacterial growth in macrophages and that it does so by phosphorylating IRF3, which is then degraded.
We found that TBK1 is required for the formation of a functional vacuole in which Salmonella replicates.
These findings reveal an unexpected function for TBK1 in controlling bacterial infections and suggest that TBK1 may be a potential therapeutic target to treat bacterial disease.

Our results show that combining machine learning with domain-specific feature selection improves prediction accuracy, and that ensembling of multiple models trained on different data sets leads to improved performance over any single model.

This study aimed to find out whether or not the Safer Nursing Care Tool could be used by hospitals to decide how many nurses they need.
This study aimed to find out whether or not using the Safer Nursing Care Tool ( SNCT) would be good value for money for the NHS.
We also looked at how well the tool worked in practice and what it would cost if used as a basis for decisions about nurse staffing levels.
We found that nurses were able to provide good-quality care for most patients, but there was some variation in how well this happened.
We found that wards with fewer nurses reported less good-quality care, had higher rates of missed nursing care and had poorer patient outcomes.
This study has shown that there are many factors that affect how much work nurses have to do and that these vary from day to day.

This study found that the tool did not accurately predict how many nurses were needed to provide safe care, and it was not possible to use the tool to determine whether or not there were enough nurses on shift.
The cost savings from reduced overtime and agency staff costs were offset by increased permanent staffing costs, so there was no net saving.
We also found that there was no clear evidence of how many staff are needed in each area, and we recommend further research on this topic.

We found that individuals who developed PML while on natalizumab treatment had an impaired immune response to JCV and that this was associated with low levels of virus-specific CD8 T cells.
These findings may help guide future studies on the pathogenesis of PML and provide insights for the design of new strategies to prevent or treat this disease.

We found that P. vivax has had a larger population size than P. falciparum over the last 100 years, suggesting that it is better adapted to its environment and has been less affected by malaria control interventions.

In a series of experiments, we show that humans do not always follow these rules and that their behavior is often inconsistent with rationality.

Prions are infectious proteins that cause fatal neurodegenerative diseases in humans and animals.
Chronic wasting disease ( CWD) is a fatal neurodegenerative prion disease affecting wild and domesticated cervids in North America.

We found that transmissible mink encephalopathy ( TME) and chronic wasting disease ( CWD) prions from moose and reindeer caused disease in transgenic mice expressing either elk or deer prion protein, but not in mice expressing mouse prion protein.
We found that the same amino acid position in the prion protein was associated with differences between elk and mule deer CWD strains, but not between elk and white-tailed deer CWD.

Inhaled beclomethasone dipropionate versus inhaled budesonide for chronic asthma in adults Inhaled steroids, such as beclomethasone dipropionate (BDP) and budesonide (BUD), are used to treat asthma by reducing inflammation in the airways.
Fluticasone is an inhaled corticosteroid used for treating asthma.
This review found no evidence from randomised controlled trials that fluticasone is better than beclomethasone or budesonide for treating people with asthma.

The review found that when used in combination with another steroid (beclomethasone dipropionate) or budesonide, fluticasone propionate was more effective than beclomethasone dipropionate and budesonide for reducing symptoms of asthma.

The review found that fluticasone is better than placebo for improving symptoms of asthma, but there was no clear evidence that it is better than other inhaled steroids such as beclomethasone or budesonide.

Study characteristics We searched for randomised controlled trials of children under three years of age with bronchiolitis who were treated with either steam inhalation or cool mist compared to other treatments such as bronchodilators, corticosteroids or placebo.
The review found only one small trial, which included 156 infants who were given either dexamethasone or placebo.

Key results The review found that nebulising salbutamol reduced the severity of breathing difficulties, as measured by the respiratory distress score, compared to either mist in a tent or nebulised normal saline.

The review found no evidence of benefit for nebulised saline compared to placebo or other treatments, but since there were only two studies included in this analysis and they had different results, we cannot be sure whether or not nebulised saline is effective.
There was not enough information from these studies to be sure whether steam inhalation helps with symptoms of bronchiolitis, and more research is needed.

The meiotic chromosome is composed of two homologous copies of each chromosome, which pair with each other and exchange genetic material during recombination to form a single copy of each chromosome in the gametes.
The first round of chromosome segregation is called meiosis I, and it involves programmed recombination between homologous chromosomes to form crossovers.
We show that the conserved AAA+ ATPase PCH2 is required for this process and that it acts by promoting the degradation of the microtubules at the site of reorientation.
We show that loss of PCH2 results in an accumulation of ASY1 along the chromosome axis, which is associated with defects in chromosome segregation.

We show that the absence of Atpch2 leads to defects in meiotic chromosome segregation and recombination, which are consistent with the role of this protein in regulating the structure of chromosomes during meiosis.
The fossil record of early Pleistocene hominins in Europe is sparse and fragmentary, but recent discoveries have provided new insights into human evolution in this region.

This new discovery provides further evidence for the presence of Neanderthals in southern Iberia during MIS 9, and adds to the growing list of diverse human fossils from this region.

We have identified two enzymes that degrade PNAG and show that these enzymes are effective in disrupting biofilm formation of several species including S. aureus, B. pertussis, S. carnosus, S. epidermidis, and E. coli.
The enzyme was found to hydrolyze the glycan chain from the nonreducing end, and mass spectrometry analysis revealed that it cleaves between two N-acetylglucosamine residues in the glycan chain.

Sensory neurons are constantly bombarded by noise from various sources and therefore need to be able to extract reliable information from these noisy inputs.

The ability to represent objects' locations in space is fundamental for our daily life, and it is achieved by combining information from multiple sensory inputs.

We found that most neurons were tuned to sounds coming from specific locations in space, but some neurons responded to sounds regardless of their location.

A ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm ( AAA) is a life-threatening condition in which the large blood vessel that carries blood from the heart to the rest of the body bursts, causing severe pain and bleeding.
Key results The review of trials found that, compared to open surgery, endovascular repair is associated with fewer deaths and less blood loss during or after treatment.
This study aimed to find out if using an endovascular technique is better than surgery for people who have had a ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm.
The study found that patients who had an immediate CT scan before surgery were more likely to have their AAA repaired using keyhole surgery than those who did not have a CT scan.
The proportion of patients who died within 30 days was similar between groups ( 2.
The proportion of patients who went straight home after surgery was higher in the endovascular group ( 94% vs. 77%).
The results showed that at 1 year after surgery, people who had an endovascular procedure were less likely to have died than those who had open surgery.

We have used a mouse model to investigate how herpes simplex virus ( HSV) infection is controlled by the immune system and how it is resolved after the initial response has been mounted.
We found evidence for cell death and tissue damage during reactivation, which was associated with the presence of CD8+ T cells.
We found that the presence of the virus did not alter the number of infected neurons, but it did affect their morphology and function.
We found that the fragmentation of the host genome is dependent on viral DNA replication and does not require viral protein expression alone.

This study provides evidence for an alternative mechanism by which HSV-1 infection is resolved from latency, and raises important questions regarding the role of viral gene expression during reactivation.

The origins of the Turkic peoples are not well understood, but archaeological evidence suggests they originated on the Eurasian steppes.
We find that genetic diversity is highest in Central Asia, and that many populations are genetically similar to their geographic neighbors.
We find that modern-day Turkic-speaking groups in Europe share more genetic material with ancient and contemporary Central Asian populations than do other European populations.
We also found evidence for recent gene flow from European populations in the last few hundred years, which is consistent with historical records of European colonization.

Our results suggest that the Turkic people have a complex history involving multiple migrations and admixtures with other ancient groups, including the Mongols, and that they are genetically similar to modern-day Kazakhs and Uzbeks.

In this study, we show that the process of recombination is not uniform but rather consists of two distinct types of events: short, frequent exchanges ( micro-recombinations) and longer, less frequent exchanges ( macro-recombinations).
We found that macro-recombination is a major driver of genetic diversity in this species, and that it has been responsible for many of the major evolutionary transitions observed in the genome.

We show that the two types of recombination are likely to be caused by different biological mechanisms, with micro-recombination being driven by the movement of transposable elements ( mobile DNA sequences) within genomes, while macro-recombination is driven by sexual reproduction in eukaryotes.

Advance care planning is an approach to help people think about what kind of care they would want if they were unable to make decisions for themselves.
