We wanted to find out what happens when people have an advance care plan ( ACP) and whether or not it makes any difference to the quality of their care.
We interviewed 37 health professionals and followed up 21 patients for 6 months to find out what they thought about ACP.

Inhaled steroids for chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) Review question We wanted to find out whether or not using inhaled steroids improved symptoms and lung function in people with COPD.
The review found that there was not enough evidence to show whether or not tiotropium is better than placebo for people with COPD.
ICS reduced the rate at which people with COPD got worse over time, but did not improve lung function or reduce exacerbations.
The review found that there is evidence that people who take inhaled corticosteroids are more likely to develop oral thrush and hoarseness than those who do not use these drugs.
Mosquitoes are responsible for spreading many serious human diseases such as malaria, dengue fever, chikungunya, yellow fever, and Zika virus.
We present a mathematical model to study how the spatial distribution of people and their homes affects the spread of diseases transmitted by mosquitoes.

We find that the most cost-effective strategy is a combination of larval source reduction and adulticide applied at high frequency in non-compliant areas, with lower frequency applications in compliant areas.

We find that cost optimal strategies for controlling dengue transmission in urban areas depend on several factors including the rate at which mosquitoes move between houses, the proportion of households that receive insecticide-treated bednets, and how close together treated houses are located.

In this study we show that RsfA is required for adaptation to slow growth conditions by controlling translation initiation.

The test is not able to differentiate between different types of tapeworms.
The diagnosis of schistosomiasis is based on finding eggs in stool or urine, but this method has limitations and may not be accurate for all individuals.
This study provides a framework for the evaluation of incidental findings of Taenia spp.
Prostate cancer is a common disease in older men and surgery to remove the prostate gland (radical prostatectomy) is one of the treatments available.

Robotic-assisted versus open surgery for prostate cancer removal in men with localised disease Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether robotic-assisted surgery is better than open surgery for removing the prostate gland in men with localised prostate cancer.
This review found that, for men with localised prostate cancer, there is no clear evidence that one type of surgery (open, laparoscopic or robotic) is better than another.

We found no evidence to suggest that radiotherapy is better than surgery for men with localised prostate cancer in terms of overall survival or disease-specific survival (the time from diagnosis until death due to prostate cancer).
Key results The review found that robotic-assisted radical prostatectomy may be associated with fewer blood transfusions and shorter hospital stays compared to open surgery.
Key results The review found that there is insufficient evidence to show whether or not robotic-assisted radical prostatectomy reduces postoperative pain compared with open surgery.

Parkinson's disease is caused by mutations in the genes encoding for the proteins PINK1 and Parkin, which are involved in mitochondrial quality control.
Parkin is activated by phosphorylation at Ser65 within its Ubl domain by PINK1, which is recruited to damaged mitochondria.
We propose a model for how PINK1 activates Parkin through phosphorylating mitochondrial polyUb chains to facilitate its rapid translocation onto mitochondria.
Chagas disease is caused by the protozoan parasite Trypanosoma cruzi, which infects cells in multiple organs throughout the body.
Intracellular T. cruzi amastigotes have been shown to be dependent on host cell lipids and cholesterol for survival.
In order to address this question we have used mass spectrometry-based lipidomics to identify lipids in both the parasite and its host cell.
We found that the majority of lipids in T. cruzi are derived from host cells, including most of its triacylglycerols.
The dengue virus is one of the most important mosquito-borne viral pathogens, and it has been estimated to infect over 390 million people annually.
The severity of dengue is often exacerbated when an individual has been previously infected with another dengue virus ( heterologous infection).
The ability to elicit a strong, long-lasting immune response against all four dengue virus serotypes is a key challenge for developing an effective vaccine.

A recent phase III clinical trial of a live attenuated dengue vaccine showed poor protection in some populations, suggesting that there is still much to be learned about how the human immune system responds to dengue virus ( DENV) infection.
Our results provide new insights into the complex nature of dengue immunity and may help to improve vaccine design.

The emergence of drug-resistant parasites in Southeast Asia makes malaria control more difficult, but there is no evidence that the disease is spreading beyond the region.

In this study, we compared three different strategies for treating malaria in Cambodia: using artemisinin-based combination therapy ( ACT) as treatment only, using ACT as both treatment and prophylaxis, or using ACT as treatment and another drug as prophylaxis.
We found that using three drugs instead of two could reduce the spread of drug resistance and slow down the decline of susceptibility to one of the drugs used in combination therapy.
We found that the use of these drugs reduced malaria transmission in the short term, but led to the emergence of drug-resistant parasites.
However, chemoprophylaxis may be more effective in reducing incidence but at the cost of increased risk of drug resistance.

The nuclear pore complex ( NPC) is a large protein structure that regulates the transport of macromolecules in and out of the nucleus.

The presence of microvascular obstruction in patients with heart attack is associated with poor prognosis, but it has not been possible to identify which patients are at risk.

The aim of this study was to find out if giving a small amount of clot-busting drug directly into the heart's blood vessels (intracoronary thrombolysis) immediately after opening up the blocked artery would improve recovery of the heart muscle and reduce the risk of further heart attacks.
The study was stopped early because it would have taken too long to recruit enough people to answer the question.

The Football Fans in Training ( FFIT) programme was developed to help men lose weight and improve their health through football.
The intervention was delivered in 12 weekly sessions, each lasting about an hour, to groups of men who were overweight or obese.

Reablement services are intensive and short-term (usually 2-6 weeks) interventions to help older adults who need more support with daily living tasks such as bathing, dressing, cooking and managing their finances.
We aimed to find out what reablement services are available in England, how they work and whether or not they help older people to become more independent.
We found that there were many different types of reablement service and they varied greatly in terms of their size, location and how they worked.
We found that there were many different types of reablement service, which varied in terms of who provided them, how they worked and what activities they offered.
The study found that these factors were important in shaping how services are delivered, but also that they did not always have an impact on the quality of care provided.
The staff who delivered the intervention believed that it was suitable for people with dementia and were positive about its potential benefits.
The study found that there is no evidence that one type of service is better than another, but that all services are effective in reducing reoffending.
The genomes of archaic hominins, including those of Neanderthals and Denisovans, are found in the genomes of modern humans living outside Africa.
The recent availability of ancient human genomes has greatly improved our understanding of human evolution and migration history.

In this paper, we describe a new method for estimating the amount of Neanderthal DNA in modern humans using only information from modern human genomes and summary statistics derived from large-scale genomic variation data.

Leukotriene receptor antagonists for chronic asthma Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether adding leukotriene receptor antagonists to inhaled steroids improves symptoms and lung function in people with asthma.

The review found that adding long-acting beta2 -agonists (LABA) to inhaled steroids may improve lung function and reduce symptoms in people with asthma but there is not enough evidence to be sure about this.

The review found that there is not enough evidence to show whether or not adding leukotriene receptor antagonists to inhaled corticosteroids are better than increasing the dose of inhaled corticosteroids alone.

The results showed that adding leukotriene receptor antagonists did not reduce the amount of steroid used by people with mild asthma, but they may help prevent worsening of symptoms and improve quality of life.

The results suggest that adding leukotriene receptor antagonists to inhaled corticosteroids may be as effective as doubling the dose of inhaled corticosteroids but more research is needed to confirm this.
The chromosome is a long, linear molecule consisting of DNA wrapped around histone proteins.
The recent development of high-throughput chromosome conformation capture ( Hi-C) has provided an unprecedented opportunity to study the three-dimensional organization of chromosomes.
We have developed a computational approach, called"HiCExplorer", which is able to identify chromatin domains ( CDs) in Hi-C data at different genomic scales and with high accuracy.

We find that the organization of chromatin is governed by two distinct mechanisms, one for the formation of large-scale compartments and another for the formation of smaller domains within these compartments.

DNA damage is a major threat to genome integrity, and one of the most common types of damage is an abasic site ( AP site).

The enzyme APEX1 is responsible for removing the AP sites by cutting the DNA at the 5' side of the lesion.
The mechanism by which APEX1 interacts with polymerase b is unknown.
In this work, we use computational methods to predict how these two proteins interact with each other in solution.
We find that the binding free energy of the complexes is very low, indicating that the interactions are weak.
We found that the binding site for pol-b is located on the 3'-side of APEX1, which is consistent with experimental data from previous studies.

Climate warming is likely to exacerbate the impacts of invasive species on native plant communities by altering their competitive interactions.
Here we test whether invasive species are more tolerant of warmer temperatures than native species in a major group of soil organisms, the Collembola ( springtails).

Our results demonstrate that invasive plants alter soil processes in ways that are likely to be exacerbated by climate warming, suggesting that biological invasions may amplify the negative effects of climate change on ecosystems.

Phytoplankton, such as diatoms, are responsible for most of the photosynthesis on Earth and play an important role in the global carbon cycle.
Diatoms have evolved strategies to survive in environments where iron is scarce by storing it within their cells.

This work provides evidence for an important role of ferritin in the ecology of marine phytoplankton communities and their response to global environmental changes.

Our results suggest that the regulation of WUS protein levels is tightly linked to its function in stem cell maintenance, which may be important for proper plant development.

The mitochondrial network is highly dynamic and undergoes rapid changes in response to environmental cues such as nutrient availability or stress.
The shape and size of mitochondria are important indicators of cellular health and have been used to monitor the effects of disease or drug treatment in many studies.
The ability to measure mitochondrial network dynamics is important for understanding how mitochondria function in health and disease.
We have developed a new method for measuring mitochondria size and shape in cells using high-resolution microscopy images.

We found that CPSF6 and LEDGF/p75 are required for HIV-1 integration, but their functions are not redundant.
Our results suggest that HIV-1 uses two different mechanisms for integration targeting: one mediated by capsid protein and another by the viral integrase enzyme.

Here, we show that rhesus macaques are willing to make costly decisions to benefit others and that they do so in a way that is consistent with a desire to maximize the well-being of their peers.

We found that the social preferences expressed by the monkeys in the laboratory were similar to their behavior in the home environment and that these preferences were influenced by the strength of their social bond with each other.

Our findings provide evidence that prosocial behavior is driven by both intrinsic motivations to affiliate with others and extrinsic rewards, which are mediated by brain regions involved in reward processing.

Key results When comparing omega-3 supplements to placebo, there was some evidence that people taking omega-3 had less need for antipsychotics and their mental state improved more than those who took placebo.

The recent advent of new technologies has led to a rapid increase in the amount of available data on brain structure and function, but it is unclear how these two types of information should be combined to better understand the brain.

We propose a novel approach to link these two types of data by using a mathematical model that describes how the brain's network structure and its internal dynamics interact to generate collective neural activity.
However, existing approaches have been limited by their reliance on linearization around an operating point, which fails to account for the nonlinearities inherent in neuronal responses.
We develop a novel theoretical framework to study how network connectivity affects neuronal activity in such networks.
We find that recurrent connections between neurons produce correlations in their activity, which are further amplified by nonlinearities.

Circadian clocks are endogenous biological oscillators that regulate daily rhythms in physiology and behavior.

We show that the circadian clock is not only required for the light response, but also that it plays an important role in modulating the amplitude of the light response.

We have used microarray analysis to compare gene expression patterns between flies with normal circadian rhythms and those lacking them, under conditions where they are exposed to light or kept in constant darkness.
We found that the circadian clock is not required for the light response and that light-induced gene expression occurs in both rhythmic and arrhythmic flies.
These results suggest that light exposure during the day is used by Drosophila to fine-tune its physiology in response to changing environmental conditions, which could be important for survival.

The mitochondrial network is constantly in flux, with mitochondria moving along actin filaments to deliver energy to areas of high demand.

We show that mitochondrial exchange is essential to maintain mitochondrial function and that it can be further enhanced by selective degradation of damaged mitochondria.

Biofilms are communities of bacteria that adhere to surfaces and produce an extracellular matrix of self-secreted polymers.

The molecular mechanisms controlling the release of milk from mammary glands are not well understood.
We show here that mice lacking the calcium ion channel protein Orai1 have reduced milk production and are unable to eject milk from their mammary glands.
This study demonstrates that calcium is an important regulator of milk ejection and lactation, which may have implications for the treatment of lactational disorders such as mastitis.
Urogenital schistosomiasis is caused by Schistosoma haematobium infection in humans and is endemic in many parts of Africa and the Middle East.
Schistosoma haematobium infection is associated with chronic inflammation, fibrosis, and bladder cancer.

The development of experimental models for human schistosomiasis is hampered by the fact that mice are not susceptible to infection with S. haematobium and other species of Schistosoma that cause urinary schistosomiasis in humans.
In this study we have used a mouse model to show that Schistosoma haematobium eggs injected directly into the bladder wall cause chronic inflammation and fibrosis ( scarring) of the bladder wall.
Infection with S. mansoni eggs in mice results in severe kidney damage, fibrosis, and bladder dysfunction, which are similar to those observed in humans.
This study provides new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying the development of bladder disease in mice infected with S. haematobium, which may be relevant to human schistosomiasis.

We found that species that are more likely to experience hurricane disturbance have larger toe pads.

The authors found that there is limited evidence to support a relationship between climate and trachoma prevalence or incidence.
The results suggest that the prevalence of trachoma is higher in areas with lower levels of rainfall and where there are more people living in close proximity to each other.

Plant water potential ( WP) is an important indicator of plant water status, but it has been difficult to measure in vivo with high spatial resolution.

Plant water status is an important parameter to monitor during drought stress experiments, but there are no simple, noninvasive, and low-cost methods available for monitoring this variable in real time.
We have developed a simple and minimally invasive method to measure plant water status using a hydrogel-based sensor called AquaDust.
AquaDust is a fluorescent dye that responds to changes in water potential, allowing researchers to measure water status in plants with high spatial resolution.
Nitrogen ( N) pollution is a major environmental problem affecting natural and seminatural ecosystems worldwide.
We report here on a long-term fertilization experiment in a tropical forest where nitrogen ( N) was added for over a decade.
Our results provide evidence for an important role of stomatal regulation in plant adaptation to nitrogen pollution, which may be relevant for other environmental stresses as well.
Our results suggest that long-term nitrogen deposition has altered the water cycle of these forests, which may have implications for their future response to climate change.

This review found that kangaroo mother care (KMC) is beneficial for low birth weight babies and should be used as an alternative to conventional care.

Key results Key results The review found that kangaroo mother care reduced the risk of death, sepsis, and hypothermia compared with conventional care at discharge or 40 to 41 weeks' postmenstrual age.
There was no difference in the number of children who had developmental delay at 18 months.

The results showed that there was no difference in the number of people who died, were admitted to hospital or had an adverse event between those receiving intravenous fluids and those receiving oral rehydration therapy.

We wanted to find out whether there is enough evidence to show that using regional anaesthesia reduces deaths, chest infections and heart attacks when used instead of general anaesthesia for major surgery.
Study characteristics The evidence is current to July 2012.
Study characteristics We searched the literature up to 20 February 2015 and found 11 studies with 1046 participants.
The studies included in this review were all randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and were published up to March 2015.
Key results Compared with general anaesthesia, regional anaesthesia was associated with lower risk of death at up to 30 days after surgery and fewer complications such as bleeding or infection.
The review found that regional anaesthesia reduced the risk of developing pneumonia and did not increase the risk of other complications such as bleeding or infection at the site of surgery.
The review found no evidence of differences in the number of people who had heart attacks or died during the study period.
Key results The review found that adding regional anaesthesia to general anaesthesia may reduce the risk of developing pneumonia by about one-third.

We found that in the absence of LEDGF/p75, HIV-1 replication is severely impaired and that this defect is due to inefficient nuclear import of viral cDNA.

We present a method for analyzing large-scale corpora of scientific articles that combines techniques from natural language processing and network science to identify topics and their authors, affiliations, and venues.
We find that citation patterns reflect both the persistence of researchers' contributions and the topical focus of those contributions.
Our methods enable researchers to identify and appreciate the full range of contributions made by scholars from around the world.
In this work, we have used computer simulation to study the process of methane hydrate formation and found that the growth mechanism is different from what was previously thought.
The results provide insights on how to control methane hydrate nucleation and growth in porous media.
The high mortality rate of glioblastoma ( GB) is largely attributed to its aggressive invasion, which is driven by the activation of the Hedgehog signaling pathway.
In this study, we found that the expression of NKCC1 is significantly increased in human gliomas compared with normal brain tissues.
Our results provide new insights into how NKCC1 regulates glioma cell migration and may help to identify potential therapeutic targets for this disease.
Our findings suggest that WNK kinase inhibition could represent a novel strategy to treat glioblastoma and potentially other aggressive cancers.
In this study we have used single-molecule experiments to investigate how reverse gyrase introduces positive supercoils into DNA.
However, the mechanism of this process is unknown because it occurs under extreme conditions and has not been amenable to conventional biochemical analysis.
The mechanism of this process is not well understood, but it has been proposed to involve a"reverse gyrase motor," which uses ATP hydrolysis to drive the rotation of DNA around its axis.
This mechanism is likely to be important for maintaining the stability of genomes in thermophilic organisms.
In this study, we show that R-loops are abundant in the genome of the bacterium Escherichia coli and that they play an important role in regulating gene expression.
The formation of an R-loop is influenced by the DNA sequence, but it has been difficult to predict which sequences are prone to form R-loops.
We have used computer simulations to study how R-loops form on supercoiled DNA and find that they are more likely to form at regions with high curvature.
The formation of an R-loop is energetically costly and thus occurs only when the DNA is under tension.
Here we show that activation of Nrf2, a transcription factor known to protect against oxidative stress and inflammation, reduces neuronal vulnerability in Parkinson's disease ( PD) models.
We found that Nrf2 is required for protection against PD-associated protein toxicity in neurons, and that this effect is dependent on the timing of Nrf2 activation.
We found that Nrf2, a transcription factor known to protect cells from oxidative stress, is also required for clearance of a-synuclein and prevention of neurodegeneration.
We found that Nrf2 is able to bind to misfolded LRRK2 and prevent its aggregation by sequestrating it in soluble proteinaceous structures called amyloid-like fibrils.

Here we show that the conformational change of the fusion protein is not only important for fusion but also plays an essential role in the regulation of viral entry.
We have determined the crystal structure of a human antibody ( 93k) bound to gB and used this information to identify key residues in gB that are important for binding to 93k.
We found that the binding site of mAb SG2 on gB is different from that of mAb 93k and does not overlap with the previously identified fusion peptide-binding site.

The infectious agent is composed of a misfolded form of the normal cellular prion protein ( PrPC), which is converted to its disease-associated isoform ( PrPSc) by a process called templated conversion.

The molecular basis of this phenomenon is not fully understood, but it has been proposed that stable variations in the structure of the infectious protein ( PrPSc) could be responsible for the different phenotypes observed.
We have used a method called analytical ultracentrifugation to separate different forms of PrPSc from each other based on their size.
We show here that the hydrodynamics of these nanodiscs is dominated by their size rather than their density.
This work provides evidence for the existence of small, highly stable, and highly infectious prion-like assemblies in the brain of infected animals.
The synchronization of mitral cell firing at high frequencies, such as gamma band ( 30-80 Hz), has been suggested to play an important role in odor coding and discrimination.
In this study we show that the synchronized activity of granule cells is not necessary for synchronizing the activity of mitral cells.
However, most studies of synchronization have assumed that the correlation between neurons is fixed.
We find that this is due to the fact that the synchronization between the mitral cells leads to synchronized activity in the granule cells that are connected to them.
We show that common input from granule cells, which is known to be important for learning, also plays an important role in synchronizing the activity of mitral cells.

The genome of mammals contains many repetitive elements ( REs) that are normally silenced by DNA methylation.
We have found that the histone methyltransferase MLL2 is responsible for the deposition of this mark in the egg and embryo.
Our study provides evidence that MLL2 plays an essential role in oocyte development, which may be related to its function as a histone methyltransferase.

This study provides evidence of a developmental switch in histone methylation patterns between two different classes of enzymes, which may be important for proper gene expression during embryogenesis.

We have developed a new computational approach, called"Network Diffusion-based Differential Co-Expression Analysis", which is able to identify genes with altered expression patterns between two conditions ( e. g., young vs. old) by analyzing their connectivity within the network.
We used this approach to identify genes with similar expression patterns in the brain of young adult ( 16 months) and aged ( 24 months) mice, which we refer to as"synonymous genes."
We find that the decline in gene expression is associated with changes in chromatin accessibility, which are likely to be caused by changes in histone modifications.
The bacterial mechanosensitive channel MscL is an important model for understanding how cells sense and respond to mechanical stress.
The molecular mechanisms underlying mechanosensation and mechanotransduction in MscL are not well understood, however.
We have determined the structure of a bacterial mechanosensitive channel protein, MscL, using a combination of X-ray crystallography and molecular dynamics simulations.

The results presented here provide new insights into how the large conformational changes in the protein are coupled to ion permeation and may help guide future efforts to develop drugs targeting this important class of membrane proteins.
In this study, we show that cell migration in the absence of external cues is not a simple random process but rather follows a correlated random walk.
Cells move using protrusive structures called pseudopodia, which are driven by actin polymerization.
We have used high-speed microscopy to study how cells move and found that they do so by forming protrusions called pseudopods.
In this study we show that the formation of pseudopodia is not random but follows an intrinsic pattern.
We have developed a mathematical model that describes how cells move by using the probability distribution of the angle between pseudopods as input.
We find that the key parameters for this process are the fraction of persistent pseudopodia, the degree of symmetry in their formation, and the variability of their directionality.

In this study we show that the bacterium Wolbachia pipientis influences the development of its insect host Drosophila melanogaster by altering the expression of genes involved in cell cycle regulation and apoptosis during early embryogenesis.
We found that the nematode Brugia malayi has a unique mechanism for establishing its microtubule cytoskeleton during embryogenesis.
We show that Wolbachia are transported along microtubules, which are part of the cell's cytoskeleton, by using the motor protein dynein.
We also show that Wolbachia is required for proper establishment of anterior-posterior polarity in the embryo.
This study provides evidence for an evolutionary link between the host and its symbiotic bacteria, which is likely to have been established during the evolution of the first multicellular organisms.

Study characteristics The evidence is current as of February 2017.
We found no evidence from these studies that giving Isoniazid prevented death in HIV-infected children who had not been exposed to TB.
The review found that there is insufficient evidence to be sure whether or not this treatment reduces the number of people who die from cancer.
The studies were conducted in South Africa and Botswana and involved children under the age of 13 years.
The review found no evidence to suggest that isoniazid prophylaxis reduces the risk of death in people with HIV infection.
The transition of mitochondria from bacterial endosymbionts to eukaryotic organelles required the development of specific protein import systems.
In this study, we have used a combination of biochemical and biophysical approaches to investigate how the translocon of the mitochondrial outer membrane ( TOM) recognizes its cargo.
Here, we show that TvTom40-2 is a functional translocon for hydrogenosome proteins and that it is essential for hydrogenosome biogenesis in T. vaginalis.
We also show that TOM is associated with Sam50, which is required for b-barrel protein insertion into the outer membrane of mitochondria.
The mammary gland is composed of multiple cell types, including luminal and myoepithelial cells, which arise from a common progenitor population.

In this study, we show that the number of these cells is increased in women at high genetic risk for developing breast cancer and that they are more likely to be found in the densest areas of the breast tissue.
We found that the expression of genes involved in cell proliferation was increased in the mammary gland of mice with high levels of circulating insulin-like growth factor 1 ( IGF-1).
We found that p27 is required for normal cell cycle progression and that it binds to specific target genes involved in cell proliferation and differentiation.
These findings suggest that p27 may play an important role in controlling the number of cells available to respond to hormones, which could influence breast cancer risk.
The ability to predict evolutionary outcomes has been a long-standing goal in biology, with important implications for conservation, medicine, agriculture, and biotechnology.
We have used a simple computational model to study how proteins evolve and found that it is impossible to predict the future evolutionary trajectory of a protein family from its current state.
We have developed a computational method for predicting how mutations will affect the fitness of proteins, which we use here to predict the evolution of a protein under selection pressure.
The accumulation of uncertainty about the effects of mutations on protein function is a major obstacle to predicting how proteins will change as they evolve.
Predicted values are commonly used as outcomes in subsequent analyses, but this practice is rarely justified by theoretical considerations or empirical evidence.
We present a method to correct such errors by combining predictions from multiple models trained on different subsets of data.

We demonstrate the utility of this method by applying it to two different types of data from very different domains: ( i) predicting disease phenotypes from gene expression profiles and ( ii) identifying causes of death from verbal autopsies.
The identification of disease-associated genes is an important step in understanding the molecular mechanisms underlying human diseases, and ultimately in developing new treatments.
The current methods for identifying disease-associated genetic variants are based on statistical models that do not take into account the biological context in which these variants occur.
However, many different approaches have been proposed to identify these networks, each with its own strengths and weaknesses.
We have compared six different network-based methods for identifying genetic variants associated with disease risk in a large cohort of families affected by breast cancer.
We found that this solution is enriched for genes involved in cell proliferation and apoptosis.
We have implemented these algorithms in R and Python and made them freely available to the research community.

Preoperative assessment for patients undergoing planned surgery Review question We wanted to find out whether or not preoperative assessment (POA), which is usually done on the same day as the operation, was better than POA before admission to hospital.
Study characteristics The evidence is current to March 2016.
The studies were small and of poor quality, so we are uncertain about whether or not this is true.
There was not enough information to assess whether or not there were any differences between groups in terms of how many people cancelled their surgery or had complications after surgery.
Bacteria are able to sense and respond to environmental stimuli, but how they do so is not well understood.

However, the metabolic potential of individual species is limited, and it is only when multiple species work together in microbial communities that they are able to perform tasks that no single species could accomplish alone.

The ability to predict how microbes will respond to multiple stressors is critical for optimizing their performance in bioreactors and other environments where they are used as tools or hosts for production of valuable compounds.
In this work, we have developed a novel computational framework for studying the metabolic interactions between different species in a community.
In this work, we present a method for identifying such emergent metabolic capabilities of microbial communities using genome-scale models.
Our work demonstrates the power of engineering microbial consortia for biotechnological applications.
The relationship between telomere shortening and aging has been well established, but whether or not telomere length ( TL) measurements are useful for predicting age-related diseases remains unclear.
The results of this study show that TL is a reliable measure, which has a clear normal range.
We found that telomeres were shorter in patients than controls, and that the degree of telomere shortening was related to the severity of the disease symptoms.

In addition to the well-known role of telomere length in cancer, we found that extremely short telomeres are associated with severe disease phenotypes such as bone marrow failure and immunodeficiency in children and young adults, whereas milder defects manifest as pulmonary fibrosis-emphysema in older individuals.
This study demonstrates that flowFISH is a useful tool to guide treatment decisions in patients with CLL.
We show that the RNA of this virus forms large liquid-like droplets when combined with its capsid proteins, which may be important for replication and movement within plant cells.
We found that the p26 protein is required for viral RNA accumulation, replication, and systemic spread of TMV in plants.
We found that G3BP, which is induced by viral infection, restricts virus replication in plants.
Exposure to fine particulate matter ( PM) has been linked to adverse health effects in children, including increased risk of respiratory disease and asthma exacerbations.
However, the mechanisms by which UFP exposure affects immune responses are not well understood.
We found that the two mouse strains differed in their response to both oxidative stress and allergens.

We found that prenatal exposure to ultrafine particles ( UFP) suppressed the development of airway inflammation in response to house dust mite allergens, which may increase susceptibility to respiratory infections and alter lung function later in life.
However, infants are able to clear carriage before they have developed specific antibodies against the capsular polysaccharide, indicating that there must be additional protective mechanisms.
In this study we show that mice vaccinated by nasal instillation of live pneumococci develop protective immunity to subsequent colonization by pneumococci.

We found that in mice with pre-existing immunity to pneumococcus, the bacteria were rapidly cleared from the upper respiratory tract within a few days after exposure, and that this was associated with an influx of white blood cells called neutrophils into the nasal passages.
We found that adoptively transferred CD4+ T cells from immunized mice were able to protect naive mice against infection with Lm, and that this protection required IFN-g production by these cells.
Our results suggest that IL-17A is an important mediator of pneumococcal immunity, which may be useful for vaccine design.

We show that our method outperforms other methods on several tasks such as regression, classification, and time series prediction.

Mesenchymal stem/stromal cells ( MSCs) are widely used for regenerative medicine due to their ability to differentiate into multiple cell types and secrete bioactive molecules that promote tissue repair.
We found that MSCs could induce monocyte/macrophage differentiation in vitro and promote their maturation to suppressive macrophages with anti-inflammatory properties.
The study provides evidence for a novel mechanism by which MSCs exert their immunosuppressive function in vivo and may have implications for clinical applications of MSC-based therapies.

Our results provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms of ZIKV pathogenesis and highlight the importance of E protein mutations in determining viral virulence and transmissibility.

Typhoid fever is caused by Salmonella Typhi bacteria, while paratyphoid fever is caused by S. Paratyphi A or B. In most countries where typhoid fever is endemic, there is no routine surveillance of typhoid fever cases.

The use of phase-change materials to create an electricity-free incubator has been proposed as one possible solution, but this technology is still in its infancy and there are concerns about its reliability and cost-effectiveness.
In this study, we evaluated the performance of a new test called Loop-Mediated isothermal amplification ( LAMP) to detect typhoid fever in children with suspected typhoid fever.

The results from this study suggest that the use of molecular diagnostics for typhoid fever may be feasible in resource-poor settings, but further studies are needed to determine whether these tests will be cost-effective.
Infection with Plasmodium falciparum causes severe disease when the host mounts an overactive inflammatory response to the parasite.
The mechanisms by which this occurs are not well understood.
In this study, we show that mice lacking regulatory T cells are able to control experimental malaria infection as well as wild-type mice.
We found that these cells are present in the blood of people with severe malaria and that they may be involved in dampening the immune response to infection.
The circadian clock is an endogenous time-keeping system that generates daily rhythms in many physiological and behavioral processes.
In this study we show that the circadian clock regulates glycogen synthesis by controlling the expression of glycogen synthase ( gsn), the enzyme responsible for glycogen synthesis.

Here we describe NGS-PrimerPlex, a software tool that is designed to generate primer pairs for any given set of genomic regions.
The method is based on the use of a set of universal primers for amplification of all targets in parallel, followed by sequencing of the amplicons using standard NGS protocols.
In this study we present a novel method for designing multiplex PCR assays using a combination of computational tools to ensure the specificity of the assay.

We have developed a method called Next Generation Sequencing PrimerPlex ( NGS-PrimerPlex) which is based on the use of short DNA sequences from the genome of bacteria as probes to identify specific strains within a sample.
The brain processes multiple sensory inputs in parallel, and it has been shown that this multi-sensory integration plays an important role for perception and action.
The nervous system of C. elegans is small enough to be mapped in its entirety and it has been shown that all sensory inputs are routed through a single ganglion, the commissural ganglion.
We found that, although some neurons responded to both stimuli, most did not respond to one or the other stimulus type.

We propose that this mechanism is a general principle for how neurons in the brain process multiple sources of sensory input, which could be used to explain higher-level cognitive functions such as attention or decision-making.

Amber preserves fossils of soft-bodied organisms in exquisite detail, but it is unclear whether this is due to the chemical properties of the resin or the taphonomic processes involved in its formation.

We found that the number and diversity of insects caught in the resin were significantly higher than those captured by standard insect traps placed nearby, suggesting that the resin is an effective trap for many species of insects.
We show that certain insect orders ( e. g., Lepidoptera) are underrepresented in amber, while others ( e. g., Coleoptera) are overrepresented.
Blood clots in the lungs (pulmonary embolism) are a serious complication of pregnancy and childbirth.

The aim of this review was to find out if there were any simple tests that could be used to identify which women with suspected endometriosis would benefit from further investigation using ultrasound or MRI scans.
We aimed to find out how well these tests work in identifying women who have a blood clot in their leg veins (DVT) or lung (PE).
This study found that using CT scans for all women who have a suspected blood clot in their leg is better value for money than using a test to decide which women should be scanned.

The review found that, for women who have been referred to hospital with a suspected blood clot in their leg veins ( deep vein thrombosis), D-dimer tests are not accurate enough to be used on their own as a test to rule out pulmonary embolism.
The review of studies found that there is no reliable test for diagnosing blood clots in pregnancy and more research is needed to find better tests.
We show here that in mice, the presence of regulatory CD8 T cells specific for the MHC molecule Qa-1 is associated with reduced immune rejection of transplanted hearts.

In this study we used a mouse model of autoimmune disease to show that the interaction between the MHC class Ib molecule Qa-1 and the TCR-CD8 complex is required for the generation of regulatory T cells.
Here, we demonstrate that in mice with reduced numbers of regulatory T cells, allo-antibodies are produced at higher levels and this is due to impaired regulation by CD8 Tregs.
This study provides insight into the mechanisms by which HLA-E regulates immune responses in vivo, suggesting new strategies for preventing transplant rejection.
We have used bioinformatic tools to identify genes in the genome of the filarial nematode, Wuchereria bancrofti, which is responsible for lymphatic filariasis.
We have developed a new method, called LIPS, which allows us to measure the levels of antibodies against each antigen using a simple and fast test.
We found that these tests were able to identify all individuals who had been exposed to Wuchereria bancrofti infection in their lifetime, but not those who had never been exposed.
This study has shown that this test is highly specific and sensitive for detecting Wuchereria bancrofti infection in individuals from endemic regions.
In bacteria, sister chromosomes are initially linked by cohesin-like proteins and then separated by topoisomerases.
In eukaryotic cells, the cohesin complex prevents premature separation of sister chromatids during DNA replication and is required for proper chromosome segregation.
Here we show that the distribution of SeqA protein on the E. coli chromosome is not uniform but rather varies in a manner that correlates with the location of specific DNA sequences.
We also show that the loss of cohesin results in poor chromosome segregation and reduced cell division rates.
The terrestrial environment is densely inhabited by microorganisms, which interact with each other through chemical signals such as natural products.
We show here that exposure to low concentrations of azalomycin F triggers the formation of a novel multicellular structure in Chlamydomonas reinhardtii called the gloeocapsoid.
This study provides evidence for an evolutionary link between multicellular development and the ability to resist bacterial infection, which is likely to be relevant in many other organisms.
The b2 adrenergic receptor ( b2AR) plays an important role in regulating airway tone and inflammation.
The b-adrenergic receptor ( bAR) is an important therapeutic target for treating respiratory diseases such as asthma.
A well-known example is the b-adrenergic receptor ( b2AR), which is involved in asthma pathophysiology and is targeted by drugs such as albuterol.
The structure of the N-terminal domain of the b-adrenergic receptor ( b2AR) has not been determined yet and its role in ligand binding and activation is unknown.
In this work, we used computer simulations to investigate how these different versions of the protein affect its function.
The results of this study provide new insights into the mechanisms underlying the variability in drug responses and may help to identify novel therapeutic targets for treatment of severe asthma.

We have used deep sequencing to study SARS-CoV-2 evolution during infection in 325 individuals, including both symptomatic and asymptomatic cases.
We also found that the amount of virus present in the sample is an important factor for identifying variants, with higher viral loads leading to more accurate results.
We find that within-host diversity of SARS-CoV-2 is very low, but varies between individuals.
We find that, in most cases, new mutations are not transmitted at all before they reach high frequencies within a population.
The drug praziquantel has been used to treat schistosomiasis since 1981, and it is now widely available in many countries where schistosomiasis is endemic.
The baseline prevalence of Opisthorchis viverrini was high in all villages, ranging from 84% to 100%.

The results of this study showed that both doses of praziquantel were effective in reducing the number of eggs per gram of feces at one week post-treatment but there was no significant difference between the two doses.

The results of this study suggest that the current standard for assessing the efficacy of O. viverrini treatments is not sufficient to accurately determine the true efficacy of these drugs, as it does not account for the large number of eggs that are missed by microscopy.
The efficacy of praziquantel at 75 mg/kg was higher than 40 mg/kg in curing Opisthorchis viverrini and Schistosoma mekongi infections.
The response of soil respiration to temperature and moisture is an important component of the terrestrial carbon balance, but its sensitivity to climate change remains poorly constrained.
Here we show that soil animals play an important role in mediating the response of soil microbes to climate change.
We found that elevated CO2 and temperature increased fungal growth but reduced decomposition rates of plant litter.

The quorum sensing system of Pseudomonas aeruginosa is composed of two homologous pairs of proteins ( LasI/R and RhlI/R) that synthesize and sense different autoinducers.

We have used a combination of genetic, biochemical, and molecular approaches to demonstrate that RhlR is a multifunctional regulator in Pseudomonas aeruginosa that controls virulence gene expression through two distinct mechanisms.
The quorum sensing system is composed of two proteins, RhlR and RhlI, which are involved in controlling gene expression.
This work provides evidence for a novel mechanism of quorum sensing regulation by a transcription factor and suggests that RhlR may respond to multiple signals.
We have identified an alternative ligand for RhlR, which is not produced by RhlI and does not require it to activate gene expression.
The photosynthetic apparatus of plants, algae, and cyanobacteria is composed of two photosystems, PS I and PS II, which are embedded in the thylakoid membranes of chloroplasts or thylakoids.
We have used state-of-the-art computational methods to study the excited states of the photosystem II reaction center in chlorophyll a/b-containing plants and cyanobacteria.

We show here that the interlayer spacing is not fixed but varies by up to 10% due to thermal fluctuations in ion concentration and hydration.
This work provides insight into how the interplay between water and ions controls the self-assembly of charged macromolecules, which may be useful for designing new materials.

The ratio of non-synonymous to synonymous substitutions is used as an indicator of natural selection in protein-coding genes.
We show that this method has been used to misinterpret the evolutionary history of many genes, leading to incorrect conclusions about the role of natural selection in shaping protein evolution.
The release of neuropeptides and neurotrophic factors is triggered by an increase in intracellular Ca2+ concentration.
We show that two proteins are required for this process and that they act redundantly to control the release of these chemicals.

The recent availability of genome sequences from many species has revealed that there are often large differences between the positions of the start codons for homologous genes in different species, even when they are very closely related.

We show here that imposing gene start consistency significantly improves the accuracy of gene start predictions, and that this improvement is due to the fact that it allows for more accurate modeling of the relationship between the location of the start codon and the surrounding sequence context.
We show that our method is able to correct many of these errors and inconsistencies in existing genome-scale maps.

The genetic basis of many human diseases is not well understood, and it has been suggested that single nucleotide polymorphisms ( SNPs), which are common variations in DNA sequences between individuals, may be associated with disease susceptibility.
The ability to detect rare variants in an individual's genome is important for identifying disease risk factors, but current methods are not sensitive enough to detect many rare variants.
However, existing methods for detecting single-nucleotide polymorphisms ( SNPs) based on electrical signals are limited by low sensitivity and specificity.
We have developed a graphene FET biosensor that uses a DNA strand displacement reaction to detect single nucleotide mismatches in target sequences.

The results presented here will aid in understanding the molecular mechanisms by which norovirus infection causes disease and may lead to new strategies for treatment or prevention of norovirus infections.
The West Nile virus ( WNV) is an important mosquito-borne pathogen that has spread rapidly throughout the world since its introduction to North America in 1999.
The virus is transmitted by mosquitoes to vertebrate hosts such as humans and birds.
However, the mechanisms underlying the high levels of genetic diversity in these species are not well understood.
We found that the mosquito immune system has a strong effect on the evolution of WNV and that this effect is strongest when the virus is most likely to be transmitted to humans.
This study provides evidence that mosquito vectors play an important role in shaping the evolution of WNV and may be responsible for the emergence of new strains.

The results of this review show that reducing sodium intake reduces blood pressure in people with high blood pressure and those with type 2 diabetes, but there is not enough evidence to be sure how much it will reduce cardiovascular events or death.
People with diabetes mellitus should reduce their sodium intake to below 5-6 grams per day as recommended for the general population.

We found that one or two doses of rMVA vaccination induced strong immune responses to SARS-CoV-2, including neutralizing antibodies and T cells, and protected mice from infection with SARS-CoV-2.

We show here that Rasa3 is essential for normal blood cell formation and maintenance of blood vessels.
We show that this mutation has a significant impact on the ability of the protein to activate RAS and RAP, two proteins involved in cell proliferation and survival.
We also identified several genes and pathways that may be involved in the pathogenesis of the disease and could potentially serve as therapeutic targets.

The HIV-1 virus depends on the host cell to provide it with the necessary building blocks and energy required for viral replication.
In this study we show that the activation of the transcription factor NFAT5 is required for efficient HIV-1 replication in primary CD4+ T cells.

The results show that, although the benefits of reduced emissions are substantial, they are outweighed by the costs of acid rain damage to forests and crops.
We find that the cost-effectiveness of these strategies varies by location, but that there are opportunities to reduce both air pollution and climate change impacts at low or no net cost.
Inflammasomes are multiprotein complexes that sense danger signals in the cell and trigger inflammatory responses.
The innate immune system is responsible for detecting pathogens and mounting an appropriate response to limit infection.
The influenza A virus ( IAV) is an important human pathogen, and its infection triggers activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome, which plays a key role in host defense against viral infections.
We found that EMCV 2B protein is sufficient to activate the NLRP3 inflammasome and induce IL-1b secretion in macrophages.
The parasite Toxoplasma gondii is a model for studying apicomplexan biology and has been used to study host-pathogen interactions, including those between T. gondii and its host cell.
We used a combination of genetic tools to study the functions of three calmodulins in Arabidopsis thaliana.
Our results provide new insights into how myosin H is regulated by calmodulin in trypanosomes.
Chromocrea spinulosa is an important model organism for studying fungal development, as it has both homothallic and heterothallic mating systems.
We found that the mating type locus contains two copies of each mating-type specific DNA segment, which are separated by a region of repeated sequences.
The recent availability of genomic data for many strains of these organisms has provided new insights into their evolutionary history.
We found that the same strains are present in both continents, suggesting that there is frequent exchange of pathogens between North America and Europe.
We also found evidence of recent adaptation to the tomato host in several genes, including those involved in virulence and motility.
We found that the ancestral allele of the gene is associated with higher levels of resistance to bacterial pathogens and also elicits a stronger plant immune response compared to the derived alleles.
We found that the ancestral allele of this gene is no longer present in modern strains, indicating that it was under strong selection pressure by the host immune system.
In this study, we identified the risk factors for mortality in patients with visceral leishmaniasis ( VL) in an urban setting in Brazil.
The study found that the prevalence of schistosomiasis in the community was high ( 43.
The main causes of death in this study were secondary infections and severe malaria.
TAR is an essential regulatory element of HIV-1, and its structure has been studied extensively.
We found that the mechanism of action of this compound is more complex than previously thought and involves both inhibition of viral gene expression and interference with virus particle production.
We found that L-50 is active against HIV-1 at two stages of its life cycle, namely during viral entry and during reverse transcription.
We show that TAR-RNA binding to the viral polymerase blocks reverse transcriptase activity and prevents the completion of reverse transcription.
We show that TAR is an important target for drug development, as it has two potential binding sites for small molecules and its inhibition would have a dual effect on viral replication.
Meiosis is a specialized cell division process in which genetic material is halved to produce gametes for sexual reproduction.
However, if the meiosis-inducing signal is removed before the cell has committed to meiosis, it will instead enter mitosis.
In this study, we show that the cell cycle machinery is not completely shut down during meiosis and that some of its components are required for proper chromosome segregation.
We show here that the Ndt80 protein is essential to drive this transition and that its expression is controlled by a positive feedback loop involving Ndt80 itself.
This study provides new insights into how the cell cycle is regulated during meiosis and how this regulation prevents the formation of abnormal gametes.
The fitness effects of mutations in one gene can be influenced by the genetic background, i. e., the genotype at other genes.
In this paper we use mathematical models to investigate how the number of low-fitness genotypes affects adaptation in a population of bacteria growing in a changing environment.
In this study we have identified and characterised a novel protein complex involved in carbon catabolite repression in Aspergillus nidulans.
In this study, we identified three novel regulators of CCR in the fungal pathogen Magnaporthe oryzae.
We have identified a mechanism by which yeast cells sense and respond to changes in their metabolic state through the activation of a protein kinase cascade.
These findings provide insight into how M. oryzae responds to environmental cues and may help us better understand how this fungus causes disease.
Chronic gastrointestinal ( GI) symptoms are common in patients with Chagas disease, but the pathogenesis is not well understood.
The aim of this review was to find out whether or not high-resolution manometry could be used as an early test for identifying people with achalasia.
We have developed a method to estimate the number of people infected with Strongyloides stercoralis using data from the prevalence of hookworm infection in children aged 5-14 years.
This study provides evidence for the need to consider the co-endemicity of S. stercoralis with other soil-transmitted helminths, such as hookworms, when planning control programs.
However, it has recently become apparent that alternative translation start sites exist for KSHV and other viruses, leading to the production of shorter proteins with unknown functions.
We identified the cGAS protein as a novel binding partner for LANA in KSHV-infected cells.
These findings suggest that LANA plays an important role in viral DNA replication, which is not limited to the latent state but also occurs during active virus production.
Marburg virus ( MARV) and Ebola virus ( EBOV) are members of the filovirus family and cause severe disease in humans and nonhuman primates.
In this study, we have identified a novel function for the C-terminal dsRNA binding domain ( RBD) of Ebola virus protein VP35 as an inhibitor of the host type I interferon response.
We have determined the crystal structure of the MARV VP35-RBD complex and show that it is similar to other known RNA binding proteins.
The crystal structure of the complex reveals how the protein binds to double-stranded RNA and how it uses its N-terminal domain to bind to the phosphate backbone of the RNA.
We show here that MARV VP35 binds to double-stranded RNA ( dsRNA) in vitro, and that this interaction is mediated by its N-terminal domain.
We have determined the structure of the MARV VP35 RBD bound to a short RNA duplex and found that it caps the end of the RNA duplex.
These results provide new insights into how MARV uses its viral protein to mask dsRNA from host detection.
The transition from one state to another is often considered as a random event, but it has been suggested that there may be an underlying mechanism that determines when this transition occurs.

In this study, we have developed a novel nanotechnology-based approach to treat H. pylori infection using liposomes encapsulating linolenic acid ( LipoLLA).
We found that the nanodiscs were prone to fuse with bacterial membranes and release their contents in a manner similar to liposomes.
We also demonstrated that LipoLLA is effective against H. pylori infection in mice, reducing bacterial loads in the stomach.
The parasite Trypanosoma brucei is an important human pathogen, causing sleeping sickness in humans and Nagana disease in cattle.

In this study we have used an imaging approach to screen a library of pyrimidine nucleoside analogs for their ability to be incorporated into the DNA of Trypanosoma brucei, a related parasite species.
The method is based on the incorporation of EdU ( 5-ethynyl-2'-deoxyuridine) into newly synthesised DNA during S-phase of the cell cycle, which is then detected using click chemistry.
This work provides new insights on the role of dNTPs in trypanosome metabolism and opens up new avenues for drug discovery against Chagas disease.
The detection of circulating tumor DNA ( ctDNA) is an attractive approach to monitor cancer progression and response to treatment.
We show that the mutated BRAF allele is enriched in smaller fragments compared to the wild-type allele.
We also show that size selection of circulating tumor DNA fragments can be used to increase the sensitivity of detection of low-frequency mutations such as EGFR T790M.
The formation of new tissues and organs requires coordinated cell-to-cell communication to ensure proper patterning and differentiation of cells.
The secondary growth of plants is driven by the activity of the vascular cambium, which produces new xylem and phloem cells in a continuous cycle of cell division and differentiation.
In plants, the vascular system is established by the coordinated action of two types of meristems, the procambium ( precursor to xylem) and phloem.
The ability to control cambial activity is important for improving wood quality, increasing biomass production, and reducing the amount of lignin produced during secondary cell wall formation.
In this study, we show that two closely related receptor-like kinases, RUL1 and MOL1, act in an antagonistic manner to regulate lateral growth by controlling cell division in the cambium.
We have identified two proteins involved in this process, which may be useful targets for improving wood yield.
The migration of neurons is an essential process in the development of the nervous system and has been studied extensively in the developing cerebral cortex.
We show that these different migration patterns are associated with distinct functional roles in the brain, and we identify specific genes that may be involved in regulating this process.
These findings suggest that the mechanisms of neuronal migration are conserved from worms to mammals and may be relevant for understanding human neurological disorders such as Parkinson's disease.
Schistosomes are parasitic flatworms with a complex life cycle involving two hosts.
We have developed an efficient and rapid qPCR-based method to determine the sex of cercariae, which is based on the amplification of specific sex-specific genes in the cercarial genome.
This method is simple and cost-effective for detecting both single- and mixed-sex infected snails.

In this study we have used the budding yeast, Saccharomyces cerevisiae, to test whether or not it is possible for cells to form multicellular structures by simply failing to separate completely after division.

We find that the production and consumption of secreted public goods is an important factor in the emergence of multicellularity, as it allows cells to coordinate their behavior and form large colonies.
In this study we show that yeast cells can also secrete proteins that help them stick together.
This work provides evidence that the evolution of multicellular organisms may have been driven by the need to protect public goods from exploitation by selfish cells.

We have used a novel experimental approach, termed qHRG, which combines high-throughput screening of large libraries of mutations with mathematical modeling to quantify the effects of each mutation on drug binding affinity.
We have used this information to develop a model that predicts how well viruses will evolve in response to different drugs.
Agricultural biodiversity is an important tool for mitigating the effects of climate change on crop yields.
However, little is known about how these diverse models affect predictions of crop yields and their uncertainty.

We show that accounting for genetic diversity in crop models improves the accuracy of climate change impact assessments and provides insights on the role of genetic diversity in adaptation to climate change.
We have previously shown that three aspartates located within the BK channel pore are critical for its regulation.
Here we show that mutations at these positions result in BK channels that are always open, regardless of membrane potential or calcium concentration.
We found that the deep-pore region of the BK channel is highly dynamic and that its structure is not fixed as previously thought.

We found that these viruses were not resistant to neutralizing antibodies, but instead were more susceptible than wild-type virus to neutralization by some of the same antibodies.
We have used this approach to study how the viral protein gp41 contributes to the process of viral entry and found that it is not required for infection of cells expressing high levels of CD4.

In this study, we have used a combination of biochemical and biophysical approaches to investigate how the structure of the viral envelope glycoprotein determines its susceptibility to inhibition by antibodies.

We show that quantitative differences in the intrinsic reactivity of different strains of HIV-1 are responsible for their differential sensitivity to inhibition by antibodies, which is consistent with previous observations that antibody-sensitive viruses require less CD4 on target cells than antibody-resistant viruses.
Programmed cell death protein ligand 1 ( PD-L1) is an important immunosuppressive molecule expressed on the surface of tumor cells and other cells within the tumor microenvironment.

In this study, we show that bladder cancer cells induce PD-L1 expression on bone marrow-derived myeloid cells and that these cells are recruited to the tumor microenvironment where they promote immune suppression.
We found that inhibition of prostaglandin E2 synthesis by either pharmacological or genetic means significantly decreased PD-L1 expression on tumor cells.
In this study we have used a combination of biochemical assays to show that the yeast DNA polymerase zeta ( Pol z) is able to bypass certain types of damage in an error-free manner.
Our results suggest that TFII-I is an important factor for TLS, which may act as a bridge between DNA polymerase z and proliferating cell nuclear antigen ( PCNA) to facilitate TLS.
These results suggest that TLS is an important regulator of gene expression, and may contribute to the phenotypic abnormalities seen in individuals with Williams-Beuren syndrome.
The brain is organized in a hierarchical structure, with higher-order areas receiving input from lower-order areas.
We find that cortical neurons are well-suited for this task and that they can be used as building blocks of a network that is able to perform multiplexing.
Our results suggest that cortical circuits are optimized for transmitting multiple signals in parallel rather than encoding information in the rate of action potentials.
We report on an ultrafast photoinduced lattice expansion in a molecular nanosheet made up of organic molecules.
The ability to control the thickness of these nanosheets is important for their use as building blocks for high-performance devices.

We have developed a method for tracing the developmental history of individual CD8^+ T cells in mice by labeling them with unique genetic markers at specific times during their development.
We found that the number of naive cells in the CD8^+ T cell pool is maintained at a constant level throughout life, while the number of memory cells increases with age.
We show that the survival of CD8^+ T cells is determined by their age and developmental origin.
The emergence of new viral diseases is often associated with changes in human behavior or environmental conditions.
The decline of WEEV is an example of how viruses can evolve to become less virulent or pathogenic over time.
We have previously shown that there are six amino acids in the protein sequence of the virus that were under positive selection pressure during this time period.
We found that changes in environmental conditions, such as temperature and precipitation, were more important than virus evolution in determining the decline of WEEV in the United States.
The recent availability of genome-wide genotyping data has enabled us to study the evolutionary history of human populations and identify genetic variants associated with diseases.
We show that the proportion of risk alleles is higher for rare variants than common ones and that this pattern holds across all studied traits.

The results suggest that genetic variants associated with diseases with strong environmental/lifestyle components are not subject to natural selection and their frequencies in the population have been determined by random processes such as mutation and migration.

Our study suggests that the use of evolutionary conservation as a criterion to predict disease-associated variants is limited, and that other approaches are needed to identify functional variants in complex traits such as obesity.
We have developed a method for identifying alternative gene models in eukaryotes, which is based on the comparison of genes from species with different numbers of chromosomes.

We show that genes on these extra chromosomes have lower levels of expression and are more variable than those on the main chromosome, suggesting that they may be expressed less often or under different conditions.
Cooperation is an important aspect of social behavior in many species, but it is difficult for individuals to cooperate if they are not able to find other cooperative individuals.
Many species of animals move around their environment, and some even migrate long distances between habitats.
However, in the absence of information about others' types, individuals are unable to choose their partners based on their type, which may prevent cooperation from evolving.
This is because the group-level selection favors the evolution of cooperation even when individual-level selection disfavors it.
We show that this mechanism is robust to various perturbations and can maintain cooperation even when individuals do not have any knowledge about their neighbors' strategies or payoffs.
We show how these interactions lead to the formation of clusters, which are important for cell-cell communication and tissue organization.
The chloroplasts are divided by a process that requires the action of several nuclear-encoded proteins, which are synthesized in the cytosol and then targeted to the chloroplast.
We also found that the chloroplast division checkpoint is conserved in the glaucophyte alga Cyanophora paradoxa, which has a single large chloroplast and no plastid division machinery.
We have identified the G3BP-binding motif of SFV nsP3 as F/GDF ( where F is phenylalanine or tyrosine).
We have identified a novel protein-protein interaction motif in G3BP1 and its binding partner USP10, which mediates their interaction.
The Hsp104 chaperone is an ATP-driven hexameric ring-shaped motor that can disassemble protein aggregates, including amyloid fibrils, by pulling them through its central pore.
The results provide insight into how Hsp104 uses energy from ATP hydrolysis to drive protein unfolding.

The immune system is composed of two main branches, innate and adaptive immunity.

In contrast, in susceptible mice, the infection is characterized by a strong inflammatory response which is associated with increased levels of TLR9 and inducible nitric oxide synthase ( iNOS) in liver cells.
In addition, we found that pretreatment with Pam3CSK4, a synthetic TLR2 agonist, reduced liver damage caused by S. mansoni eggs.

The use of proton-detected solid-state NMR spectroscopy for protein structure determination has been hampered by the low sensitivity of proton detection due to the short T2 relaxation time of protons in proteins.

We demonstrate here that, with fast magic-angle spinning ( MAS) NMR spectroscopy, it is possible to obtain high-resolution spectra for fully protonated proteins, which are suitable for determining protein structures.

We have developed an experimental system to study the dynamics of protein-protein interactions in living cells using a combination of high-speed microscopy, microfluidics, and machine learning algorithms.
The method is expected to be widely applicable to other protein complexes and will help us better understand their functions in health and disease.
The ability of bacteria to persist on or within their hosts is an important determinant of disease progression and transmission.
The ability to identify and quantify the number of colonization events is important for understanding the epidemiology of infectious diseases, but has not been addressed in previous studies.
The authors have developed a new method for estimating these parameters using genomic data from multiple strains within an individual host.
The model accounts for the variability in the number of infected cells at each measurement point, as well as the uncertainty in the parameters describing viral dynamics.
The canonical Wnt/b-catenin pathway has been shown to play an important role in regulating the balance between self-renewal and differentiation of breast epithelial progenitors.
We have previously shown that the noncanonical WNT pathway is essential for normal development of the mammary gland.
Here, we show that Wnt5a is an inhibitor of mammary gland development and that it acts by binding to its receptor Ror2.
We found that Wnt5a promotes mammary epithelial cell proliferation and survival, whereas Wnt5b has an opposite effect.
Here we show that the receptor tyrosine kinase RYK is required for Wnt5a-induced mammary epithelial cell proliferation and tumorigenesis in vivo.
Neurons use two types of electrical signals to communicate with each other: graded potentials and spikes.
In particular, we show that the presence of voltage-gated sodium ( Na+) channels in neurons is crucial for maintaining high information transmission rates.

We find that the process of generating an action potential introduces several sources of distortion to the input signal, including increased noise, non-linearity, and a"footprint" caused by the finite duration of the action potential itself.
The Hv1 channel is an important player in these processes, but its molecular mechanism of action has remained elusive.
Here we show that zinc ions bind to the open state of the human voltage-gated proton channel ( Hv1) in addition to binding to its closed states.
We show here that zinc binding to Hv1 is not cooperative between subunits but rather occurs independently at each subunit.
We show here that cell density regulates the activity of the Hippo signaling pathway, which is known to regulate organ size and tissue homeostasis.
We show that Hippo pathway inhibition potentiates the efficacy of gemcitabine in vitro and in vivo using both preclinical models.

We found that cancer cells with mutations in genes of the Hippo signaling pathway, which is involved in organ size control and tissue homeostasis, are more sensitive to gemcitabine treatment than wild-type cells.
Our results provide new insights into how YAP is regulated and may lead to novel strategies for treating drug-resistant cancers.

The enzyme hydrogenase is one of the most efficient catalysts known for the production and oxidation of H2, but its mechanism has been difficult to study because it contains multiple metal centers with different redox potentials.
The H-cluster of [FeFe]-hydrogenases is an organometallic cluster with a unique structure and reactivity.
The mechanism by which the iron-sulfur clusters are assembled in proteins is not well understood.
The enzyme is also shown to contain a [5Fe-5S] cluster and to be capable of catalyzing the reduction of N2O to NH3.
Phlebotomus argentipes, the main vector of visceral leishmaniasis in South Asia, has been shown to be highly susceptible to insecticides and resistant to pyrethroids.
The only way to prevent infection is by controlling the sand fly population.
This study provides important information on the reproductive biology of this species, which is essential for understanding how it transmits disease.
This study describes the complex courtship behaviour of P. argentipes in detail for the first time, providing insights into the mechanisms underlying mate choice and mating success.
The single X chromosome in males is hypertranscribed to compensate for the lack of an additional copy of the gene.
In addition to the sex chromosome dosage compensation mechanism, there is another mechanism that ensures equal expression of X-linked genes between males and females.
We find that in the absence of sex-determining genes, the default state of the embryo is male, with high levels of X-linked gene expression.
In this study, we show that female flies use the same mechanism of gene expression regulation as males to control the production of the sex-determining protein Doublesex ( Dsx).
Theoretical models have been used to predict how species expand their ranges based on simple assumptions about individual organisms' behavior.
