The shape of bacterial cells is determined by the interplay between forces generated by the cytoskeleton and turgor pressure.

We propose that the evolution of this novel structure was achieved by repurposing existing cellular machineries, which is an efficient way for cells to acquire new functions.
We present a new record of global tropical cyclone damage from 1900 to 2016, which is based on the equivalent area of total destruction.

We find that the historical record of climate-related disasters is more informative than previously thought and provides a better proxy for future losses than current methods based on present-day damages.
We find that the number of extreme events is increasing and that the severity of these events is also increasing.
The pyrenoid is a unique, proteinaceous organelle found in algae and some protists where photosynthesis takes place.
Here, we show that EPYC1 is essential for the formation of the pyrenoid matrix and that it directly interacts with Rubisco.

The structure of the protein is consistent with the known crystal structures of other members of this family, and we show how it could form a scaffold for the assembly of the large complex of proteins that make up the pyrenoid.
Our results provide insights into the mechanism of photosynthetic CO2 fixation and may be useful for improving crop productivity.
During meiosis, homologous chromosomes pair up and exchange genetic material through recombination events called crossovers.

We show here that when Kluyveromyces lactis Zip1 is used instead of yeast Zip1, it does not support normal chromosome pairing and recombination, but still allows for high levels of crossovers in meiosis I.

We show here that, although the meiotic recombination protein Zip1 is not required for crossover formation, it does play an essential role in promoting crossover maturation and chromosome segregation.
The Polycomb Repressive Complex 2 ( PRC2) is an epigenetic regulator of gene expression and has been implicated in cancer development.
We show here that Polycomb group ( PcG) proteins are present on mitotic chromosomes of Drosophila cells.
We find that many of these regions are located at the boundaries between different types of chromatin domains, and we show that they are enriched for specific histone modifications.

Our results suggest that the loss of long-range interactions during mitosis is important for the release of Polycomb proteins from their target genes, but that some Polycomb proteins remain bound to certain sites even when they are not associated with long-range interactions.

The environmental pollutant benzo [a] pyrene ( BaP) is one of the most carcinogenic substances known to date and causes more than 20% of all human cancers.
The authors describe a new method to capture DNA lesions in single-stranded DNA ( ssDNA).

We also show that the DNA damage induced by benzo[a]pyrene diol epoxide ( BPDE) is repaired with high fidelity and that the sequence context surrounding the lesion plays an important role in determining the efficiency of repair.

In this study, we have investigated the role of Vpu in the pathogenesis of HCV infection using recombinant viruses expressing different Vpu proteins derived from seven HCV isolates collected in Cameroon, the country where the virus was first identified.
We have identified mutations in the transmembrane domain of Nipah virus that are required for its ability to interact with human tetherin.
We found that these mutations disrupted the ability of the protein to bind to other proteins, which is important for its function.
We found that the Vpu protein from this new group N strain is able to block tetherin, unlike other non-pandemic strains.
We have developed an immunotherapy platform based on spherical nucleic acid ( SNA) nanoparticles that targets tumor-associated macrophages in triple-negative breast cancer ( TNBC).
This study provides a proof-of-concept for using SNA-based nanomedicine to target and treat cancer cells in an antigen-independent manner.
The NHS Health Check programme aims to reduce CVD by identifying people at increased risk and providing them with advice on how to lower their risk.

The results showed that people who responded to the invitation were similar in age, sex and ethnicity to those who did not respond but they had slightly better health than those who received an opportunistic check-up.
The study found that people who received an invitation to take part in the survey were more likely to complete it than those who did not receive an invitation.
We found that the questionnaire increased the number of people who took part in screening by about 2%.
The study found that, on average, 2% more people completed the questionnaire when offered an incentive compared to those who were not offered an incentive.

People who received an opportunistic check-up were more likely to have high blood pressure and other cardiovascular disease risk factors, which may explain why they had a greater chance of developing heart disease.

In particular, we show that the high GC content of the genome is reflected by an excess of A over T on the leading strand and a corresponding deficit of A on the lagging strand.
We find that recent mutations in non-coding regions are enriched for transitions from C to T and G to A, but not from A to T or T to A, which contradicts the predictions of the classical model.
The authors show that this bias is not due to mutation or selection on the genome as a whole but rather reflects an intrinsic property of the DNA molecule itself.
Estrogen receptor beta ( ERb) is an important driver of triple-negative breast cancer ( TNBC).
We identified several genes that are highly up-regulated in metastatic tumors, including those involved in cell proliferation, migration, invasion, angiogenesis, and immune response.
We found that the expression of several cytochrome P450 enzymes was altered by vitamin D treatment, including those involved in the metabolism of fatty acids and steroids.
Estrogen receptor b ( ERb) is expressed in approximately one-third of triple-negative breast cancers, but its role in this disease has not been fully elucidated.
Our results suggest that ERb may not act as a tumor suppressor in TNBC, which has implications for the design of new therapies targeting this receptor.
The potato spindle tuber viroid ( PSTVd) causes severe disease symptoms on potatoes including stunted growth, distorted leaves and deformed tubers.
The replication of PSTV is accompanied by the production of small RNA molecules, which are thought to be involved in disease symptoms.
In this study, we found that PSTVd small interfering RNAs ( siRNAs) target the host gene StTCP23, which is involved in plant growth and hormone response.
We found that the expression of StTCP23 is negatively regulated by PSTVd infection.
We found that the StTCP23 mRNA is targeted by virus-derived small RNAs, which are produced from viral genomic RNA and act as siRNA-like molecules to target host mRNAs for degradation.

We found that expression of these small RNAs in transgenic plants results in similar symptoms to those observed in virus infected plants, suggesting they play an important role in disease development.
We found that StTCP23 is required for normal GA accumulation during tuber formation and that its loss results in reduced GA levels and altered expression of GA-related genes.
Our study provides evidence for the first time that virus-derived siRNA-mediated gene silencing is involved in plant growth regulation by altering gibberellin biosynthesis.
Lake Ohrid is one of the oldest lakes on Earth and has been continuously accumulating sediments since its formation ~2 million years ago.
We show that the first major loss of tropical forest biodiversity occurred during the Eocene-Oligocene transition ( 34 million years ago), when forests were still warm and humid.
In this study, we used a recently developed method to measure the amount of viral and host RNAs within individual cells infected with dengue virus ( DENV).
We have developed an experimental system to study the interaction between virus and host at the single-cell level.
We found that host gene expression is dramatically reduced in infected cells but that some genes are up-regulated in"superproducer" cells with very high vRNA levels.
We also found that some of these pathways are commonly affected in multiple viral infections, while others are specific to each virus.
This work provides an approach to prepare dendrimer-based nanomaterials with controlled topology, high rigidity, and diverse surface functionalities.

We demonstrate that the vibrational modes of these plasmonic molecules are coupled to the substrate, which enables the observation of molecular-scale phonon-mediated energy transfer between the plasmonics and the substrate.
This work provides a route for the optical manipulation of nanoscale mechanical motion and opens up new possibilities for optomechanics at the nanoscale.
We found that estrogen receptor b ( ERb) is required for maintaining the integrity of the vasopressin-producing neurons in the hypothalamus.
In this study, we show that estrogen receptor b ( ERb) is required for the maintenance of muscle mass in aged mice.
These findings suggest that selective estrogen receptor b ( ERb) agonists could be useful for treating or preventing prostate diseases such as BPH, prostatitis, and prostate cancer.

The environmental legacy of human activities has been studied in many regions around the world; however, there are few studies that examine the long-term effects of prehistoric human activity on the landscape.
We show that the effects of past human activities persist in the landscape, and that these legacies are still important for shaping contemporary plant communities.
We find that species richness is positively related to growing degree days, which may reflect the importance of warm temperatures for plant growth and seed production.
We show that ESR is positively related to past investment in agriculture and animal husbandry, suggesting an evolutionary legacy of past human activities on ecosystems.
The siderophore biosynthetic pathway of A. fumigatus is essential for its virulence and absent from humans, making it an attractive target for drug development.
We have identified a novel siderophore, hydroxyferricrocin, which is produced by Aspergillus fumigatus during conidial germination and used to sequester iron in the developing germ tube.

Dengue is an infectious disease caused by four different serotypes of virus transmitted to humans through mosquito bites.
The bacteria Wolbachia are being used to control mosquitoes and other insects by limiting the ability of viruses such as dengue to replicate within them.

In this study, we show that Wolbachia infection does not prevent dengue virus replication in mosquitoes but rather reduces the amount of virus present in mosquito saliva after an infected blood meal.

We found that Wolbachia infection significantly reduced the amount of virus present in the mosquito's saliva and also delayed the time it took for the virus to be transmitted from an infected mosquito to its offspring.
We found that Wolbachia-infected mosquitoes produce less saliva than uninfected mosquitoes, which may explain why they are less able to transmit dengue virus.

We show that the spatial information encoded in the grid cell population is not fully transferred to the place cells, and that this is due to the sparseness of the place cell representation.

We found that worms with mutations in all four SOD genes are more sensitive to oxidative stress than those with single and double mutant genotypes.

Anaerobic hydrocarbon degradation is an important biogeochemical process, but it remains poorly understood due to its low abundance and difficulty in detection.
We used stable isotopes to track the fate of methane in the environment, which allowed us to identify the source of the methane and its degradation products.
The carbon isotopic composition of propane has been used as an indicator of its origin, but this approach has not yet been applied to natural samples.

The results presented here provide evidence for the presence of anaerobic methane-oxidizing archaea in deep subsurface environments, which has implications for our understanding of the global carbon cycle and the potential for bioremediation of contaminated aquifers.
During meiosis, homologous chromosomes pair and undergo recombination to ensure proper chromosome segregation.
In this study, we have used mouse models to investigate the function of the replication protein A ( RPA) complex during meiosis I and II.
The protein complex RPA ( Replication Protein A) is essential for DNA replication in all organisms.

We show here that RPA is essential for the formation of recombination intermediates on meiotic chromosomes by promoting the recruitment of the recombinase proteins DMC1 and RAD51 to sites of programmed DNA damage.
We show here that the MRN complex ( MCM8-9-NBS1) is recruited to DSBs independently of RPA but requires RPA for subsequent recruitment of the recombinase proteins RAD51 and DMC1.
We show that RPA is required for efficient DSB repair, but also has a second function during late stages of recombination to promote crossover formation.

We find that the capacitance is not constant but varies with applied voltage in a way consistent with previous experimental observations.
Beta blockers reduce blood pressure by reducing heart rate, which reduces the amount of blood pumped through the body's blood vessels.
The review found no evidence to support the use of these drugs in this way, but there is not enough information to be sure about their effects.
The review found that carvedilol was better than placebo at reducing blood pressure in people with high blood pressure and heart failure.

The review found that these drugs are not better than other types of drugs for reducing high blood pressure.
Protein post-translational modifications ( PTMs) are known to regulate protein function by altering their structure and interactions with other proteins.

Our results show that acetyl groups are generally stabilizing to protein-protein interactions, whereas phospho groups destabilize them.

The results presented here provide new insights into how post-translational modifications affect protein-protein interactions and add to our understanding of the role of post-translational modification cross-talk in regulating protein function.
The folding of large proteins is often considered to occur in a sequential manner, where each domain folds as it emerges from the ribosome during translation.

This is because the directionality of translation prevents cotranslational folding of the C-terminal domain, which must fold after completion of translation.
We find that the stability of each domain in EF-G is not constant and varies with the presence or absence of other domains.

The results of this study provide new insights into the role of terrestrial ecosystems as carbon sinks, which is important for understanding the global carbon cycle.

This study provides evidence of large-scale terrestrial mercury sequestration by forests, which is important for understanding the global mercury cycle and mitigating its impacts on human health and ecosystems.

Body-worn cameras for improving police accountability and reducing use of force Body-worn cameras are increasingly used by law enforcement agencies in an effort to increase transparency and accountability.
We conducted a randomized controlled trial to test whether or not wearing body-worn cameras would reduce police use of force and improve citizen complaints against police.
We find no evidence that body-worn cameras reduce police use of force or citizen complaints against officers.
The ERBB3 receptor tyrosine kinase is an attractive drug target for cancer therapy, but its role in normal physiology remains poorly understood.
We found that mice lacking ErbB3 had an increased number of adenomas and carcinomas when compared to wild-type mice.
We found that the loss of ErbB3 resulted in an increase in the number of polyps, which is due to increased cell proliferation.
We found that loss of Erbb3 in the intestine causes an increase in the number of intestinal polyps and that this is due to an increase in cell proliferation.
Our results suggest that targeting ERBB3 may be an effective strategy to treat colorectal cancer.
The identification of sand flies is important for disease control and prevention, but it has been difficult due to their small size and morphological similarity between species.
In this study, we evaluated the performance of MALDI-TOF mass spectrometry ( MS) in identifying species of sand flies collected from different locations in Brazil.
The sand fly species collected from the four sites of capture were identified as Phlebotomus papatasi, P. longicollis and Sergentomyia minuta.
The method was also used to identify ticks collected from humans and animals in the field, but the efficiency of this approach was lower than when using DNA extracted from ticks preserved in ethanol.
This method is simple, fast and cost-effective to identify species of sand flies collected from the field.

The use of immunosuppressive drugs to prevent rejection in heart transplant recipients Heart transplantation is an effective treatment for patients with advanced heart disease, but it carries risks due to the need for long-term immunosuppression.
The immune system of the recipient may reject the new heart, which would require further treatment with drugs to suppress the immune response.
The aim of this review was to find out which drugs are the most effective in preventing rejection after heart transplantation.

The aim of this review was to find out whether using drugs that suppress the immune system (immunosuppressants) in addition to antilymphocyte globulin (ALG) or anti-thymocyte globulin (ATG) reduces rejection and improves survival after heart transplant.
This review found that there is not enough evidence to show whether one type of antibody treatment is better than another in terms of preventing rejection after heart transplantation.

We found no evidence of benefit from using these drugs in terms of survival or rejection episodes, but there was some evidence that they may reduce the risk of kidney failure and improve quality of life.

Key results This review found that IL-2 receptor antagonists may reduce the risk of acute rejection when compared to no treatment or placebo, but there is uncertainty about this finding due to small numbers of participants in some studies.
More research is needed to determine whether or not this treatment has any benefit in terms of reducing rejection episodes and improving survival after transplantation.

The Asian tiger mosquito ( Aedes albopictus) is an invasive species that has been spreading rapidly around the world since the 1980s.

The results provide an important contribution to our understanding of the evolutionary history of this species, which is likely to be useful in tracking other epidemiologically relevant traits such as insecticide resistance.

Alzheimer's disease ( AD) is a neurodegenerative disorder caused by the accumulation of amyloid-beta ( Ab) peptides in the brain, which form toxic oligomeric species and insoluble fibrils.

The classical explanation for this phenomenon is based on the idea that the interfacial energy barrier to crystallization is lower than in bulk solution, but recent experiments have shown that the situation is more complex and depends on the specific conditions.
In this study we investigated an outbreak of Chagas disease in the state of Rio Grande do Norte, Brazil.
In this context, we evaluated the role of Triatomine bugs as vectors of T. cruzi in the Serido region, which is located in the semi-arid zone of Rio Grande do Norte state, Northeastern Brazil.

In this study, we found that the majority of the sand flies collected from peridomestic environments were infected with Leishmania parasites and that the prevalence of infection was higher in peridomestic than in sylvatic sites.
The results showed that the two main genotypes ( TcI and TcII) are present in all sites, but with different proportions.
The presence of triatomines in these areas is an important indicator of the risk of Chagas disease transmission to humans.
The Efficacy and Mechanism Evaluation ( EME) programme is a research funding body that supports research on how well treatments work, and why they work.
The review found that only about one in seven studies reported an improvement in quality of life for people with dementia and their carers after receiving the intervention.
The projects we looked at were all funded by the EME programme and addressed a wide range of health issues.
This study aimed to identify why some research ideas are selected for funding while others are not.
We found that research ideas generated by patients were more likely to be unfunded than those generated by researchers, but we do not know why.
The study found that the EME programme has had an overall positive impact on the health of people with epilepsy in low- and middle-income countries.
Pressure ulcers (also known as bedsores or pressure sores) occur when there is prolonged pressure on the skin and underlying tissues.
Pressure-relieving devices (such as foam mattresses or pillows) are often recommended for people with pressure ulcers.

The review found that, compared with standard care, using an alternating pressure mattress or overlay may reduce the number of people who develop pressure ulcers and improve quality of life for those who do not develop them.
The use of pressure-relieving overlays on the operating table may help to prevent pressure ulcers but they may cause some skin problems.
We show that destabilized mutant p53 proteins form aggregates with wild-type p53, p63, and p73 in cells.
We show that the aggregation mechanism of p53 is different from that of other proteins, which may explain why it has not been observed in vitro.
The model also predicts that the accumulation of mutant p53 protein in cancer cells will lead to the formation of large aggregates containing both mutant and wild-type p53.
We show that the mechanism of action is not based on seeding but rather on trapping by cross-reaction and coaggregation, which may be relevant for other amyloid diseases.
The strength of connections between neurons is modified by experience, and this process is known as synaptic plasticity.
We show that the same mechanism is also responsible for the emergence of global patterns in a recurrent neural network model.

In this paper, we show how spike timing dependent plasticity ( STDP) -a learning rule for synaptic connections between neurons-acts as an effective mechanism to generate these patterns in networks of excitatory and inhibitory neurons.
In this paper, we study the effect of spike-timing-dependent plasticity ( STDP) on the connectivity between neurons.
The authors show that the same mechanisms that are known to be at work in single synapses also act on larger scales and shape the structure of neuronal networks.

Theories for the evolution of sexual reproduction and recombination have been developed under the assumption that the fitness landscape is smooth, i. e., that small changes in genotype lead to small changes in fitness.

We used computer simulations to investigate how sexual reproduction affects adaptation in populations evolving under different conditions, including those where interactions between genes are important ( i. e., epistasis).
In contrast, we find that in complex fitness landscapes, none of these methods are able to predict the evolutionary outcome with high accuracy.
We find that the simplest method is the most robust but least accurate, whereas the more complex methods are less robust but more accurate.
This work provides an example of how the complexity of biological systems can make it difficult to establish clear predictions from theory, even when the system is well understood in principle.
The CRISPR-Cas system is an adaptive immune system found in bacteria and archaea, which provides protection against invading foreign nucleic acids.
We propose that the primed adaptation complex ( PAC) is formed upon recognition of the primed target site by crRNA-Cascade, which recruits Cas3 to mediate integration of the new spacer sequence.

Here we show that the CRISPR-Cas system is physically associated with the type I-F ICE in Bacillus thuringiensis, which is consistent with the model that the CRISPR-Cas system acts as an adaptive immune defense against ICEs.
Neurogenesis is the process by which neurons are generated from neural stem cells.

We have found that the transcription factor Prox1, which is known to be important for the formation of the dentate gyrus during embryogenesis, also plays an essential role in the regulation of adult hippocampal neurogenesis.
The emergence of new rabies viruses in bats has been reported from several parts of the world, and it is likely that many more remain undiscovered.
The authors found that the virus was transmitted by direct contact with infected bats, but also through contaminated soil and water.
The clinical signs of disease in dogs are similar to those seen in humans, including fever, cough, and respiratory distress.
We found that the pathogenicity of viruses varied significantly among different virus species and even within species.
We found that IRKV infection is associated with an increased number of infected astrocytes and a higher viral load in the brains of mice compared to other rabies viruses.
Our study provides new insights into the mechanisms by which rabies virus causes disease and may help to develop novel therapeutic strategies for treatment of rabies.
The parasite expresses an inhibitor of cysteine protease, which is essential for its survival and development in the mosquito vector.
We have used genetic approaches to generate a parasite lacking ICP and found that it is essential for survival during the blood stage.
In addition, we show that PfIcPb plays an important role in the development of parasites within both liver cells and red blood cells.
Theories of speech production and comprehension suggest that there are two main routes to speech perception, one for phonology ( sound-based) and another for semantics ( meaning-based).
The neural mechanisms underlying this ability are not well understood.
Our results suggest that there are at least two distinct pathways in the brain that support speech perception, which may be differentially affected by hearing loss or neurological disorders.
The ability of C. albicans to grow at low pH is important for its pathogenicity, but how it achieves this is not well understood.

We found that the fungal cell wall polysaccharide b-glucan is masked under neutral pH conditions but becomes exposed at low pH, which enhances its ability to stimulate an inflammatory response from host cells.
This study provides new insights on how C. albicans senses its environment and responds to it by altering its surface properties.
Pain is an important factor in the development of these complications, but it has not been studied systematically.

We found that the expression of c-Fos, an immediate-early gene involved in learning and memory, is reduced in the hippocampus of mice lacking the CCR2 receptor.
Salmonella is an important human pathogen causing food poisoning, diarrhea, typhoid fever, and life-threatening systemic infections.
Infectious diseases remain the leading causes of death in developing countries, particularly among young children and people with weakened immune systems.
However, there is no licensed vaccine available to prevent nontyphoidal salmonellosis in humans and the emergence of antibiotic resistant strains has made it difficult to treat infections.
The type III secretion system ( T3SS) is an essential virulence determinant for many Gram-negative pathogens.
In this study we have evaluated the ability of two proteins to induce protection against S. Typhimurium infection when delivered via different routes ( subcutaneously, orally and intranasally).
We found that mice vaccinated with SipD alone or in combination with PrgI were protected from oral infection with S. Typhimurium.
This study provides evidence that SipD-based vaccines may have potential as a new tool to prevent infant mortality due to typhoid fever in developing countries.
The number of asthma attacks increases when children go back to school at the start of term and decreases during holidays.
We find that the peak of the seasonal variation is in September for the Northern Hemisphere, and in February for the Southern Hemisphere.
This review found no evidence from randomised controlled trials that any treatments or strategies used to prevent asthma attacks before the start of the new school year were effective.

We found no evidence to suggest that either treatment reduced the number of children who had an asthma attack severe enough to require admission to hospital or emergency department attendance, or required oral steroids.
The studies were small and varied in their methods, so it is difficult to draw firm conclusions from them.
The review found no evidence from randomised controlled trials to support or refute the use of oral immunotherapy in children with severe asthma and allergy.

The review of trials found that giving injections in the muscle was not more effective than injecting into the fat layer under the skin and did not cause more side effects except for pain at the injection site.
The review found that there was not enough evidence to show whether these treatments are effective in reducing symptoms and improving quality of life for people with asthma.
The review found that there is not enough evidence to show whether or not these treatments are effective in reducing the number of flare-ups.
Buruli ulcer ( BU) is a neglected tropical disease caused by Mycobacterium ulcerans, which causes severe skin lesions in humans.
The mode of transmission is not well understood and has been debated in the literature.
The results showed that Mycobacterium ulcerans was present in the salivary glands and saliva of the vector, suggesting that it is transmitted to humans through mosquito bites.
The endosymbiotic origin of mitochondria and chloroplasts is well established, but the evolutionary history of other eukaryotic organelles remains unclear.
We show that the N-terminal domain of LAH-1 is responsible for binding to the worm body wall ( WB) specific protein WSC, while the C-terminal domain is important for cortical localization.
The mechanism by which the actin cytoskeleton is anchored to the plasma membrane in fungi has been studied extensively in yeast but not in filamentous fungi.
We have used a novel technique to create a hybrid protein containing both domains and found that it is functional in yeast.

We use single-molecule FRET microscopy to study the molecular mechanism of this process and find that the motor undergoes a large-scale conformational change during the transition from forward to reverse direction.

We found that dynein motors switch direction by detaching from one train and attaching to another, which is consistent with previous biochemical studies but contradicts the prevailing model in which dynein moves independently of IFT trains.
The high-speed imaging revealed that myosin V undergoes rapid detachment and diffusion between binding sites on actin filaments, which is essential for its processive stepping behavior.
Sleep is essential for maintaining health and performance in all animals, including humans.
We find that flies lacking BMM show an increased homeostatic response to sleep loss and reduced sleep pressure.
We find that flies lacking LSD2 have reduced lipid stores and are unable to increase them in response to sleep deprivation.
Our study provides the first genetic evidence for a link between lipid metabolism and sleep regulation.
In this study, we show that mirtrons, which are miRNAs generated by intronic splicing, require the splicing machinery to be processed from precursor RNAs.
We have used computational methods to predict which introns in the genomes of mice and humans are likely to produce these small RNAs.
We show that mirtrons are more likely to be found in genes with many introns, and we identify specific characteristics of mirtron-containing genes that distinguish them from other genes.
We show that mirtrons are not only different in their biogenesis pathway but also have distinct characteristics compared to canonical microRNAs.
We find that mirtrons are often uridylated at their 3' ends, have heterogeneous 5' ends due to exonucleolytic processing, and sometimes lack a 5' cap.
The enzyme isocitrate dehydrogenase 1 ( IDH1) has been known to be involved in cellular metabolism for decades but only recently has it been found to have an important role in cancer biology.
In addition, we found that IDH1 deficiency resulted in decreased levels of a-ketoglutarate ( KG) and glutamate within cells, which are required for the synthesis of aspartate from oxaloacetate.
We found that IDH1 deficiency resulted in increased glucose production and decreased fatty acid oxidation in the liver.
We show here that IDH1 plays an important role in AA metabolism in vivo and that its deficiency impairs gluconeogenesis.
In this study, we have identified a protein called CAMSAP3 as a key regulator of microtubule orientation in polarized epithelial cells.
We found that CAMSAP3 localizes to the apical side of the cell membrane in a manner dependent on its coiled-coil domain.
We show here that CAMSAP3 is required for the proper alignment of microtubules and organelles at the apical side of polarized epithelial cells.
We found that the organization of ventral temporal cortex ( VTC) is determined by the brain's internal state rather than by visual experience.
We found that the brain regions involved in object vision are active when blind participants imagine objects, and that these brain areas respond to specific categories of objects.
We found that visual cortex of blind participants showed stronger selectivity to sound stimuli than did sighted participants.
We found that the organization of this region is similar to that of sighted participants, suggesting that it may be organized by other sensory inputs or cognitive processes.
In this study, we show that the long-distance migration of granule cells is not restricted to mammals and occurs also in birds.
We have used time-lapse microscopy to study how these neurons move during development and find that they form chains of cells as they migrate.
We show that Cadherin-2 is required for proper cell polarity and migration, as well as for the establishment of the correct position of the centrosome.

We have used high-speed microscopy to visualize the movement and interactions of these cells in real time, and found that they use their cadherins to adhere to each other as well as to the surrounding tissue.
We show that cadherins are present at the front of migrating cells and that they are involved in the control of cell polarity.
We show that Cadherin-2 is essential for maintaining cell-cell adhesion, cell polarity, and actomyosin contractility in migrating cells.
In this study, we have identified a fungal secondary metabolite, called"mellein", which is produced by the human pathogen Aspergillus fumigatus.
The discovery of bioactive natural products is a long-standing challenge in drug discovery, but it has become increasingly difficult due to the large number of compounds produced by fungi.
We have developed a high-throughput screening platform to identify immunosuppressive compounds from natural product libraries using a combination of in vitro and in vivo assays.
This technology is capable of identifying compounds that inhibit neutrophils from migrating to sites of inflammation, and it has been used to identify novel anti-inflammatory compounds.
The blood-brain barrier is formed by tight junctions between brain capillary endothelial cells and active efflux transporters such as P-glycoprotein ( Pgp) on their surface.
P-glycoprotein ( Pgp) is an ATP-dependent efflux pump that transports many anticancer drugs out of cells and contributes to multidrug resistance in cancer.
We show here that P-glycoprotein ( Pgp) is present on the surface of human and pig brain capillary endothelial cells, which form the blood-brain barrier.
This study provides evidence for an endogenous detoxification pathway in the brain and may have implications for drug development.
We have found that when C. elegans males mate with hermaphrodites, they are killed by the sperm of their mates.
Male reproductive cells ( sperm) cause harm in females by displacing their own sperm from the female reproductive tract, invading the ovaries, and even breaching the gonads to invade other organs.
In contrast to the common view of sex as an evolutionary trade-off between reproduction and survival, we find that in some species, being male is associated with higher survival than being female.
We found evidence for strong genetic differentiation between species, but no evidence of hybridization or introgression.

The ability to control and manipulate the internal structure of materials has been a long-standing goal in materials science, which has led to the development of various classes of materials with unique properties such as negative mass density, negative stiffness, and negative Poisson's ratio.
The challenge in protein engineering is to predict how changes in amino acid sequences affect the structure and function of proteins.
However, the relationship between protein sequence and function is complex, making it difficult to predict how changes in one will affect the other.

Antibiotics to prevent meningitis after head injury Background Meningitis is an inflammation of the membranes surrounding the brain and spinal cord, which can be caused by infection.

The review of trials found no evidence that one surgical technique is better than another for preventing CSF leaks, but there was some evidence that endoscopic surgery may be less likely to cause complications such as infection or meningitis compared to open surgery.
The review found no evidence to support the use of these drugs in this way, and there was some evidence that they may be harmful.
The review found no evidence to suggest that antibiotics are beneficial for preventing meningitis after head injury.
The review found that there was not enough evidence from randomised controlled trials to determine whether or not antibiotics should be given routinely to people with basal skull fractures.
Insects have evolved an extraordinary ability to produce a tough, yet lightweight, protective cuticle that has been studied for decades as a model system for biomimetic materials research.
The insect exoskeleton, or cuticle, is composed of multiple layers and its structure is determined by the interaction between the plasma membrane and the extracellular matrix.

The association of two or more species is called a symbiosis, which can be mutualistic ( beneficial to both), commensal ( beneficial to one but not the other), or parasitic ( harmful to one but not the other).
The ability to manipulate these interactions in the laboratory is essential for understanding how they have evolved, but it has proven difficult to do so with many of these relationships.

We used genetic engineering to replace the endosymbiont in one line with a genetically distinct strain from another line, demonstrating that this is possible by using heat stress to reduce the number of native bacteria and then introducing the new strain.
We also found that aphid hosts carrying the Buchnera replacement had increased heat tolerance, suggesting that this new endosymbiont may have provided an adaptive benefit to its host.
The genetic contribution to AMD has been well established; however, the role of CFH in disease pathogenesis remains unclear.

The white-rot fungus Phanerochaete chrysosporium is one of the most efficient organisms for lignin degradation, and its genome has been sequenced to identify genes involved in this process.
The ability of white-rot fungi ( WRF) to use lignin as a carbon source for growth and energy production has long been debated.
Corticosteroid drugs, such as prednisolone and budesonide, are commonly prescribed to treat asthma attacks in children.
The review found that there was no clear evidence to support the use of corticosteroids in this situation.
The review found that there is not enough evidence to show whether or not antibiotics are effective for treating this condition.
Corticosteroids are effective in reducing symptoms of asthma attacks and improving lung function, but they have side effects.
More research is needed to determine whether or not steroids are effective in reducing the risk of death, and if so, which dose and route of administration is most beneficial.
Pain in the side or back (ureteric colic) is a common symptom of kidney stones and is often described as severe.
The review found that there was not enough evidence from trials to show whether or not MET improved the chances of passing stones spontaneously.
There is still some uncertainty about how well they work in people with dementia and whether or not they are safe to use.
We wanted to find out if giving these drugs would help more stones pass on their own than taking no treatment ( placebo).
We have generated mice lacking PP2A activity in mature T cells and show that this results in an inability to generate TH17 cells, which are important for host defense against fungal infections.
We found that PP2A is required for TGFb-induced activation of Smad2 and Smad3, which are transcription factors that regulate gene expression in response to TGFb signaling.
We found that the phosphorylation state of SMAD2/3 is regulated by IL-1b and TNF-a signaling pathways, which are known to be involved in Th17 cell differentiation.
These results suggest that targeting PP2A may be an effective strategy to treat inflammatory diseases driven by TH17 cells.
Surgery for epilepsy Epilepsy is a chronic condition characterised by recurrent seizures.

Immunotherapy for focal epilepsy in adults Review question We wanted to find out whether adding an immunomodulator (a drug that affects the immune system) to antiepileptic drugs could reduce seizures in people with focal epilepsy.
One trial was found, which included 61 participants with various types of epilepsy.
The review found no evidence for the effectiveness of immunomodulatory drugs in reducing seizures, and there was insufficient information on their side effects.

Piwi-interacting RNAs ( piRNAs) are small non-coding RNAs that play an important role in maintaining genome integrity by silencing transposons, which are mobile genetic elements that can cause genomic instability if not properly controlled.
In contrast, transposon activation in somatic cells is not inherited and has no effect on the germline.
Here we report that the loss of function of Hinfp, a highly conserved protein in animals, results in the activation of transposons and other mobile genetic elements.
Our results suggest that Hinfp may be a novel therapeutic target for treating diseases associated with genome instability such as cancer, neurodegenerative disorders, and aging.

We show that nonconsensus binding is a common feature of many transcription factors and that it plays an important role in determining their binding specificities.
The aim of this review was to find out whether or not changing the position of critically ill people regularly is beneficial for their recovery.
The aim of this review was to find out whether positioning the patient on their side is better than any other position for critically ill adults.
The review found that there is not enough evidence to show whether one position is better than another for reducing the risk of death, serious complications, or need for reoperation.
The review authors searched for studies in which people with dementia were given music therapy, compared to those who did not receive music therapy.
Study characteristics We found no evidence from randomized controlled trials to support the use of lateral positioning for critically ill patients.
The review found that there is not enough evidence to be sure whether or not lying on either side of the body is better than lying in any other position.
We found no evidence from randomised controlled trials (RCTs) on this topic.
The review found that there was no clear evidence to show whether or not one type of treatment is better than another for treating people with schizophrenia.

The outbreak of cholera in Haiti in 2010 has been described as one of the largest and most devastating outbreaks of this disease in recent history.
We conducted an evaluation of the performance of the CATI system in Haiti from July 2013 to June 2017.

The cost-effectiveness analysis showed that the CATI approach was more cost-effective than the standard approach in terms of the number of people treated and the proportion of those treated who were cured.
Peatlands store large amounts of carbon and are sensitive to climate warming.
We show that, on average, soil warming increases soil respiration by 130% per degree of warming.
We found that shrubs warmed at an increasing rate in warmer years and that this effect was more pronounced for deciduous than evergreen species.

This study provides evidence for an unexpected increase in root growth and carbon sequestration in response to drying of saturated soils, which may explain the observed expansion of shrubs in northern peatlands under climate warming.
The exocyst complex, which consists of eight proteins, plays an essential role in polarized secretion by tethering secretory vesicles to the plasma membrane.
We show here that the C-terminus of EXO70A1, which has been previously shown to be important for pollen tube growth, is also required for endocytosis and exocytosis during pollen germination.
We show that in plants, the exocyst complex is anchored at the plasma membrane via interactions of its Exo70 subunit with specific anionic phospholipids.

The use of human embryonic stem cells ( hESCs) and induced pluripotent stem cells ( iPSCs) has been proposed as an alternative source of donor tissue for transplantation in RP patients.
We have used two new nonhuman primate models of retinal degeneration to evaluate the feasibility and efficacy of human embryonic stem cell-derived retinal tissue for transplantation.
We also demonstrated the feasibility of hESC-RPE transplantation in nonhuman primates using an animal model.

This study provides important information on the potential of human retinal progenitor cells to replace damaged photoreceptors in humans, which is an essential step toward the development of cell-based therapies for blinding diseases.

We show here that the metabolic interactions between two species of bacteria, Bacillus subtilis and Staphylococcus aureus, generate a collective oscillation in their population dynamics when they grow together in a biofilm.
Here, we present a mathematical model to describe the spatial and temporal dynamics of this process.

Our model suggests that the interplay between these two signals is important for determining the optimal survival strategy of the cells, which may be relevant to other biological systems where multiple signals are involved.

The ability to form multicellular colonies is an important trait for many organisms and has been studied in yeast.
The mechanisms by which these patterns form are not well understood.
We have developed a method to quantify the growth pattern of yeast colonies on solid media with different nutrient conditions.

We show that the ability to sense and respond to cell-cell contact is important for the ability of yeast cells to expand in two dimensions, as cells lacking this ability are outcompeted by their wild-type counterparts when grown on agar surfaces.

We show that the human brain uses a combination of both visual and motor information to guide reaching movements in space.
Here, we recorded electrical activity from the surface of the brain using electrodes implanted in epilepsy patients to study how visual attention is controlled at a millisecond time scale.
We show that high gamma activity is present during the entire attentional allocation period, but only when attention is directed to the stimulus.

The metastasis-associated lung adenocarcinoma transcript 1 ( MALAT1) is an oncogenic lncRNA that promotes tumor progression and metastasis.
We found that METTL16, a putative RNA methyltransferase, is required for MALAT1-mediated splicing inhibition and that it interacts with MALAT1 in vivo.

The lithium stripping and plating processes are critical for the performance of lithium metal anodes in high-energy-density batteries, but they have not been well understood at the atomic scale.
The results provide insight into the degradation mechanism of Li metal anodes in solid electrolyte batteries.

The results show that the Li^+ diffusion in the solid electrolyte interface ( SEI) is the limiting factor for high-rate charging/discharging of lithium metal anodes, which has been overlooked by previous studies.

The review found that adding radiotherapy to breast-conserving surgery (BCS) did not improve survival, but it may reduce the risk of local recurrence and increase the chance of avoiding mastectomy.
The review found no evidence to support the routine use of radiotherapy after surgery for early breast cancer.

Malnutrition is common among patients admitted to hospital, with up to 55% of adults being underweight or overweight.

Study characteristics We searched for randomised controlled trials (RCTs) comparing dietary advice with or without oral nutritional supplements versus no dietary advice, dietary advice only or oral nutritional supplements only.
The results of this review show that giving people with schizophrenia dietary advice leads to a small reduction in their body weight compared to those who do not receive dietary advice.
The evidence for other outcomes was not clear-cut, but suggested that dietary advice may help to reduce fatigue and improve muscle strength.
The review found no evidence to support or refute the use of nutritional supplements in this population.

In this study, we found that patients who received chloroquine had better clinical outcomes than those who did not receive chloroquine.

We found no significant differences in the proportion of patients with severe dengue, or in the time taken for the virus to clear from the blood stream between those who received intravenous fluids and those who did not.

We found that mice lacking Neo1 have increased seizure susceptibility, which is associated with altered neuronal excitability.
We show that the absence of this protein causes seizures and that restoring its expression in these cells is sufficient to prevent seizure activity.

Our study provides new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying astrocyte-mediated regulation of neuronal excitability and seizure susceptibility, and identifies NEO1 as a potential therapeutic target for epilepsy treatment.

The availability of large-scale experimental datasets has led to the development of computational methods for predicting gene function from these data.
However, there is a lack of experimental validation for most of these predictions, which has limited their adoption by biologists.
We have used experimental approaches to test predictions made by three different algorithms for identifying genes involved in mitochondrial function.
We show that many genes have multiple functions and that some genes may be involved in more than half of all biological processes.
Our results show that the choice of method, as well as the type of data used to train the model, have large effects on the accuracy of these predictions.

The histone deacetylase 6 ( HDAC6) is an enzyme that removes acetyl groups from proteins, including histones, to regulate gene expression.
There are eleven human histone deacetylases ( HDACs) and they have been implicated in many diseases including cancer.
The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster is an excellent model organism for studying gene regulation because of its simple genome and well-characterized genetics.
We have identified several mutations in the histone deacetylase 3 ( hdac3) gene, which is required for normal development of the nervous system.
In this study we show that HDAC1 and HDAC3 have overlapping functions in regulating gene expression at the level of chromatin.
HDAC inhibition has been shown to induce apoptosis in cancer cells, but the mechanism of this effect remains unclear.
We found that loss of HDAC3 function leads to increased acetylated histone H3 and increased expression of pro-apoptotic genes, resulting in increased cell death in developing tissues.

Mosquitoes of the genus Aedes transmit several important human pathogens including dengue virus ( DENV), chikungunya virus ( CHIKV) and Zika virus ( ZIKV).
We have developed a method to detect and identify mosquito eggs in pools of mosquitoes using molecular techniques, which is more efficient than traditional methods.
We found no evidence of Aedes aegypti in any of the 477 ovitraps used to collect eggs for this study.

The chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing ( ChIP-seq) method has become an important tool in this regard by allowing researchers to identify regions of DNA bound by specific proteins.
We have developed a new statistical approach called BCP ( Bayesian Change Point), which is able to identify such changes in the genome-wide distribution of ChIP-seq data.
We show that BCP is able to accurately model the variability of reads and their dependencies, and we demonstrate its ability to improve the accuracy of genome assembly on simulated data.
BCP is generally applicable to any type of ChIP-seq data and can be used as an alternative to existing methods.

This method is highly specific and sensitive for all types of T. cruzi strains and has been successfully applied to various biological samples ( blood, serum, urine, tissue biopsies) from infected individuals in different stages of disease progression.

We have discovered a new family of small cysteine-rich peptides, called LURE1 peptides, which act as pollen tube attractants.
We found that AtLURE1 is expressed in the synergid cells, which are adjacent to the pollen tube entrance site, and is secreted from these cells toward the pollen tube entrance.
We found that Arabidopsis LURE1 peptides were more effective than those of its close relative A. lyrata in attracting Arabidopsis pollen tubes but not vice versa.
We show here that the Arabidopsis LURE1 protein is able to functionally replace the Torenia LURE1 protein in vivo, suggesting that these proteins are functionally equivalent.
We show that the LURE1 gene family is responsible for pollen tube guidance in Arabidopsis thaliana and that this function has been conserved during evolution.
Mycobacterium bovis causes tuberculosis in humans and animals, but its transmission from animals to humans is rare.
We found that the proportion of M. bovis isolates in human cases was increasing, and that the proportion of M. bovis isolates with resistance to rifampicin was also increasing.
The results of this study suggest that the prevalence of M. bovis in cattle is higher than previously thought, and that it may be more common in areas where there are no cases of human TB.
We found that the proportion of Mycobacterium tuberculosis complex ( MTBC) species was lower than previously reported, and that Mycobacterium bovis was more prevalent than expected.
We found that drug-resistant TB is common among patients with TB in this region, including multi-drug resistant TB ( MDR-TB) caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis and M. bovis.
The results show that there are high levels of drug resistance to first line drugs in M. bovis isolates, which may be associated with the use of these drugs for treatment of tuberculosis in humans.
The ability to identify the origin of an infection from pathogen genetic data has been hindered by the complexity of host-pathogen interactions.
In this study, we show that unsampled individuals can be important sources of infection and also play a role in the spread of disease by acting as intermediaries between other infected individuals.
We show how these models can be used to identify factors that affect the likelihood of disease spread, and we demonstrate their application to real-world data from the COVID-19 pandemic.
We apply our approach to the HIV epidemic in Detroit, MI, where we find that the majority of sequences are from individuals who have not been previously reported as infected with HIV.
Our results suggest that current methods used for drug resistance testing may not be suitable for forensic purposes and that new approaches will need to be developed.
Antibiotics are commonly used to treat bacterial infections, but their overuse has led to the development of antibiotic resistance.
The reasons for this are not well understood, but many studies have been conducted to try and find out why doctors prescribe antibiotics when they might not be needed.
This systematic review aimed to find out whether educational interventions for health professionals could improve the quality of antibiotic prescribing in primary care settings.

The review found that there is some evidence to support the effectiveness of these strategies in reducing antibiotic prescribing for children with sore throats, ear infections, and urinary tract infections.
The studies found that using printed educational materials, audit and feedback, or both together did not improve the quality of care for people with dementia.
The studies found that interactive educational meetings were better than traditional teaching methods, but there was no clear evidence of an effect on clinical outcomes.
The studies of educational outreach visits to physicians showed mixed results, with some showing an increase in screening rates but others not showing any effect.
The review found that giving patients a prescription to be filled later if they develop symptoms of an infection reduces antibiotic prescribing rates without increasing adverse events.

This is because the success of interventions depends on how well they are tailored to the specific context, including the local culture and the characteristics of the doctors who prescribe antibiotics.
The review found that no single intervention is effective across all settings and types of behaviour change.

The results of this study suggest that multi-faceted interventions, which include education at multiple levels (e.g., individual, family, community), are more effective than single-level interventions in changing health behaviours and improving health outcomes.
We used whole-brain volumetric serial two-photon tomography to visualize and quantify B cells in the brain after stroke.
We found that B cells are required for the production of new neurons in the adult brain after stroke, and that their absence leads to increased neuronal death.

The mechanisms by which B cells contribute to neuroinflammation are not well understood, but it has been suggested that they may be involved in the production of antibodies that target neurons or other glial cells.
The geographic range size of species is an important component of their ecological niche, but we know little about how it varies across taxa or what factors drive variation in range size.
We show that species' range sizes are strongly influenced by their body size, with larger species having smaller ranges than smaller species.
Our results show that the distribution of fish species is more strongly determined by their evolutionary history than by current environmental conditions.
Our results suggest that connectivity is the main driver of fish species' global distribution patterns, which may be threatened by ongoing habitat destruction and degradation.
The genetic variant Thr300Ala in ATG16L1 is associated with an increased risk of developing Crohn's disease; however, it has not been clear how this variant contributes to disease pathogenesis.
We found that mice expressing the mutant protein did not have spontaneous colitis, but had abnormal morphology of Paneth and goblet cells.
We found that this mutation impairs autophagy and increases IL-1b secretion in vitro and in vivo.

Our results provide insight into the molecular mechanisms underlying the pathogenicity of the T300A mutation in ATG7, and may help explain why some individuals with mutations in ATG7 are more susceptible to certain diseases than others.

We show that our method is able to identify clones with high accuracy in simulated data and in real tumor samples from patients with glioblastoma.
We have determined the crystal structure of the human kappa opioid receptor in its active conformation bound to a G protein and an agonist.
We have used molecular dynamics simulations to identify key residues in the k-opioid receptor ( kOR) that interact with the G-protein, which is essential for signal transduction.
The results provide insight into how the binding of G proteins to their receptors is regulated by ligand and may help guide drug design for opioid analgesics.

The alternative pathway is activated by factor B ( FB) bound to its receptor, CR1, on host cells and cleaved by factor D ( FD).
Here we report that ecotin, a protein found in snake venom, blocks the activation of MASP-3 by Factor D and thereby prevents complement activation.
We show here that ecotin is a potent inhibitor of complement and lysozyme activity in vitro and in vivo.
The cell cycle is regulated by a network of proteins that ensure proper progression through each stage.
In this study, we have identified a protein complex called the"meiotic cohesin regulator Wapl" as a key player in ensuring proper chromosome segregation during meiosis.

Here we report that the protein TDM1 is required for proper chromosome segregation in plants and that it acts by interacting with the APC/C ( anaphase-promoting complex/cyclosome), a conserved E3 ubiquitin ligase complex involved in cell cycle regulation.
We show that TDM1 is required for proper meiotic progression in Arabidopsis thaliana, and that it acts as an APC/C component during meiosis II.
We propose that this mechanism is responsible for the difference in the number of rounds of cell division between meiosis I and meiosis II.
The Himalayan region is highly vulnerable to climate change due to its high sensitivity to temperature changes.
We show that the number of avalanches has increased significantly over the last few decades in the Western Himalayas.
We show that the number of avalanche events is increasing with rising temperatures, but we also find evidence for an important role of precipitation variability in driving this trend.

The fossil record provides evidence for an early human presence in this region during the Middle Pleistocene, when the climate was warmer than today.

The aim of this review was to find out whether or not it is safe and effective to perform endotracheal suctioning without disconnecting the baby from the ventilator.
Study characteristics We found five studies (involving 246 participants) that compared suctioning with and without disconnecting the patient from the ventilator.
This review found two studies comparing suctioning with and without disconnection of the tube.

The review found that there was some evidence that using a catheter to remove mucus from the airway, while still connected to the ventilator, may be better than disconnecting the person from the ventilator and then removing the mucus with a catheter.
The malaria parasite, Plasmodium falciparum, is dependent on iron for its survival and proliferation within red blood cells.
However, little is known about how metals interact with mosquitoes and whether they are toxic to them.
In this study, we have used mass spectrometry-based proteomics to identify proteins containing metals in two important mosquito species, Anopheles albimanus and Aedes aegypti.
We also found that the mosquito's ability to produce eggs was reduced when it had low levels of iron.
We have used genetic crosses between albimanus mosquitoes with different levels of resistance to malaria parasites to identify genes involved in parasite resistance.
The iron chelator restored susceptibility to infection in the resistant strain and reduced the virulence of the susceptible strain.
We found that iron chelation led to a decrease in copper levels and an increase in reactive oxygen species ( ROS) production in mosquito tissues.
Monkeypox virus ( MPXV) is a neglected viral disease of humans caused by a member of the Orthopoxviridae family, which includes smallpox and cow pox viruses.

The smallpox vaccine has been shown to provide protection against monkeypox virus ( MPXV) but this vaccine was discontinued after the eradication of smallpox in humans in 1980 and therefore most people are not protected against MPXV.
There is an urgent need for safe and effective treatments and vaccines against monkeypox virus ( MPXV).
The use of recombinant BoHV-4 vectors expressing different MPXV glycoproteins was evaluated for their ability to protect against lethal MPXV infection in immunocompromised mice.

The results presented here demonstrate the potential of bovine herpesvirus 4 ( BoHV-4) as an effective vector for delivering heterologous antigens to elicit protective immune responses against infectious diseases in cattle.

The evolution of influenza viruses is driven by two main mechanisms: reassortment ( genetic exchange) and mutation.
We have found that reassortment provokes an increase in the rate of protein evolution for all three proteins we examined ( HA, NA and PB2).
We have used a phylogenetic approach to study the evolution of the eight genomic segments of the human H3N2 viruses since their emergence in 1968.
We find that, after reassortment events, there is an excess of amino acid substitutions in the segments involved in the reassortment event compared with other segments.

We have used a combination of experimental evolution and mathematical modeling to study how CWD strains emerge and adapt in deer.

The pain from a post-dural puncture headache ( PDPH) is usually described as a dull ache or pressure sensation in the head, which may be worse when standing up than lying down.
The aim of this review was to find out whether changing the design of needles could reduce the risk of post-dural puncture headache (PDPH).
The review authors searched the literature up to March 2015 and found 12 studies, which included 1041 participants.
Study characteristics The evidence is current to July 2016.
The review found that using traumatic needles increased the risk of developing post-dural-puncture headache by about two times more than using atraumatic needles.
The review found that there is some evidence that using atraumatic needles may reduce the occurrence of post-dural puncture headache after spinal anaesthesia.
Atraumatic needles may be less likely to cause headaches, but more research is needed to confirm this.
Quality of the evidence The quality of the evidence was low or very low due to small numbers of participants, poor reporting and imprecision in results.
High-dimensional data is ubiquitous in biology, and dimensionality reduction techniques have been widely used for data analysis.
The analysis of these data is complicated by various sources of noise and variability, which may lead to incorrect conclusions.
We present an approach called"adjustment by principal components" ( AC-PCA), which combines dimensionality reduction with adjustment for confounders in a single step.
We demonstrate that AC-PCA is able to remove unwanted variation from both human and mouse datasets, and we show that it outperforms existing methods on several metrics.
Archaeological sites are being destroyed by urbanization and other human activities, threatening to erase our understanding of the past.
We use historical aerial photographs from 1917 to 2015 combined with airborne LiDAR to track changes in the landscape around the ancient city of Jerash, Jordan.
The study of ancient cities such as Pompeii provides an opportunity for us to learn about how people lived in the past by analyzing their built environment.
In this study, we have developed a computational tool that integrates gene expression and genome-scale metabolic network models to predict the metabolic requirements of pathogens during infection.
We found that the metabolism of fatty acids and amino acids is essential for intracellular survival of Listeria monocytosis, an important foodborne pathogen.

Our results provide new insights on how bacteria sense environmental cues to control gene expression in response to host infection.
The ability to predict the outcome of an infection based on the host's gene expression profile could be used to identify patients at risk of severe disease and guide treatment decisions.
Trichomonas vaginalis is an obligate human pathogen causing the most prevalent STI in women of reproductive age.
However, little is known about how the host responds to infection with this parasite.
The parasite is cleared by neutrophils, which are recruited to the site of infection in large numbers.
Here we show that human neutrophils are able to kill T. vaginalis by phagocytosis and release of reactive oxygen species.
We found that neutrophils kill T. vaginalis by taking bites out of the parasite and then engulfing it in a process called trogocytosis.
We found that both processes require the presence of human serum, which contains complement proteins and other molecules that may be important for parasite killing.

This study provides evidence that neutrophils use a previously unappreciated mechanism called"trogocytosis," in which they engulf pathogens by forming an invagination of their plasma membrane around them.

The mechanism by which dogs lap water is not well understood, but it has been suggested that the hydrodynamics of the tongue-pool interaction play an important role in determining the amount of water taken up with each stroke.
We show that the tongue is able to lap up liquids at high rates by using a combination of two mechanisms, one based on capillary action and the other based on surface tension forces.
We show that the fluid dynamics of the jet is important for explaining the behavior of the dog, and we use this knowledge to predict when the dog will catch its prey.
We show that the speed at which dogs lap water is determined by the acceleration of their tongues and that they use this acceleration to maximize the volume of water in each lap.

Physiotherapy for complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) Complex regional pain syndrome (CRPS) is a painful condition which affects some people after an injury to a limb.
Study characteristics We found no studies of this treatment approach, so we could not draw any conclusions about its effectiveness or safety.
The studies were conducted in different settings and used a variety of treatments, including exercise therapy, manual therapy, education, relaxation techniques, and cognitive behavioural therapy.

The quality of evidence was low for all outcomes.
The review found that there is no clear evidence that any of these treatments work better than another treatment for reducing pain in people with CRPS I.
The review found that there was not enough evidence to show whether any treatments were effective in reducing pain or improving function in people with CRPS type I or II.

SaPIs are mobile genetic elements that encode virulence factors and have been implicated in pathogenesis of Staphylococcus aureus infections.

Integrated Information Theory ( IIT) is a theory of consciousness that has been proposed to explain how integrated information generated by the causal interactions between neurons in the brain gives rise to conscious experience.
We show that these principles can be formalized into postulates that constrain the design of any mechanism that generates conscious experience.
The theory posits that consciousness arises from the integrated information generated by the causal interactions between elements within a system.
The theory also predicts that consciousness is not necessarily related to the complexity of the underlying neural circuitry, but rather to the number of active components in the complex.
