We have developed an automated imaging platform for the study of single-cell-level drug delivery in tumors.
This work provides a framework for predicting nanoparticle delivery in tumors and enables personalized treatment strategies based on the unique biology of each tumor.
Transposable elements ( TE) are mobile genetic elements that have been shown to play an important role in the evolution of bacterial genomes, including the spread of antibiotic resistance genes.
The mechanism by which this occurs has been unclear.
We show that the transposase is initially active as a dimer but that it must be converted to a monomer for efficient transposition.

We use a framework to assess the effectiveness of three case studies--the Great Barrier Reef, Southern Ocean fisheries, and Swedish forests--to identify key factors that contribute to successful implementation of adaptive governance for natural capital management.

This study provides an assessment of the effectiveness of different social-ecological governance arrangements for managing natural resources, including their ability to adapt to changing environmental conditions.
We show that adaptive governance has been successful in improving the management of natural resources and enhancing the provision of multiple ecosystem services.
The results provide insights for the design of future conservation efforts in other regions.
Dengue fever is an infectious disease caused by dengue viruses, which are transmitted to humans through mosquito bites.
Surveillance data are often incomplete and do not capture all cases of disease.
The number of people who have been infected with a disease is often unknown, but it can be estimated from the number of reported cases.
We estimated the number of dengue cases for each country in SEA from 2001-2010, based on reported cases and estimates of underreporting.
The role of CD4+ T cell memory in protective immunity is not well understood.
However, it is unclear whether this response is due to the presence of long-lived memory T cells or short-lived effector T cells generated during the initial infection.
Here we show that these cells are generated during primary infection and are not derived from memory T cells.
However, it has been suggested that some pathogens are able to evade this response by producing proteins that interfere with the function of Tregs.
Cells sense and respond to their environment by sensing the physical properties of the surrounding tissue, which is composed of cells and an extracellular matrix ( ECM).

We found that thrombospondin-1 ( Thbs1), a secreted protein that is present in the ECM, plays a key role in mediating this process by binding to integrins on the cell surface and promoting the formation of focal adhesions.
We show that Thbs1 activates YAP through a mechanism involving the small G protein Rap2, which is known to be involved in cell adhesion and migration.
Social isolation is a major public health problem in many countries, but there is little evidence on effective ways to address it.
Mindfulness is defined as paying attention to the present moment in a nonjudgmental way.
We measured participants' loneliness and social contact using wearable devices for 3 d before and after the intervention.
This study provides evidence that acceptance skills training can reduce loneliness and increase social connection in individuals who are lonely.
Th9 cells are known to be involved in autoimmunity and cancer but their role in infectious diseases is less clear.

We used single-cell RNA sequencing to identify the gene expression profile of individual T helper 9 ( Th9) cells in mice with experimental autoimmune encephalomyelitis ( EAE), an animal model for multiple sclerosis.
We found that blockade of CD96 on Treg cells restores their suppressive function, while blocking PD-1 has no effect.
Our study provides evidence for a novel mechanism by which CD96 may act to limit inflammation and suggests potential therapeutic targets for treating inflammatory diseases associated with IL-9.
The Schlafen family member, Schlafen11 ( SLFN11), is known to be an inhibitor of HIV-1 replication in vitro and in vivo.

We found that the version of SLFN11 in humans is less active than those from other primates, which may explain why it has not been able to prevent HIV-1 infection as effectively as these other species.
The stochastic nature of ion channels causes fluctuations in membrane voltage, which are known to affect the timing of spikes.
The slow adaptation current is an important feature of many neurons, but its physiological role has not been fully understood.
The resulting electrical signals are generated by finite numbers of ion channels, which introduces stochastic fluctuations in the signal.
In this paper, we use mathematical analysis and computer simulations to show how the variability in the time between spikes is affected by the presence of adaptation currents.
In particular, we show that the stochasticity in the adaptation process is crucial for the ability of neurons to respond to stimuli.

We show how the distribution of interspike intervals ( ISIs) can be used as an indicator of the relative importance of these two sources of variability, and we provide a simple method for estimating this ratio from the observed ISI distribution.
Lassa fever ( LF) is an acute, severe and often fatal disease caused by Lassa virus ( LASV).
The genetic diversity of the virus was low and the majority of the isolates were genetically related to each other, suggesting that there was a common source of infection for most cases.
The diversity of these organisms is vast, with numerous species found in different parts of the world's oceans.
We have sequenced the genome of a new strain, Synechococcus sp.
We found that there is a large number of genes that are common to all species and that these genes have been conserved in their expression patterns across species.
We found that the two ecotypes are not as distinct as previously thought, but rather represent a continuum of adaptation to light intensity.

We show that many genes have been gained or lost in recent evolutionary time, suggesting that much of the diversity is due to rapid evolution within species rather than large-scale changes between species.
We also find that the genomes are highly similar to each other, but have some differences that may be important for their ecological success.
The aggressive nature of glioblastoma multiforme ( GBM), the most common primary brain cancer, has made it difficult to treat effectively.
However, cancer cells exposed to radiation can also develop resistance to treatment by becoming more aggressive and invasive.
We have identified MDA-9/Syntenin as a novel prognostic marker for glioblastoma ( GB) patients, whose expression correlates with increased aggressiveness of tumors and reduced survival time.
We have developed a small molecule inhibitor of MDA-9/Syntenin and demonstrated its ability to inhibit EGFRvIII-MDA-9/Syntenin interaction in vitro and in vivo.
We found that PDZ1 is required for GBM cell survival and proliferation but not for normal astrocytes.
Agriculture is responsible for 70% of freshwater withdrawals, and irrigation accounts for 40% of global water use.
Groundwater is an important source of water for irrigation in the Great Plains region of the United States, but it is being depleted at unsustainable rates in some areas.
We show that when water is abundant, farmers use less water and produce more crops per unit of water used.
We find that the lagged effects are larger for more efficient irrigation systems, which is consistent with the hypothesis that water use has delayed impacts on streamflow.
Protein hydrolysis involves breaking down proteins in milk into smaller pieces so they are easier to digest.
This review found no evidence to support the use of protein hydrolysate formula for preterm babies.
The studies varied in size, quality of reporting and design.
The studies included babies who were born before 34 weeks' gestation and/or had a birth weight of less than 1750 grams.

Key results The review found very little information on the effect of using protein hydrolysate compared to standard formula for preterm babies, and there was no clear difference in growth or development between the two groups.
Neddylation is a posttranslational modification in which the small protein Nedd8 is attached to target proteins by an enzyme called Ube2S.
We have found that a mutant strain of the filamentous fungus Aspergillus nidulans lacking both deneddylase enzymes is defective in multicellular development.
We show that the deneddylase activity of Ulp2 is required to maintain proper levels of Smt3 in cells, and that this activity is essential for normal multicellular development.
The process of infection is initiated when a bacteriophage attaches to its host cell and injects its genetic material into the cytoplasm.
We found that SprA is expressed in vegetative cells but not in spores, while SprB is only expressed during sporulation or when the cell is treated with an antibiotic that induces the lytic cycle.
In addition, we found that SprB is sufficient to trigger prophage excision and that it does so independently of the lytic cycle.
We show here that the protein SprB, which is encoded by the prophage genome, is responsible for controlling the timing of prophage excision and that it does so by binding to the host DNA.
In this study, we have used a combination of genetic and biochemical approaches to identify a novel protein complex involved in the regulation of SIN activity.
In this study we show that the SIN is activated from both spindle poles during mitosis and that it is inactivated on one pole by the Cdc16-Byr4 GAP complex.
However, it is not known how these proteins are distributed to the two SPBs in an asymmetric manner.
We show that this model is able to explain many previously unexplained phenomena, including the effects of mutations in the SIN proteins on the timing of mitosis.
We found that the model was able to predict most of the known interactions with high accuracy, but there were some exceptions.
The speed of these movements is not constant and depends on the distance to be covered.
The nonlinearity of saccades has been attributed to saturation in the brainstem circuitry, but this is not supported by experimental evidence.
Here we show that the visual information encoded by the SC is not static but rather dynamic and depends on the temporal structure of its activity.
We find that the activity of these cells is coordinated across the brain and that they are synchronized with the movement plan.
This precise tuning of SC burst activity is consistent with the idea that the SC acts as a linear controller to generate straight saccadic eye movements.
The initiation of gene expression is a complex process involving multiple steps and factors.
Here, we use single-molecule fluorescence microscopy to study the dynamics of RNA polymerase during transcription initiation and elongation.
We found that the RNA polymerase pauses frequently during initiation, adopting a backtracked conformation in which it is unable to synthesize new DNA.
We found that the paused state is more stable under conditions of low NTP concentrations or when there is an imbalance between NTP concentrations.

This work provides evidence for a long-sought-after"paused" intermediate in transcription initiation by RNA polymerase II, which has implications for our understanding of gene expression regulation and potential applications to cancer therapy.
There is an urgent need for more accurate prediction of MS disease progression, which could be used to guide treatment decisions.
We used statistical methods to model the natural history of disability progression in people with RRMS and SPMS, and then estimated how DMTs affect this progression.
We used data from two large multiple sclerosis ( MS) cohorts in the UK and Canada to investigate whether or not there is an association between vitamin D levels and disease activity.
The study was designed to see if there were any differences in how well people with RRMS or SPMS responded to treatment with fingolimod compared with placebo.
We developed a statistical model using data from the UoWMS to predict EDSS scores at any point in time after diagnosis.
The model also suggests that, if treatment is stopped, patients will deteriorate faster than expected from their current level of disability.
The results of this study suggest that disease progression is slower in people treated with disease modifying therapies compared to those not treated with these drugs.
More research is needed to find out how long the effects last, as well as whether or not they are sustained after stopping treatment.

The study of how information spreads through animal populations has been revolutionized by the use of social network analysis, which allows researchers to identify who is connected to whom and what behavior they are performing.
In contrast to humans and some other species, however, there is currently no empirical evidence for social learning in wild primates.
We found that social transmission was responsible for 85% of all observed behaviors and that individuals learned at a much faster rate when they were exposed to experienced individuals.
However, it has long been known that such membranes are unstable under conditions that would have existed on Earth at the time when life first emerged.
We show that the presence of certain amino acids in the lipid bilayer is sufficient to stabilize membranes and prevent their complete disintegration upon drying.
Our work provides insight into how proteins may have been recruited to membranes in early cells and how they might have stabilized them.
The extraction and processing of bitumen from oil sands is a major source of greenhouse gas ( GHG) emissions.
The results show that emissions from these facilities are higher than those estimated in the Canadian National Pollutant Release Inventory ( NRPi).

This study provides an example of how direct measurements can be used to improve the accuracy and completeness of emissions from oil sands operations, which is important for assessing the environmental impacts of these activities.
Multiple sclerosis ( MS) is an autoimmune disease characterized by inflammation and demyelination in the central nervous system ( CNS).
The authors have previously shown that variation in the MERTK gene, which encodes for a protein called MerTK, increases the risk of developing multiple sclerosis ( MS).

We have identified two genetic loci ( rs12719855 and rs12979860) that are associated with an increased risk of developing MS, one of which is located near the gene for Mer tyrosine kinase receptor ( MERTK).
We have identified two genetic variants in MERTK that are associated with multiple sclerosis ( MS) risk and severity.
Here, we report that mutations in the gene wntA cause changes in wing patterns in multiple species of butterflies.
We also show that WntA has a novel function in the formation of intervein patterns in the monarch butterfly, which is not seen in other butterflies and flies.

This study provides insight into how changes in the expression pattern of a single gene can lead to dramatic differences in morphology, and demonstrates that the same gene can have different functions depending on its location within a developing tissue.
The human prion diseases fatal familial insomnia ( FFI) and CJD178 are caused by mutations in the PrP gene, but they have distinct clinical features.

This study provides evidence for the existence of multiple distinct prion strains in the brains of affected animals and shows that the disease phenotype is influenced by the presence of different prion strains.

Here, we have used transgenic mice to study the pathogenicity of the human FFI mutation in vivo and found that these mice develop severe neurological symptoms similar to those seen in humans with FFI.

We have now found that mice expressing this mutant protein in their brains develop a disease very similar to familial fatal insomnia ( FFI), which is caused by a mutation at codon 178 of the prion protein gene.
We found that the two strains of prions differ in their effects on protein trafficking within cells, which may explain some of the differences between these diseases.

We show that, in addition to the well-known role of color in social communication, humans also use facial skin color to infer emotional state.

The authors have developed a new grading system for eyelid contour abnormality after blepharoplasty.
The authors have developed a grading system to classify the severity of these deformities, which is based on the degree of asymmetry and the extent of lid malposition.
The results of this study show that the new graders were able to achieve high levels of agreement with the senior graders in all three categories.
The grading system is easy to use, reproducible, and has good inter-rater reliability.

We show here that many proteins with diverse functions contain APH domains, which are conserved in evolution.
Our findings reveal an unexpected function of APH domains in maintaining membrane curvature and suggest that they may be involved in other cellular processes where membrane curvature is important.

We show that the binding affinity and dissociation rate of these proteins are dramatically affected by the lipid composition of the membrane.
We show here that ER stress is a novel mechanism for the induction of neuropathic pain in diabetic mice.
We show here that the endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) stress response is activated in the peripheral nervous system of type I diabetic rats, and that this response is associated with axonal degeneration.
This study provides insight into the mechanisms by which endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) stress contributes to chronic pain, opening new avenues for treatment.
HCV translation is initiated from an internal ribosome entry site ( IRES).
The m^6A modification is found on both messenger RNAs and small non-coding RNAs, including microRNAs and transfer RNAs.
Hepatitis C virus ( HCV) infection is a major cause of liver disease worldwide.

We show here that the m^6A reader protein YTHDC2 is required for efficient HCV IRES-mediated translation by binding to m^6A at position 331 of the IRES and recruiting the cellular La antigen to facilitate IRES conformational rearrangement.
Insecticide resistance in insects is often caused by overexpression of specific cytochrome P450 enzymes, which metabolize insecticides and render them ineffective.
Insecticide resistance in mosquitoes is often associated with increased expression of cytochrome P450 enzymes, but how this occurs is not known.
The CYP321A8 gene is responsible for resistance to chlorpyrifos, cypermethrin, and deltamethrin in mosquitoes.
The action potential ( AP) is initiated by an electrical signal in cardiac cells and triggers contraction of the heart muscle.
In this study we use mathematical modeling to investigate how the strength of coupling between L-type calcium channels affects cardiac function.
In this study, we show that the degree of coupling between L-type calcium channels is an important determinant of cardiac action potential duration.

We show that the coupling between calcium release from the sarcoplasmic reticulum and membrane potential is stronger in the presence of cooperativity, which may explain why it is harder to observe this phenomenon experimentally.
The visual system is organized in a hierarchical manner, with each stage processing increasingly abstract representations of visual information.
However, it has been unclear to what extent visual information is represented in the human brain.
This study demonstrates that visual information is not only processed within the primary visual cortex but also in other brain areas such as the default mode network ( DMN) and the hippocampus.
The use of islets from nonhuman primates as an alternative to human islets for clinical trials has been hampered by the lack of a suitable immunosuppressive regimen.
The encapsulation of pancreatic islets in macroscopic capsules, or macroencapsulated devices ( MEDs), has been proposed as an alternative approach for transplantation.

The method is demonstrated on a set of images of neuronal dendrites, which we use as input to generate a mesh that is used in finite element simulations.

We show that Sec10 is essential for PKD2 function in vivo by demonstrating that Sec10 knockdown recapitulates many of the same phenotypes observed with PKD2 knockdown.
We show that Sec10 is required for ciliogenesis in vivo and that it interacts with other cilia proteins to regulate ciliogenesis.
We show that the exocyst complex is required for the proper localization of polycystin-2 to the cilium and that this localization is important for normal cilia structure and function.
The aim of this review was to find out whether removing PICC lines early reduces the risk of infection, compared with leaving them in place for longer periods of time.
This review found no evidence from randomised controlled trials to support the routine use of early removal of PICC lines, as there were not enough data on the effects of this practice.
The review found there is not enough evidence from trials to determine whether or not it is better to remove a PICC line early (within one week) or later (after two weeks).
This study aimed to find out whether or not there was enough evidence from existing research to answer this question, and if so, what the results of these studies were.

The illegal wildlife trade is a major threat to biodiversity and human health worldwide.
The magnitude of illegal harvesting is unknown in most species, making it difficult to assess the impact on biodiversity and to develop effective management strategies.
We use this method to estimate elephant poaching mortality rates, which are used as an indicator of illegal hunting pressure, for African savannah elephants across Africa.
We found that elephant poaching in this area increased dramatically after 2008, coinciding with an increase in the local ivory price and seizures of ivory shipments to China.
We found that elephant poaching was unsustainable for African savannah elephants between 2010 and 2012, with an estimated 40, 000 elephants killed annually during this period.
We use mathematical modeling to explore how different vaccine characteristics, such as efficacy and coverage, affect the potential for vaccination to reduce disease burden and eliminate infection.

We show that the model is able to predict the observed patterns of variation in the abundance of different species, and we use it to make predictions about how these patterns will change under climate warming.
The results suggest that a vaccine-based control program would require a high level of effectiveness, early vaccination of potential carriers, and multiple rounds of mass vaccination.

We found that close proximity to other people with cholera or diarrhea is associated with increased risk of infection, but we were unable to determine whether this was due to direct transmission between individuals or because of shared environmental exposures.
However, few studies have examined how these factors vary in space and time to determine whether they are responsible for clustering of disease cases.

We found that people who had been exposed to certain risk factors were more likely to have symptoms of diarrhea, but we did not find evidence that these risk factors increased the likelihood of having cholera.
We found that people living near each other are more likely to be exposed to the same risk factors, suggesting that targeted interventions may be effective.
Infectious diseases often fluctuate significantly in prevalence, and these fluctuations have important consequences for disease control efforts.

This work provides new insights into how pathogens interact with their hosts and how they evolve to evade host immunity.

The regulation of nitrogen fixation by nitrogenases is an important area of research because it has implications for our understanding of how microorganisms sense and respond to their environment.
The model cyanobacterium Anabaena vinelandii is an ideal system to study this process because it contains multiple nitrogenase systems with different properties.
We have constructed an artificial nitrogenase system in E. coli by combining the anf gene products with those from N2-fixing bacteria.
The ability to produce hydrogen gas from N2 is a key step toward the development of sustainable energy production systems.
The O-GlcNAcylation of proteins is an important posttranslational modification that has been implicated in many cellular processes.
We have developed a method to quantify the dynamic changes of protein O-GlcNAcylation by using a combination of metabolic pulse-chase labeling with an O-GlcNAc chemical reporter and SILAC.

Screening for active TB in people who have been in close contact with someone with TB Background Tuberculosis (TB) is an infectious bacterial disease caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis.
Active case finding is when health workers actively look for cases in people who have been in contact with someone with TB, rather than waiting for them to come forward themselves.
The review found no evidence from randomized controlled trials to support the use of active case finding in contacts of people with TB.

We have developed a novel nanotechnology-based drug delivery system to treat nonmelanoma skin cancer.
We demonstrate that the addition of nanoparticles to the drug formulation enhances the therapeutic efficacy of the drug by increasing its accumulation in tumors.

The ability to deliver therapeutic proteins directly to solid tumors may provide an effective means to treat cancer by reducing systemic side effects associated with traditional chemotherapy and radiation therapy.
These findings demonstrate that nanoparticles represent an effective and safe method to deliver chemotherapeutics directly to tumors in vivo.
Pain relief for ureteral stones Review question We reviewed the evidence on pain-relieving treatments for ureteral stones.
The two main treatments for kidney stones in the ureters are extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy (ESWL) and ureteroscopy.
The review found that both treatments are effective and safe, but there is not enough information to be sure which one is better.
ESWL is an alternative treatment for kidney stones, which uses shock waves to break up the stone and then the fragments are passed out of the body through urine.

The review found that there is insufficient evidence to support routine use of amniotomy for induction of labour as it does not appear to reduce adverse outcomes such as meconium aspiration syndrome, perinatal death or severe morbidity, nor does it improve the outcome of the baby.

The review found that amniotic fluid infusion may be beneficial to babies when there is no access to ultrasound or other monitoring equipment, but it does not appear to improve outcomes in settings where such equipment is available.
There is not enough information about the risks and benefits of amnioinfusion for women with severe preterm labour, so more research is needed.

We show here that the binding of cholesterol to the N-terminal domain ( NTD) of the protein is important for its function, and that it also affects the conformational flexibility of the NTD.

Liver X receptors ( LXRs) are transcription factors that regulate genes involved in cholesterol homeostasis, including those involved in cholesterol uptake from the diet, transport within cells, and excretion.
The liver X receptor ( LXR) family of nuclear receptors is known to regulate cholesterol, bile acid, and lipid metabolism.
In this study, we show that LXRs play an important role in controlling the host response to the protozoan parasite Leishmania infantum, which causes visceral leishmaniasis ( VL).
We found that LXR activation by synthetic or natural ligands suppressed the production of proinflammatory mediators and cytokines in infected cells.
Our study provides new insights into the role of host lipid metabolism in controlling infection with an important human pathogen and identifies a novel target for anti-leishmanial therapy.

The initiation of cardiac arrhythmias is often attributed to abnormal electrical activity in the form of after-depolarizations, which are triggered by calcium release from intracellular stores.

In this study, we show that the arrhythmogenicity of the cardiac myocyte is increased in conditions where the intracellular calcium concentration is high and the cell membrane potential is depolarized.
We find that, in contrast to previous studies, heterogeneous populations can be robustly synchronized even when they contain a significant fraction of non-rhythmic cells.
We find that arrhythmogenicity is enhanced in regions of the tissue with fewer neighboring cells and that this effect is due to reduced coupling between cells.

The spike protein is responsible for binding to host cell surface receptors, and its receptor-binding domain ( RBD) has been used as a target for many vaccine approaches.
We have developed a new SARS-CoV-2 receptor-binding domain ( RBD) variant with improved manufacturability, affinity for human ACE2, and immunogenicity.
The antibodies elicited by this vaccine candidate bind to the receptor-binding domain of the spike protein and are able to neutralize the virus in vitro, including several variants of concern.
In this study we have investigated how the yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae responds to changes in glucose availability using a combination of genetic, biochemical, and computational approaches.
We have used genome-wide approaches to identify genes involved in the regulation of glucose and xylan metabolism in the filamentous fungus Aspergillus nidulans.

We show here that the yeast Saccharomyces cerevisiae uses two distinct mechanisms to control glucose and galactose metabolism, which are regulated by protein kinase C ( PKC) -dependent phosphorylation events.
The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the need for rapid, sensitive, and specific tests for detecting antibodies against SARS-CoV-2 in patients' sera.
In this study, we have compared the performance of several different types of tests for detecting antibodies to SARS-CoV-2 in blood samples from patients with COVID-19.
We compared ten commercial tests with our own laboratory-developed assays using a set of 160 samples from COVID-19 patients and healthy controls.
We found that the sensitivity of these tests was highly dependent on the timing of blood collection after symptom onset and that some tests were better suited to certain time points than others.
We found that some of the patients who had no detectable antibodies at all were those with the mildest symptoms and shortest duration of illness.
The results presented here provide an important step towards the evaluation of rapid diagnostic tests for COVID-19, which is critical to support public health efforts to control the pandemic.

In this study we show that the transcription factors URE3-BP and KRiP1 are required for the expression of genes involved in the production of extracellular vesicles.

The human extracellular calcium-sensing receptor ( CaSR) is an important regulator of calcium homeostasis in many tissues, including bone, kidney, parathyroid gland, and intestine.
The results presented here demonstrate that allosteric modulators of the CaSR are effective in correcting hypercalcemia and hypocalcemia, two major disorders of calcium homeostasis.

Asthma is a common chronic disease of the airways, which causes wheezing, cough, shortness of breath and chest tightness.

The use of electronic devices, such as mobile phones or computers connected to the internet, allows people with asthma to monitor their own lung function and symptoms from home.
The patient can also use the app to track their symptoms and medication adherence, which is sent to their doctor for review.
This review found that there is not enough evidence to show whether or not using telemonitoring improves health outcomes for people with COPD.

Key results The evidence was insufficient to determine if telemonitoring reduces the number of people who have an asthma attack requiring treatment with oral steroids, visits to the emergency department or hospital admissions.
Quality of evidence The quality of evidence was generally low or very low, mainly due to small numbers of participants and studies, poor reporting of study methods, and imprecision in results.

This study reveals how the activity of these proteins is coordinated with the process of cell division, and may provide insights into how other proteins are regulated in this way.
The bacterium Campylobacter jejuni is a leading cause of food-borne disease in humans and animals worldwide.
These findings suggest that Campylobacter jejuni has evolved mechanisms to survive and replicate in macrophages.
Inhibitor of apoptosis protein-1 ( cIAP-1), which is expressed in HBV-infected hepatocytes, has been implicated as an important factor for viral persistence.
This study provides evidence for a mechanism by which HIV-1-infected cells are eliminated from the body and suggests that drugs that target this pathway may have potential as antiviral agents.
We have shown in this study that birinapant, a small molecule IAP inhibitor, is able to clear HBV from infected cells by inducing apoptosis of these cells.
Birinapant is currently being tested in clinical trials for cancer treatment; our study suggests it also has potential as an antiviral drug.
The use of passive immunization with broadly neutralizing antibodies ( bNAbs) has been proposed as a potential approach to achieve long-term control of HIV-1 infection in infected individuals.

In this study we show that in rhesus macaques treated with broadly neutralizing antibodies ( bNAbs), the time to viral rebound after treatment interruption was significantly longer than in untreated controls and that bNAb therapy prolonged the duration of viral suppression when given concurrently with ART.
However, in clinical trials of chronically infected individuals, this antibody did not have substantial effects on viral load.
This study provides insight into how antibodies fail to neutralize HIV-1, which may help improve antibody-based HIV-1 prevention strategies.
The immune system is constantly exposed to pathogens, toxins, and other foreign substances in our body.
Plasmacytoid dendritic cells ( pDCs) are innate immune cells that play a key role in antiviral immunity and autoimmunity.

We have used camelid single-domain antibodies ( nanobodies) to target the lactose permease in its outward-open state and found that they bind at the same site on the protein as the inhibitor Hg^2+, which is known to block transport by binding to this site.
We have determined the crystal structure of the complex formed by the binding of an antiporter inhibitor, Nb9047, to the lactose permease ( LacY) from Escherichia coli.

The sulfur isotope composition of atmospheric sulfate has been used as an indicator of atmospheric chemistry, but this approach requires knowledge of the fractionation factors for the oxidation of reduced sulfur compounds in the atmosphere.
The sulfur isotope composition of ancient sulfates has been used to reconstruct atmospheric conditions on Earth during the Archean Eon ( 2.
The results provide new insights into the evolution of the Earth's surface environment, including the early oceans and atmosphere, during the Archean Eon ( 2.
The sulfur isotopic composition of sulfate in the atmosphere has been used as an indicator of atmospheric oxidation processes, but this approach has not yet been applied to ancient atmospheres.

We show that the sulfur isotope composition of barite from the sedimentary record has been used to reconstruct past atmospheric oxygen levels, but this approach is not reliable because it does not account for the effect of variable sulfur isotopic compositions in the source rocks.

Human-induced pluripotent stem cells ( hiPSCs) have been used to generate cardiomyocytes for drug testing, but there is still a need for more robust methods to assess their quality and relevance to clinical disease models.
The ability to predict whether an ion channel blocker will cause Torsades de Pointes ( TdP) would be of great value.
We have developed a computational approach that combines experimental data with molecular dynamics simulations to predict whether drugs will block or modulate cardiac ion channels.
We propose an efficient algorithm for this task, which is based on the construction of a dictionary of patterns in the data, followed by a greedy search for the best combination of these patterns.
We also developed a computational model to identify which ion channel types are involved in drug-induced arrhythmias.
The acquisition of new genetic information by horizontal gene transfer is an important mechanism for bacteria to adapt to changing environments.

We show that although the evolutionary distances between homologous enzymes are not predictive of their ability to support growth, the product of enzyme abundance and catalytic efficiency is highly correlated with growth rate.
We found that the most fit mutants had mutations that increased the amount of dihydrofolate reductase ( DHFR) protein inside the cell.
This mechanism may be important for preventing the deleterious effects of foreign gene expression in cells.
The regulation of gene expression is controlled at multiple levels, including post-transcriptional processing of messenger RNA ( mRNA).
In this study, we have identified a novel function for the Arabidopsis thaliana protein FPA in regulating alternative poly( A) site selection.
Here, we use direct RNA sequencing to show that the FPA protein is required for efficient termination of transcription at many genes.
We have found that in the absence of FPA, many different types of RNA molecules are produced by combining parts from different genes.
The HIV-1 capsid protein is known to be targeted by several cellular restriction factors including Fv1 and TRIM5a proteins.

The mechanism by which TRIM5a recognizes viral capsids has remained elusive, however, due to the lack of structural information on the complex between TRIM5a and its cognate retroviral capsid protein.
We have used X-ray crystallography to determine the structures of two mutant forms of the enzyme, one of which is resistant to restriction and one of which is not.
Our results suggest that the entire surface of the capsid is important for interaction with TRIM5a and that the interaction between these two proteins is likely to be highly dynamic.
The endothelium is covered by an endovascular layer called the glycocalyx, which is composed of proteoglycans and glycoproteins.
We found that heparansulfate is rapidly removed from the blood stream in sepsis and accumulates in the brain, where it may affect neuronal function.
Polycystic ovary syndrome ( PCOS) is a common endocrine disorder in women of reproductive age.
We found that neuron-specific deletion of androgen receptor ( AR) in mice resulted in abnormal ovarian function, polycystic ovaries, and metabolic dysfunction.
Our study provides new insights into the mechanisms by which androgens regulate ovarian function, and may lead to novel therapeutic strategies for PCOS.
These findings suggest that androgen receptor ( AR) signaling plays an important role in the pathogenesis of PCOS and may be a potential therapeutic target for this disease.
We have previously shown that T2DM exacerbates Mtb infection in mice.
We found that IL-22 is required for the development of hyperglycemia, dyslipidemia, and fatty liver in mice fed with high-fat diet.

These results provide new insights into the role of IL-22 in the pathogenesis of tuberculosis and diabetes mellitus, and highlight the potential of targeting this cytokine as a treatment strategy to improve outcomes in co-endemic areas.
The fossil record is sparse for the earliest radiation of modern birds ( Neoaves), making it difficult to reconstruct their evolutionary history.
We describe a new species of enantiornithine bird from the Early Cretaceous of North America and show that it is one of the earliest known members of this group.
We also show that the earliest known bird fossils are not as old as previously thought and that the first modern birds evolved rapidly in morphology and ecology.
SARS-CoV-2 is evolving rapidly, and new variants have been reported in multiple countries.
The rapid emergence of new viral variants during acute infection has been observed in other viruses, but it is not clear whether this process occurs with SARS-CoV-2.
We found that HIV-1 populations in each individual host have relatively little genetic diversity, suggesting that only a small number of viruses establish infection in each person.

We found that most of the genetic diversity generated by HIV-1 replication in an individual host is lost when the virus is transmitted to another host, suggesting that this diversity is not important for viral fitness and may be detrimental to the virus.
Jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes) is a common symptom in many infections, including malaria, hepatitis A virus infection, and typhoid fever.
Yellow fever is endemic in the Democratic Republic of the Congo, and it has been reported to be responsible for most cases of acute viral hepatitis in this country.
The majority of these cases ( 95%) do not have evidence of yellow fever virus infection but rather of other pathogens such as dengue or chikungunya viruses.
Leptospira are bacteria that infect humans and animals worldwide causing disease in both species.
We found that 1% of patients with suspected yellow fever had antibodies against Leptospira, suggesting that leptospirosis is present in the DRC.

We show here that PIAS1 and PIAS4 are required for efficient transcriptional elongation by RNA polymerase II ( RNAP2) through their ability to regulate the sumoylation status of PCNA, which is essential for RNAP2 processivity.
We also show that SUMOylation of PCNA is required for efficient translesion synthesis, and that this process is dependent on PIAS1 and PIAS4.
We find that genes involved in early reproductive development are under strong selection to change rapidly during evolution.

Our results suggest that the evolutionary history of Bam is closely tied to the coevolutionary dynamics between Drosophila and Wolbachia, which may have led to the rapid adaptation of Bam to its current function as an essential protein for Wolbachia infection.
We propose that this is due to the fact that the germ cells are exposed to pathogens and thus have evolved mechanisms to protect themselves from infection.

This review found that non-drug interventions such as relaxation, distraction, hypnosis, music therapy, massage, aromatherapy, acupuncture, and cognitive behavioural therapy (CBT) are effective in reducing anxiety and improving cooperation during venepuncture.

Non-drug methods to help reduce anxiety and improve cooperation in children undergoing general anaesthesia Review question We wanted to find out if there were any ways to help children feel less anxious and more cooperative when they are having an operation under general anaesthetic.
The results showed that children who had their parents present were no less anxious than those who did not have their parents present.
We use atmospheric measurements to estimate national emissions of CCl4, which is an important ozone-depleting substance.
We find that emissions from oil and gas operations in the Marcellus Shale are significantly higher than those reported by the U. S. Environmental Protection Agency's Toxics Release Inventory.
We find that Texas and Louisiana are the largest emitters in the United States, with combined emissions of ~1.

The post-translational modification of proteins with small ubiquitin-like modifier ( SUMO) is a common feature of many cellular processes and has been shown to be hijacked by several viruses to control their life cycle.
We have identified several EBV proteins that affect SUMOylation in B lymphocytes, including the viral protein vBHRF1, which is required for efficient virus replication.
We show here that all three viruses encode proteins with SUMO E3 ligase activity, which is required for efficient virus replication.

The review found that there was insufficient evidence from randomised controlled trials to determine which corticosteroid should be used for preventing respiratory distress syndrome in preterm babies.
Study characteristics We searched for studies up to March 2015.
The review of 12 studies found that giving vitamin C supplements to pregnant women reduced the risk of preterm birth by about one-third.
The review found that dexamethasone may reduce the risk of bleeding in the brain (intraventricular haemorrhage) and shorten the time babies spend in hospital.
The review found that there is not enough evidence to show whether giving steroids for 12 hours or 24 hours is better.
The review found no evidence to support the use of any particular type of treatment, but there was some evidence that early intervention may be beneficial.
More research is needed to determine which treatments work best for people with this condition.
Depression is a serious mental health problem, which affects many young people.
The review found that there is some evidence to suggest that newer antidepressant drugs may be more effective than older ones in reducing symptoms of depression and anxiety.
The studies did not include people who were more severely ill or had other mental health problems, so it is unclear how well these results would apply to them.
The review found that there is some evidence that antidepressants may be effective in reducing pain, but this effect is small and not consistent across all studies.
The quality of evidence was low for all outcomes due to problems with study design, small numbers of participants, and poor reporting of methods used.
The studies were small in size and did not provide information on how well the results could be applied to other people with similar characteristics.
The evolution of resistance to drugs is an important problem for public health and medicine.
We propose a method to visualize and compare these properties in a single image.
We show that this is not always true, however, and that in some cases, using multiple colors can actually reduce the effect of background heterogeneity on signal detection.

The long-term ecological record provides a unique opportunity to study how humans have altered ecosystems, but it is often difficult to determine whether observed changes are due to natural or anthropogenic causes.
We use ancient DNA from human remains found in the Canary Islands and Cabo Verde to reconstruct past human population dynamics and migration patterns.
The Canary Islands are an archipelago in the eastern Atlantic Ocean where human colonization has led to widespread deforestation and introduction of nonnative plant species.

These results highlight the importance of long-term monitoring to assess the impact of human activities on island ecosystems and provide insights into how to restore degraded forests in these unique biodiversity hotspots.
Habituation is a fundamental learning process by which organisms learn to ignore repetitive stimuli and focus on novel ones.
We show how this mechanism is sufficient to explain habituation of the fruit fly's response to odors.
This model provides an explanation for how animals can detect subtle differences between smells that are otherwise indistinguishable from one another.
We show that the same computational principles that underlie habituation in neurons also apply to human olfactory perception.
The mechanism by which chemical reactions enhance permeability is still unclear.
We show here that volume-increasing chemical reactions can enhance permeability in confined systems.
We show that both range shifts and range expansions lead to similar levels of genetic diversity loss, but that range shifts are more likely to result in reduced fitness than range expansions.

We show that the speed at which an environment changes affects how fast adaptation occurs, and we identify conditions under which rapid environmental change is more likely to lead to extinction than slow change.
We show that this is not always true, however, as we find that in some cases the population will evolve to be less fit than it was initially.
We show that range contractions are more likely to be associated with low genetic diversity than range expansions or no change in range size.
This study provides evidence that climate change is causing rapid changes in the distribution of a widespread, ecologically important species.
The plant hormone auxin plays an essential role in many aspects of plant growth and development, including cell elongation, cell division, differentiation, and organogenesis.
The model plant Arabidopsis thaliana is widely used to study the molecular mechanisms of plant growth and development.
The mechanism by which auxin polar transport is regulated by NPA remains unclear.
Our findings provide a simple mechanistic model for how NPA inhibits polar auxin transport and explain many previously puzzling observations about the action of NPA in plants.
Transcription factor binding is an important step in gene regulation, but it is not clear how the presence or absence of specific TFs affects the overall accessibility of the genome for TF binding.
The mechanism by which chromatin remodeling occurs during transcription initiation is not well understood.
We show that Z is required for the formation of a specific type of chromatin structure in which nucleosomes are positioned at regular intervals along the DNA.
We propose that this mechanism is a general feature of eukaryotic gene expression and may be used to regulate genes in other organisms.

The accumulation of hyperphosphorylated a-synuclein in Lewy bodies and Lewy neurites is a hallmark of Parkinson's disease ( PD) and dementia with Lewy bodies ( DLB), but the mechanism by which this occurs remains unclear.
We found that the catalytic subunit of PP2A was hypomethylated in PD brains, which impaired its ability to dephosphorylate a-synuclein.
We also show that the combination of EHT and caffeine is more effective than either drug alone at protecting neurons from oxidative stress-induced cell death.
We show that InPCA is able to reveal subtle patterns in data that are not apparent with standard PCA methods.
The mitotic checkpoint ensures accurate chromosome segregation by delaying cell division until all chromosomes are properly attached to microtubules of the spindle apparatus.
The mechanism of how this checkpoint control is achieved has been elusive.
We found that the chaperonin CCT/TRiC, which is involved in protein folding, binds to MCC and releases Cdc20.
We show here that the combined action of two proteins, CCT and TRIP13, is required to completely dismantle the mitotic checkpoint complex ( MCC) and restore cell cycle progression.
The MerR family of metalloregulators is an important class of transcription factors that control gene expression by binding to specific DNA sequences near target genes.
However, it is not known how the binding of these regulators affects RNAP activity and how this effect is transmitted through the protein-DNA interface.
However, our understanding of how these factors interact is limited by the scarcity of long-term observations of soil erosion in natural settings.
We reconstructed sedimentation rates for 632 lakes and found that sedimentation rates have increased significantly since the end of the last ice age ( ~12, 000 years ago).
We find that many areas experienced an increase in soil erosion during this time period, consistent with deforestation.
Typhoid fever is an important cause of morbidity and mortality in low- and middle-income countries, particularly in South Asia.

The Jervell and Lange-Nielsen syndrome ( JLNS) is an inherited disorder characterized by a combination of congenital long QT interval on electrocardiogram, syncope, and sudden death due to ventricular fibrillation.
Here we generated human induced pluripotent stem cells ( hiPSCs) from patients with JLNS carrying either a splice site or a missense mutation in KCNQ1.
We found that hiPSC-CMs from patients with JLNS exhibited abnormal electrophysiology consistent with the disease phenotype, including prolonged action potentials and reduced repolarization currents.
We found that JLNS cardiomyocytes are more sensitive to arrhythmogenic drugs and that this increased sensitivity is due to altered calcium handling.
Visceral leishmaniasis is a neglected tropical disease endemic to the Indian subcontinent, where it causes significant morbidity and mortality.
The current gold standard for diagnosis of visceral leishmaniasis ( VL) is based on microscopic examination of splenic aspirate or bone marrow biopsy samples.

In this study, we have evaluated the performance of a simple, rapid and cost effective molecular diagnostic test ( SYBR Green I based closed tube loop mediated isothermal amplification) for detection of Leishmania donovani in blood samples from patients with visceral leishmaniasis.
In this study we have evaluated the performance of these tests using samples collected from patients with suspected VL cases at two different endemic sites in India.
Cohesin is a conserved protein complex that mediates the cohesion between sister chromatids during cell division.
The mechanism by which cohesin tethers sister chromatids together has been unclear, however.
We have generated a collection of mutations in the SMC1 gene that disrupt cohesin function.
We have identified two domains in Smc1 that are required for this interaction.
Our results suggest that cohesins bind to both sister chromatids in a sequential manner, with one event being regulated by Ctf7 and the other by CAR.
The new scheme is based on seven genes, which are all found in all Leptospira species and have been shown to be highly variable between strains.
The new scheme is more accurate than the previous one, but it does not improve the ability to distinguish between species.
We found that all isolates from each species formed distinct groups in phylogenetic analyses, indicating that they are genetically different.
This study provides a comprehensive resource for the identification of Leptospira strains isolated from humans and animals worldwide.
The molecular mechanisms by which Ca2+ regulates neurotransmitter release are not fully understood.
The uterus is highly responsive to glucocorticoids, which act through binding to the glucocorticoid receptor ( GR) to regulate gene expression.
We have used a genetically engineered mouse to study the role of glucocorticoids in uterine function and found that they are essential for maintaining uterine homeostasis.
We show that the absence of glucocorticoids in the uterus results in a severe defect in implantation and embryonic development.
We found that mice lacking glucocorticoid receptor ( GR) function in the uterus had an exaggerated inflammatory response during pregnancy and were unable to maintain normal levels of progesterone.

This study demonstrates that glucocorticoids are essential for establishing the proper uterine environment for implantation, which may be important in understanding the etiology of infertility associated with preterm birth.
We found that microglia are essential for controlling virus replication in the yolk sac, and that their absence results in increased virus levels in the developing brain.
These findings suggest that microglia play an important role in Zika virus pathogenesis during pregnancy and may be a potential target for therapeutic intervention.
However, most quantitative genetic studies have focused on the average effect of genes on traits rather than their effect on individual-to-individual variation in those traits.
We studied the genetic basis for this variability by using genetically identical fruit fly larvae raised under identical conditions and then measuring their locomotor behavior as adults.
We show that the same genotype can produce a wide range of phenotypes, and that some genetic backgrounds are more prone to producing variable phenotypes than others.
However, these studies have focused on identifying genetic variants associated with average levels of disease risk or trait values rather than the variability in those traits.
We believe our work will have significant impact on many applications of machine learning and statistics, such as privacy-preserving data sharing in healthcare, finance, and social networks.
The DSB repair pathway known as non-homologous end joining ( NHEJ) uses a protein called DNA ligase IV to seal the broken ends.
The role of Dnl4 in DSB repair has not been fully elucidated due to the lack of an animal model with a mutation at the Dnl4 locus.
Histone variants are important regulators of gene expression, DNA repair and replication.
We have identified a novel variant of the Lamin B Receptor protein, which is expressed in testes and is associated with RNA processing factors and RNA polymerase II.
We found that Z is required for efficient splicing of exons in the testes and brain, but not in other tissues.
The interaction between proteins is an important part of many biological processes, such as signal transduction and protein degradation.
Intrinsically disordered proteins ( IDPs) are known to play important roles in many biological processes, but their functions are not well understood.
We show that the binding of the protein to DNA is an induced fit process, where the protein undergoes significant conformational changes upon binding to DNA.
The structure of the complex reveals how the protein-protein interface is formed by transient nonspecific interactions, which are mediated by both hydrophobic and electrostatic forces.
The results provide insight into how SH3 domains interact with their ligands, which is important to understanding the function of these proteins.
We show that, over the past 715 y, the oldest living boreal forest trees have increased their water-use efficiency by increasing photosynthesis while maintaining constant stomatal conductance.
We found that elevated CO2 stimulated photosynthesis and water use efficiency but did not increase biomass production due to reduced leaf area per unit of plant mass.
The liver sinusoidal endothelial cells ( LSECs) are specialized endothelial cells of the liver sinusoids and play an important role in maintaining the blood-liver interface.

Prostaglandin E1 for improving circulation in infants with congenital heart disease Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether prostaglandin E1 (PGE1) improves circulation in babies with congenital heart disease before they undergo balloon atrial septostomy or cardiac surgery.
This review found no evidence to support the use of prostaglandin E1 for infants with ductal-dependent congenital heart disease.
The review found that there is insufficient evidence to support the use of prostaglandin E1 for more than 24 hours, as it may increase the risk of bleeding.
Study characteristics We searched the literature up to 20th February 2015, and found no randomised controlled trials comparing these two treatments.
The lung parenchyma consists of airspaces (alveoli) surrounded by capillaries, which are separated from each other by thin walls.
We found that Notch2 is required for the proper development of the lung airway epithelium and smooth muscle.
The ability to predict which patients are at high risk of dying from COVID-19 is crucial for prioritizing care and resource allocation.
However, most existing studies have focused on predicting mortality using only static data collected at admission.
We show that the combination of these two types of data can improve the accuracy of predictive models for COVID-19 disease severity and mortality.
We show that these biomarkers are predictive of mortality and can be used to predict disease severity at different stages of illness.
We have identified a novel protein, called cystatin C, which is secreted by the epithelium of the epididymis and forms an amyloid-like structure within the lumen of the epididymis.
This work provides new insights into how proteins assemble into functional amyloids, which is important for understanding their biological function in vivo.
The high genetic diversity within each individual host is thought to be driven by purifying selection, which acts on the virus population in order to maintain its fitness.
The study of the evolution of viruses within hosts ( intra-host evolution) has provided important insights on how viruses adapt to their host environment.
The epidemiology of dengue virus ( DENV) in Taiwan is characterized by frequent importation of viruses from other countries and subsequent local transmission.
We found that group Ib variants were more likely to be maintained in the host than other groups, suggesting they may have an advantage for long-term persistence within hosts.
The results also suggest that the virus evolved rapidly during the first year of the outbreak, with new variants emerging at different times and locations.
Our results provide new insights into the dynamics of the ongoing Ebola outbreak and highlight the importance of considering spatial heterogeneity when studying disease outbreaks.
This study demonstrates the utility of combining phylogenetic and deep sequencing approaches to identify and track specific viral lineages during an outbreak.
We have used RNA interference to identify several host factors that are required for efficient replication of human cytomegalovirus ( HCMV) in cultured human cells.
We found that blocking the receptor with antibodies or small molecules significantly reduced virus replication in vitro and in vivo.
Our results suggest that ERa plays an important role in supporting CMV infection by regulating cellular metabolism.
The results suggest that changes in DNA methylation may be an important mechanism for the regulation of gene expression during inflammation and potentially contribute to disease pathogenesis.
The identification of such biomarkers has been challenging due to the complexity and heterogeneity of disease phenotypes.
The authors have used genome-wide DNA methylation analysis to identify genes whose expression is associated with seasonal allergic rhinitis, a common disease caused by allergens such as pollen.
We found that DNA methylation patterns were significantly different between SAR patients and healthy individuals, both in the presence or absence of allergen exposure.
We found no evidence for differences in gene expression between patients and controls.
We found that the proportion of different types of immune cells differed between patients and healthy individuals, and this was associated with differences in DNA methylation.

The ability to selectively target protein-protein interactions has been a major goal of drug discovery efforts, but it is still challenging to identify small molecules that specifically disrupt these interactions.
We have used molecular dynamics simulations to generate an ensemble of conformations of Bcl-2 family proteins and identified potential drug-binding sites on these proteins.

This study provides insight into how subtle changes in protein structure and dynamics can be exploited for selective inhibition of protein-protein interactions, which is an important step toward the development of more effective therapeutics.
The protein a-synuclein is found in these aggregates and forms fibrils that are toxic to neurons.

We show here that the ubiquitination of alpha-synuclein in Lewy bodies is predominantly through K63-linked polyubiquitin chains, which are also present in other neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's disease ( AD) and Parkinson's disease ( PD).
We found that the levels of ubiquitin were reduced in Lewy body-containing neurons, which was associated with decreased activity of the deubiquitinating enzyme Usp8.

Our study provides new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying a-synuclein aggregation and suggests that Usp8 plays an important role in regulating a-synuclein homeostasis in dopaminergic neurons.
The activation of NFAT by calcium is mediated by the dephosphorylation of its inhibitory domain by the enzyme calcineurin.

We show that even a small-scale nuclear conflict in South Asia would inject massive amounts of black carbon aerosol into the stratosphere, which would cause significant cooling over the next few years.
We find that, although the initial loss of production is large, the system can recover quickly through the use of stored grain reserves and international trade.

The aim of this review was to find out whether or not there are any treatments that could help people with hip fractures recover more quickly, regain their independence sooner and reduce the risk of further falls.
Hip fractures are common in older adults and many people who suffer from them also have dementia or other problems with thinking and memory.
One trial found that people who received enhanced care had better quality of life, less depression and anxiety, and fewer hospital readmissions than those who did not receive enhanced care.
The studies showed that some of these interventions may reduce the risk of developing delirium, but there was not enough information to be sure about this.
The hepatitis C virus ( HCV) is an important human pathogen, but its life cycle has not been fully elucidated.
We show here that p7 is required for efficient viral RNA replication in the endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) and that it acts by promoting the localization of the viral genome to this organelle.
We found that the viral protein p7 is responsible for this difference in localization between HCV and JFH1-infected cells.
Influenza A viruses were more prevalent than influenza B viruses during the first half of the season, but then influenza B viruses became predominant.
In this study, we show that the seasonal patterns of influenza A and B are not explained by differences in their basic reproductive numbers ( R0) or by short-term immunity.
Our results suggest that the increased transmissibility of influenza B viruses is likely to be due to changes in the virus itself, rather than differences in host immunity.
Hantavirus infections are caused by viruses of the genus Hantavirus, family Bunyaviridae and have been reported to cause severe disease in humans worldwide.
The role of T cell responses in protection against HTNV infection is not well understood.
In this study, we found that CD4+T-cells play an important role in the pathogenesis of HFRS by inducing severe inflammation and tissue damage.
We found that the host defense is mediated by CD4+ T cells, which induce an antiviral state in infected cells and also kill virus-infected cells via granzymes.
The Carney complex ( CNC) is a rare genetic disorder characterized by multiple tumors and excessive hormone production.

The most common clinical presentation of this disorder is primary pigmented nodular adrenocortical disease ( PPNAD) which presents with symptoms of Cushing's syndrome, including weight gain and high blood pressure.
The most common cause of this disease is an overproduction of cortisol due to a tumor on the adrenal gland.
We show that R1a is required for proper development of the adrenal gland and for the maintenance of its structure during adulthood.
We show here that mice lacking R1a develop adrenal hyperplasia and increased steroid production, similar to what is seen in patients with PPNAD.
This study provides new insights into the pathogenesis of congenital hypercortisolism and may help in the development of novel therapeutic strategies for this disease.

Gefitinib for the treatment of non-small cell lung cancer Review question We reviewed the evidence about whether gefitinib, an oral drug used to treat non-small cell lung cancer, works and what side effects it causes.

This review found no evidence of benefit for any treatment in terms of reducing the number of people who died or were admitted to hospital, but there was some evidence that those treated with antibiotics had less pain and discomfort than those not given antibiotics.
Gefitinib is an oral drug that targets the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) tyrosine kinase enzyme.
Study characteristics The evidence is current up to 17 February 2017.
Study characteristics We found 35 studies with 12,089 participants.

Key results In general, gefitinib did not appear to have an effect on overall survival (the length of time after treatment started that the person is still alive) and progression-free survival (the length of time after treatment started that the disease does not get worse).
In addition, there was evidence that second-line treatment with gefitinib may prolong survival for people of Asian ethnicity.
The combination of gefitinib and chemotherapy was better than gefitinib alone for overall survival.
Maintenance treatment with gefitinib also reduced the risk of disease progression by about half.
Key results Gefitinib treatment after chemotherapy may improve overall survival and progression-free survival in patients with advanced NSCLC.
The review found that gefitinib is more effective than chemotherapy in terms of response rate and progression-free survival, but it does not improve overall survival.

Gefitinib compared to chemotherapy for advanced non-small cell lung cancer with an epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) mutation Review question We reviewed the evidence on whether gefitinib was better than chemotherapy for people with non-small cell lung cancer who have a specific genetic change called an epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) mutation.
Borna disease virus ( BDV) is an enigmatic neurotropic RNA virus that causes neurological disease in horses, sheep, rodents, and primates.
BDV infection also blocks the increase in synaptic vesicle recycling that occurs during long-term potentiation ( LTP), a cellular correlate of learning and memory.
We found that the blockade of neurotransmitter release is caused by reduced levels of phosphorylation of MARCKS and Munc18-1/nSec1, two key regulators of vesicle fusion.

The two diseases are characterized by impaired energy production in muscles due to different reasons: McArdle disease is caused by a defect in the breakdown of glycogen, whereas mitochondrial myopathies result from defects within the mitochondria itself.

We used metabolomics to identify changes in muscle metabolism during exercise in patients with myalgic encephalomyelitis/chronic fatigue syndrome ( ME/CFS), a debilitating disease characterized by persistent fatigue and muscle pain.
These findings provide new insights into the molecular mechanisms underlying mitochondrial dysfunction in human disease and may have important implications for the treatment of metabolic disorders.
Wolbachia are bacteria that live inside mosquitoes and other insects and have been shown to interfere with the ability of viruses such as dengue virus to replicate within their hosts.
We also found that the microRNA aae-miR-2940-5p may be involved in this process by regulating pelo expression.
We found that pelo is required for efficient dengue virus replication, and that it is suppressed in cells infected with Wolbachia.

The rate at which species are being lost from Earth's ecosystems has been increasing over recent decades and is predicted to continue to rise as human activities such as deforestation and urbanization expand.
We used a highly accurate method for estimating species richness, which is based on the number of individuals in each sample and their genetic diversity.
We find that about 40% of all species are missing from disturbed sites, but we also show that many of these species are not necessarily lost forever.
We show that local communities are more resilient than regional ones, and that this is due to the fact that local species composition is less affected by disturbances.

The autoxidation of unsaturated lipids is a major source of reactive oxygen species ( ROS) in living organisms and contributes to many diseases such as cancer, neurodegenerative disorders, and cardiovascular disease.
This work provides insight into the mechanism by which hydrocarbons are oxidized in the atmosphere, and how this process is affected by the presence of NOx.
We show here that OH radicals add to double bonds in unsaturated fatty acids, leading to the formation of Criegee intermediates.
The results suggest that chlorine atoms may be important for atmospheric chemistry and potentially other environments where they are present.

The results of this study suggest that in addition to preventive chemotherapy, other control strategies such as environmental management and health education should be implemented to reduce the prevalence of intestinal worm infections in children.

Our results demonstrate the power of combining experimental and computational approaches for systematic characterization of metabolic networks and their regulation.
The ability to measure multiple parameters simultaneously is an important feature of modern cytometers, but it also creates new challenges for data analysis and interpretation.
The recent advent of high-throughput, high-precision technologies such as flow and mass cytometry have enabled researchers to measure hundreds of proteins in individual cells simultaneously.
CytoPy is an open-source software package that provides a flexible and user-friendly interface to analyze cytometry data in a reproducible manner.

Study characteristics We searched scientific databases and trial registers to find randomized controlled trials (RCTs) comparing intensive disease management programs for people with COPD.
The studies lasted between three and 24 months.
The studies included participants from different age groups, with some studies including only children or older adults.
The results showed that participants who received the intervention were able to walk further than those who did not receive the intervention at 12 months.
The results showed that there were fewer people who had at least one admission to hospital within three to 12 months after receiving an intervention than those who did not receive an intervention.

The review of trials found no evidence that one method is better than another in terms of reducing death, but there were differences in other outcomes such as the number of people who had a relapse and the time it took for them to recover.

CAR T cell therapy has shown promising results in treating hematological malignancies but has been less successful in solid tumors.
However, there are no imaging tools available to track the distribution and persistence of CAR T cells in vivo.

The endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) is an organelle found in eukaryotic cells where most secreted and membrane-bound proteins are synthesized.
The localization of proteins to the endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) membrane is an important step in protein maturation and secretion.

We show here that some mRNAs are retained in the endoplasmic reticulum ( ER) for extended periods of time after their synthesis is complete, and that this retention is independent of protein synthesis.

We found no evidence for a causal link between gray matter volume and sexual arousal.

We found that MAIT cells from HIV-infected individuals had reduced numbers and function compared with healthy controls.
We found that the MAIT cells from these patients had reduced cytolytic activity, lower levels of activation markers, and altered gene expression profiles compared to healthy controls.

Genome-wide association studies ( GWAS) have identified many genetic variants associated with blood pressure.
We hypothesized that genetic variants could influence blood pressure through effects on proteins and their expression levels.
We identified 7 genes whose expression is regulated by rs3184504, a variant previously shown to be associated with blood pressure.
Inflammation plays an important role in the pathogenesis of severe HFMD and thus anti-inflammatory treatment has been suggested for severe HFMD.
In this study, we found that the levels of IL-6 and TNF-a were significantly higher in the serum of HFMD patients than those of healthy controls.
Our results suggest that the imbalance of immune response is associated with severe disease outcomes, which may contribute to the pathogenesis of HFMD.

The quality of evidence was low due to differences in study design, methods used for data collection and analysis, and the small number of participants involved in each trial.

The intestinal epithelium is constantly renewed by stem cells located in the crypts, which are small finger-like projections of the epithelium.
We show that this process is driven by an increase in cell proliferation and a decrease in apoptosis.

We found that cells with shared intermediate metabolites in their metabolic networks showed increased synergy, but also had more instances of antagonism as the number of species increased.

We show that amino acid biosynthetic pathways have evolved to be highly efficient and streamlined for each species, but also to be broadly compatible with other species' needs through cross-feeding interactions.

These variants are thought to affect gene expression by changing the binding of regulatory proteins to DNA.

Our findings provide new insights into how RNAP is organized in bacteria and how it may be regulated to control gene expression.

The length of time that people stay in hospital after being admitted to an acute psychiatric ward varies greatly between hospitals and countries.
The length of time that someone is admitted to hospital for treatment has been shown to have an impact on their recovery from mental illness.
This review found that there is not enough evidence to show whether or not shorter hospital stays are better than longer ones for people with severe mental illness.

The ability to record from multiple neurons simultaneously has opened up new avenues for studying how groups of neurons interact with each other and process information.

We propose a new model for the collective dynamics of neuronal populations in the brain, which is based on the idea that neurons are connected in a network with a global coupling strength near the critical point.

We find that the nonlinearities are consistent across all cell types, and that they can be described by a single parameter, which we interpret as a measure of the strength of global coupling between the network.
The effects of environmental experiences on gene expression are well documented, but whether these changes in gene expression are passed on to offspring is less clear.
The mechanism by which this information is transferred between generations is unknown.
This phenomenon has been called"RNA interference," or"RNAi."

This study demonstrates that environmental influences on behavior are transmitted through the transfer of RNA molecules from one generation to the next, providing evidence for a novel mechanism by which environmental factors may influence the development of subsequent generations.

Progesterone is a steroid hormone that acts by binding to its cognate nuclear receptor, PR, which then regulates gene expression.

In contrast to other mammals, Catarrhine primates exhibit unique reproductive traits such as spontaneous decidualization and menstruation, which suggest that progesterone receptor ( PR) function has been modified during primate evolution.
We find that PGR has undergone rapid evolution in humans and other great apes, but this is not due to positive selection for increased expression or function.
We show that the two main forms of progesterone receptor ( PR-A and PR-B) have different functions in humans compared to other mammals.
This study provides evidence for divergence between human and mouse progesterone receptor isoforms, which may impact our understanding of progesterone action in humans.
Cryptococcus neoformans is an opportunistic fungal pathogen of humans and animals.

The emergence and spread of antimicrobial resistance is a major public health concern.
In this study, we have developed and validated a mathematical model to identify asymptomatic carriers of MRSA in hospitals.
We use this model to estimate the probability of colonization for each patient in the hospital network.
We find that interventions targeting patients with a high colonization probability are more effective than those based on hospitalization history or contact tracing.

The diversity of frogs is greater than any other group of vertebrates, with over 6, 000 known species.
The evolutionary history of frogs is not well known, but recent studies have shown that they are one of the most diverse groups of vertebrates on Earth.
We find that three major groups of frogs--the tree frogs, microhylids, and natatanurans--all experienced rapid radiation in the aftermath of the K-Pg mass extinction.
Our findings provide evidence for an important role of the Cretaceous-Paleogene ( K-Pg) mass extinction in shaping the diversity and distribution of modern frogs.

We show that ESCRT machinery proteins are required for efficient viral RNA synthesis and virion assembly.

Rheumatoid arthritis ( RA) is an autoimmune disease characterised by inflammation in the joints.
The aim of this review was to find out if there are any reliable ways of predicting how well people will respond to treatment for rheumatoid arthritis (RA).
We found no evidence to support the routine use of any test or tool for predicting which people are more likely to respond well to treatment.
